```
Nige wrote:

> Can anyone point me to an IBM Publication or can tell me the
> difference between Cobol and Cobol II.
> 
> Thanks
> Nige

Ann Prince wrote a good book (I think it's called "VS COBOL II", of all 
things) on the differences, with coding examples.  It's published by 
Mike Murach.

LiamD
```

<a id="t-386f8fd92b"></a>
## Thread: OS/390 Cobol - return codes in Re-entrant programs — 12 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### OS/390 Cobol - return codes in Re-entrant programs

- **From:** "Matthew" <none@none.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-02T00:05:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wX846.41050$xW4.329712@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`

```
For those interested in the nuances of re-entrant code : I'm using COBOL for
OS/390 & VM 2.1.1 (this is a Language Environment level of COBOL).

I load (using an assembler stub) the program into memory and call it. The
program executes a MOVE <number> TO RETURN-CODE. The program completes and
passes back the return code to the caller, as expected. I now call the
program a second time (this invokes the copy of the program already in
memory). This time, the program does not do the MOVE to set the return code.
To my surprise, on the second call the program passes back the return code
from the first call.

I have compiled and linked my program as REENTRANT. To me this implies that
the program should initialize all it's data areas (including the return
code) every time. This does not appear to apply to the return code. I even
found a section in the manual that hints at this - it's SC26-9046 Language
Reference page 9 "special registers". The implication there is that I need
the INITAL attribute as well as RENT.

Has anyone come across this before? The current behavior does not make sense
to me.

Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: OS/390 Cobol - return codes in Re-entrant programs

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-01T18:23:35-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<92r70v$htu$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<wX846.41050$xW4.329712@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`

```
You are incorrect in thinking that compiling (or link-editing) a program as
RE-ENTRANT in ANY WAY impacts the COBOL definition of "last-used state".  If
you compile (and link-edit) a COBOL program as RENT and have an Assembler
driver that does not CALL the module - but rather "starts it as a separate
task" multiple times, then it is true that each "instance" will get its own
copy of Working-Storage.  HOWEVER,  as long as your COBOL environment
remains "active" (and there are a NUMBER of ways this can be done), each
CALL from an assembler driver to the same program will get it in "last-used
state" - and this does include return-code.

If you want to be guaranteed of getting a "fresh copy" of the COBOL program
each time, then add the "INITIAL" attribute to the Program-ID paragraph -
and I think you will get the results that you are looking for.  (This does
NOT require the RENT compiler option - as that really does NOT have anything
to do with "last-used state")
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OS/390 Cobol - return codes in Re-entrant programs

- **From:** "Matthew" <none@none.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-02T00:54:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bF946.41087$xW4.329940@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`
- **References:** `<wX846.41050$xW4.329712@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <92r70v$htu$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Thanks Bill, that kind of helps. But I'm still a little confused:

The whole point of running RENT is that to allow concurrent executions of a
*single* instance of the program - that is to say, there is ONE instance of
the program code in memory, and multiple instances (one for each active
caller of the module) of items that can change - basically, working storage.
RETURN-CODE would have to fall in that category, too - correct. So if I have
complied my program as RENT, then when the program is called, it presumably
sets a separate copy of working storage, return-code etc. So the question
is, how do these get initialized?  Are you saying that, without INITIAL,
both working storage and return-code inherit the values from a previous
caller? And if so, which one (given that there can be several active at the
same time)? Or does that only apply to return-code.

I guess the bottom line is - is RENT useless without INITIAL?

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:92r70v$htu$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net...
> You are incorrect in thinking that compiling (or link-editing) a program
as
> RE-ENTRANT in ANY WAY impacts the COBOL definition of "last-used state".
If
> you compile (and link-edit) a COBOL program as RENT and have an Assembler
> driver that does not CALL the module - but rather "starts it as a separate
> task" multiple times, then it is true that each "instance" will get its
own
> copy of Working-Storage.  HOWEVER,  as long as your COBOL environment
> remains "active" (and there are a NUMBER of ways this can be done), each
> CALL from an assembler driver to the same program will get it in
"last-used
> state" - and this does include return-code.
>
> If you want to be guaranteed of getting a "fresh copy" of the COBOL
program
> each time, then add the "INITIAL" attribute to the Program-ID paragraph -
> and I think you will get the results that you are looking for.  (This does
> NOT require the RENT compiler option - as that really does NOT have
anything
> to do with "last-used state")
>
> --
> Bill Klein
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OS/390 Cobol - return codes in Re-entrant programs

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-01T23:56:53-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<92rqft$rft$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<wX846.41050$xW4.329712@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <92r70v$htu$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <bF946.41087$xW4.329940@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`

```
The issue is that "RENT" is an MVS (OS/390 - z/OS - whatever) concept and is
NOT a COBOL concept (although there is a COBOL compiler option). For the
RENT compiler option to allow for "multiple" instances of the same program
to be "active" at the same time - with multiple copies of "data" - you need
to have SOME driver that is handling "MVS tasks".  The most obvious example
of such a driver is CICS - which does this for EVERY COBOL program (which is
why RENT is required for CICS - COBOL programs).  If you want your
"home-grown" assembler driver to do this, then you need to get into "task"
initiation and management and NOT use standard "CALL" logic.

INITIAL is a (relatively) new feature in COBOL which "guarantees" that every
time that you enter this program (whether from Assembler, COBOL, or anything
else) that you get its data in "initial state".  The default, in COBOL,
always was (and continues to be) that you get it in "last-used state" on the
2nd thru Nth CALL to the same COBOL program in a run-unit.  There is one
other way to get "initial state" for an "already called" COBOL program - and
that is to use the COBOL CANCEL statement against that module between CALLs
to it (but this is only really available to COBOL drivers).

NOTE: One thing that "last-used state" allows is for you (the programmer) to
initialize only those individual fields that you want reset between CALLs.
Simply provide a "switch" to indicate whether or not a subprogram has ever
been called.  Check it - on the first time in, it will be set to whatever is
in the VALUE clause.  Change it to another value.  Then whenever it is "that
other value" initialize (in your own program logic) and/all fields that you
want to be initialized.

NOTE2:  I do want to be clear about COBOL drivers.  The "default"
(especially with pre-LE or non-LE-enabled Assembler) is to start a new
run-unit EVERY time you call a COBOL program.  The symptoms that you report,
make me think that you are using an LE-enabled Assembler driver (or the
Assembler driver is actually under some OTHER program).

NOTE3: There are also some issues related to CANCEL and last-used state that
apply when you have "statically" linked load modules rather than
"dynamically" called subroutines.  If this is an issue for you, let me know
and I can give you some more "hints and tips" on them.

I hope this makes things a little clearer.  If not, please ask again.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OS/390 Cobol - return codes in Re-entrant programs

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Matthew" <none@none.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-02T06:52:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uUe46.41450$xW4.333037@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`
- **References:** `<wX846.41050$xW4.329712@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <92r70v$htu$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <bF946.41087$xW4.329940@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <92rqft$rft$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill, Thank you for your time in clarifying this; I think I see the picture
now.
An explanation of what my assembler programs do might help. An assembler
main program (non-LE) ATTACHes multiple subtasks (MVS TCBs), each of which
starts running a small stub program (also non-LE assembler). The stub
running on the first subtask OS LOADs the COBOL program. The main program
then posts the stubs running on all the other subtasks. These stubs branch
to the entry point of the COBOL program, which runs and then returns. The
stubs can do this many times.

There were two issues here; the COBOL program can be run from many subtasks
simultaneously (hence it needs to be RENT), and also each individual subtask
can enter the COBOL program many times, hence storage needs to be
initialized properly on each entry. I had originally confused these two
issues; thank you for pointing out the distinction.

Interestingly, I coded a COBOL program with the INITIAL attribute (and
compiled with RENT), and found that when my stub "called" (ie branched to
the entry point of the program) the COBOL program twice (under the same
subtask), working storage still does NOT get reinitialized.  It appears that
this means a new run unit is not started every time I branch to the COBOL
program. I am running with LE option RTEREUS=(ON) - could that be a factor?

Does this all make sense? I'm much happier about the behavior now.

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:92rqft$rft$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net...

every
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OS/390 Cobol - return codes in Re-entrant programs

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-02T00:59:01-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<92ru6i$3ic$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<wX846.41050$xW4.329712@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <92r70v$htu$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <bF946.41087$xW4.329940@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <92rqft$rft$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <uUe46.41450$xW4.333037@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`

```
"Matthew" <none@none.com> wrote in message
news:uUe46.41450$xW4.333037@news-server.bigpond.net.au...
<snip>
> Interestingly, I coded a COBOL program with the INITIAL attribute (and
> compiled with RENT), and found that when my stub "called" (ie branched to
> the entry point of the program) the COBOL program twice (under the same
> subtask), working storage still does NOT get reinitialized.  It appears
that
> this means a new run unit is not started every time I branch to the COBOL
> program. I am running with LE option RTEREUS=(ON) - could that be a
factor?
>

Yes, RTEREUS=(ON) may make a difference.  HOWEVER, that is "normally only" a
VS COBOL II run-time option and not something that (normally) is used with
LE.

Have you looked at the IMPLICATIONS of using this under LE? (For example,
check out

http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.exe/BOOKS/CEEA3031/2%2e47%2e1
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OS/390 Cobol - return codes in Re-entrant programs

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2001-01-02T13:56:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010102085649.28714.00000823@ng-ct1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<uUe46.41450$xW4.333037@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`

```
"Matthew" none@none.com  writes ...

>Bill, Thank you for your time in clarifying this; I think I see the picture
>now.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

If you compile your program RENT and ATTACH it every time, instead of LOAD and
branch from existing tasks, you will let the system take care of providing
separate copies of working storage for each ATTACHer, which will give you
initial values for each copy. However, it would be very advisable to make the
parent driver LE-conforming and you should probably INCLUDE the COBOL signature
CSECT (CEESG005) in the base load module (this will ensure that LE will
initialize the COBOL component of the run-time). If the subprogram is compiled
RENT, then contents management will make sure only one copy of the code is in
your job pack area, and it can be shared by all ATTACH'ers


>There were two issues here; the COBOL program can be run from many subtasks
>simultaneously (hence it needs to be RENT), and also each individual subtask
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>program. I am running with LE option RTEREUS=(ON) - could that be a factor?


This could definitely be a factor. The IBM pubs state that this is provided for
compatibility with COBOL II behavior and is "not recommended for general use".


You might also find the COBOL LOCAL-STORAGE SECTION useful in some contexts;
basically, calls to a subroutine find working-storage in last-used state, but
local-storage items are always intialized to first-used state on CALL.

Hope this helps,




Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OS/390 Cobol - return codes in Re-entrant programs

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-02T11:57:08-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<92t4oh$icv$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<uUe46.41450$xW4.333037@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <20010102085649.28714.00000823@ng-ct1.aol.com>`

```
A few follow-up ideas/thoughts/comments:

1) Use of INITIAL in the program-id *only* impacts Working-Storage items
with VALUE clauses.  It does *not* reset other data items to the value
specified in the STORAGE LE run-time option (in case that was what was
expected/desired).  This is true regardless of RTEREUS or what starts the
program going.

2) Use of INITIAL does *not* mean that the program will always "start a new
run-unit".  It only means that the working-storage items will always be in
"initial state" rather than last-used state (and that, of course, for items
without a VALUE clause means that their contents are "undefined")

3) Use of Local-Storage in a program that is not defined as recursive *may*
give the results desired, but there are some things to think about related
to it as well - especially in a program that is not defined as recursive.
The draft Standard gets into some detail on exactly when you "push and pop"
Local-Storage items - and when they are or are not "still active".  IBM may
or may not have implemented these the way they will (eventually) end up - so
I am not positive that I would recommend this for multi-tasking.

4) I did some more searching in the LE R10 manuals and there is a LOT more
on the implications of RTEREUS with "multiple enclaves".  I would STRONGLY
recommend that you look at these pretty thoroughly before using this option
in the type of application described in this thread.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OS/390 Cobol - return codes in Re-entrant programs

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "mangogrower" <mangogrower@telocity.co>

```
I agree Bill, under OS/390 LE, IBM recommends for best performance that the
RTREUS be ON if you will be calling a routine multiple times BECAUSE (and
this is
why with CICS NOW under LE they recommend a 'CALL' instead of a 'Link') the
LE
creates a NEW environement with LARGER DATA / Control Structures each time
without
the RunTimeReUsable attribute set TRUE.
```

#### ↳ Re: OS/390 Cobol - return codes in Re-entrant programs

- **From:** croxbo@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2001-01-02T20:16:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<92tcu1$fjf$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<wX846.41050$xW4.329712@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`

```
Hi,

The return-code is a global variable which is passed through all the
called modules. If a program does not initialize it, it can give back a
value that is set by a previous called program.

It is logical that it behaves like this because the return-code is not
a private variable from one program.

Good Luck!

  Croxbo


Sent via Deja.com
http://www.deja.com/
```

<a id="t-837b1a4cf7"></a>
## Thread: mainframe editors — 1 this month / 48 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

_Thread started [2000-12](2000-12.md#t-837b1a4cf7)._

#### ↳ Re: mainframe editors (and Xedit on PCs)

- **From:** "Glenn Wood" <glenn_wood@compuserve.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-02T12:18:55
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<92sh1n$nhs$1@sshuraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<91p32l0j9s@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
I always liked VM/CMS/Xedit on the mainframe. Especially when they added
Rexx (years before it got to TSO/ISPF). That environment was almost
infinitely configurable, and Rexx skills came easily in a PL/1 development
shop (many similarities of syntax).

When we migrated from VM to MVS in the mid 80s I recommended ROSCOE over
TSO/ISPF for developers because:
- (a) there was the significant performance hit of multiple address spaces
(TSO) over a single shared address space (ROSCOE) - our developers were used
to being logged on all day and we didn't want to end up with them having to
queue for machine time;
- (b) there were [very] superficial similarities between the look-and-feel
of ROSCOE and CMS/Xedit - in particular, emphasis on the command line - no
vast collection of ISPF panels to navigate to do simple tasks (and we didn't
like the ISPF "=" command-line shortcuts for aesthetic and learning-curve
reasons);
- (c) ROSCOE had a macro language (RPF) that didn't quite live up to Rexx
but was still a whole lot preferable to Clists.

Of course, all the Sysprogs and Ops had TSO/ISPF but the developers got (and
still have) ROSCOE. Our Sysprogs and Ops still prefer ROSCOE for tasks that
don't absolutely require ISPF. They use another non-IBM product for the
machine consoles.

Over the years we've built up a fairly large library of macros providing a
range of day-to-day functions. We have LIBRARIAN as well.

A number of TSO products can be run under ROSCOE (ROSETSO) though not all
have appropriate threading models for running in a shared environment. We
run ISPF products such as SDF in a slave ROSETSO region. Not having full
FILE-AID support has always been a limitation, though.

In those days ROSCOE was developed and sold by ADR. When CA took it on there
were the usual promises of much more development investment, which went
largely unfulfilled and subsequently dried up entirely. No surprises
there.... I certainly recognise the management inclination to ditch CA.

Anyone who has a PC or Unix workstation but is nostalgic for Xedit - or just
wants to get back to coding via a keyboard rather than a mouse - might like
to look at Mark Hessling's excellent GNU freeware packages at:

http://www.lightlink.com/hessling/

Mark has a very good simulation of Xedit called THE (The Hessling Editor) to
run on various platforms (including Win 95/NT) - it incorporates Rexx
support. He also maintains the equally excellent Regina Rexx interpreter.
Add some file transfer services and you've got an interesting proposition
for a cheap offline coding environment. No interactive PL/1 or COBOL
compilers, though. (You can always pay IBM for the Visual Age products if
you really need it.)

Glenn Wood
Wolverhampton City Council, UK
These are personal opinions and do not represent the policies of
Wolverhampton City Council
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help: program works when I step thru but not while run to cursor

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** dxmixxer@netdirect.net (Doug Miller)
- **Date:** 2001-01-02T18:33:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3a521efa$0$35016$4156c999@news.netdirect.net>`
- **References:** `<3a487f61.23076342@news.adelaide.on.net> <3a48ea56$0$1503$7ea90a65@news.netdirect.net> <3a4df4b0.6970052@news.adelaide.on.net> <3a4f8722$0$1501$7ea90a65@news.netdirect.net> <3a5078a0.5073430@news.adelaide.on.net>`

```
In article <3a5078a0.5073430@news.adelaide.on.net>, goble@gtech wrote:
>On Sun, 31 Dec 2000 19:12:27 GMT, dlmiller@netdirect.net (Doug Miller)
>wrote:
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>ie 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 or 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12 etc...

If the range of possible numbers is 1..45, then there are only 40 series of 
the form (k, k+1, k+2, ...): (1,2,3...) thru (40,41,42,43,44,45). Similarly, 
there are only 17 series of the form (2k, 2k+2, 2k+4, ...): (2,4,6...) thru 
(34,36,38,40,42,44).

How many other series there might be following some particular patterns would 
of course depend on what patterns you choose. If you're only interested in 
arithmetic progressions, it's not difficult to calculate how many there are. 
Given that all possible progressions will be of the form (n, n+k, n+2k, n+3k, 
n+4k, n+5k) where k and n >= 1 and n+5k <= 45, all you need do is figure out 
how many different values of k there can be for each value of n:

If 1 <= n <= 5, then 1 <= k <= 8
If 6 <= n <= 10, then 1<= k <= 7
If 11 <= n <= 15, then 1 <= k <= 6
..
If 31 <= n <=35, then 1 <= k <= 2
If 36 <= n <= 40, then k = 1

This means (8 + 7 + 6 + ... + 1) * 5 = 180 possible arithmetic progressions.

In the small range of 1..45, the only possible geometric progression is the 
powers of 2 (1,2,4,8,16,32).

>
>and how would I best be able to remove these sets?

You could check to see if (u - v) = (v - w) = (w - x) = (x - y) = (y - z), but 
it's probably simpler to remove them after the fact.
```

#### ↳ Re: help: program works when I step thru but not while run to cursor

- **From:** lcccpiasabird@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2001-01-02T18:12:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<92t5lk$8u7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<3a487f61.23076342@news.adelaide.on.net>`

```
If you really want to make millions of sets of numbers to be
printed/written to a file, try setting up collumns of numbers and see
how many accross you want to go.  If you are writing them to a file one
write per number will really slow things down.  If you computed say
5,000-30,000 numbers at a time in a table and wrote them in blocks of
the largest number of bytes that is possible That might really speed
things up.  For instance if you are writing really short record and the
size was say 5 2-digit,lottery numbers side by side that is Ten
Characters, but if you make that a packed number it could even be
smaller.  As a packed number, pic "9(11) comp 3" only takes up 6
character positions! If you are using a 512 character block you can
write about 80 records or so in a block.  This would really speed up
the writing process.  I don't know if you can do that on a PC or not,
but I am guessing that you can.

What type of MODE you write in will make the file smaller. If all of
the numbers are end to end with no control characters for end of record
then you can save space.

It takes one extra half bite to get an odd number of packed digits in a
packed number because one half byte is used for the sign. If you
redefine the packed number as a 6 character alphanumeric field it will
sort even faster.  That is a little harder and a pain to code but it
would speed up a sort.

Another thing is what if some of the winning combinations are repeats?
If these are lottery balls then each number can only appear once.  For
instance you can not have "01 01 01 01 01".
If you had an array of numbers to pick from with no duplicates and you
chose one number the first time by using a random number for the
subscript, then you would want to move a valid number from the last
array element into that position (Unless it is the last), and then use
a random number of one less to choose the second number.  Therefore the
possible number combinations are reduced by one for each succeeding
ball picked.
Once the array size is less than the total number of balls picked you
are done, which will also limit the number of possiblities.

Since the lottery people always display the balls in numeric order,
once you choose all the balls combinations with a one in it you can
remove the number one from the group of 60 or 50 or how ever many. This
will also reduce the number of combinations quite a bit.


In article <3a487f61.23076342@news.adelaide.on.net>,
  goble@gtech wrote:
> Hi All;
>
…[165 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Sent via Deja.com
http://www.deja.com/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and Scope-terminators - again (was: IGYPS2112-E

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-02T07:16:52-07:00

- **Message-ID:** `<3A51E2D4.7B25E6ED@brazee.net>`

```
RJones1005 wrote:

> Taking one of our famous page long (multi-IF imbedded) IF statements all
> terminated by a single period... A NEXT SENTENCE would pass execution to the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> end of the entire page-long IF.

I don't think so.  I have used the following construct to make it easy to debug:

    IF SW-EOF-FILE1
           CONTINUE
D        DISPLAY ' END OF FILE 1'
    END-IF.

Compiled with debug on, the If logic does a continue, and then it does a display.

I have also maintained CoBOL programs which had GOTO EXIT logic which went to
paragraphs without EXIT statements.  With our upgraded compiler, I got rid of a
message by putting in a CONTINUE (I could have put in an EXIT, but CONTINUE works
just as well, but allows me to add a debug display without removing the EXIT
statement).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and Scope-terminators - again (was: IGYPS2112-E

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Jeff Baynard <jeffbaynard@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-03T06:16:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<B6782DD6.22E%jeffbaynard@home.com>`
- **References:** `<B672B640.1E6%jeffbaynard@home.com> <20001230100611.23172.00006692@ng-fv1.aol.com> <3A51E2D4.7B25E6ED@brazee.net>`

```
You are correct.  CONTINUE is basically a NO-OP.  Meaning nothing occurs.
It does not branch control anywhere; not even after the scope delimiter.  It
is especially useful when avoiding NOT conditions:

IF ( A NOT = B ) AND ( C NOT = D )
   YADDA YADDA
END-IF

I like this better:

IF ( A = B ) OR ( C = D )
   CONTINUE
ELSE
   YADDA YADDA
END-IF


Jeff

in article 3A51E2D4.7B25E6ED@brazee.net, Howard Brazee at howard@brazee.net
wrote on 1/2/01 9:16 AM:

> RJones1005 wrote:
> 
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and Scope-terminators - again (was: IGYPS2112-E

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Joe Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2001-01-03T22:21:06+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<josephk-7E0ECC.22210603012001@news.m.iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<B672B640.1E6%jeffbaynard@home.com> <20001230100611.23172.00006692@ng-fv1.aol.com> <3A51E2D4.7B25E6ED@brazee.net> <B6782DD6.22E%jeffbaynard@home.com>`

```
In article <B6782DD6.22E%jeffbaynard@home.com>, Jeff Baynard 
<jeffbaynard@home.com> wrote:

> You are correct.  CONTINUE is basically a NO-OP.  Meaning nothing occurs.
> It does not branch control anywhere; not even after the scope delimiter.  
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
I prefer the first form. This is because my mind naturally assumes that 
the true condition will execute "YADDA". The second form it's the false 
condition that executes the desired code, so I have to make a mental 
transformation to determine when the desired code is to be executed.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and Scope-terminators - again (was: IGYPS2112-E

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-03T09:46:46-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<92vhe0$2d4$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<B672B640.1E6%jeffbaynard@home.com> <20001230100611.23172.00006692@ng-fv1.aol.com> <3A51E2D4.7B25E6ED@brazee.net> <B6782DD6.22E%jeffbaynard@home.com> <josephk-7E0ECC.22210603012001@news.m.iinet.net.au>`

```
I *generally* prefer putting the "most commonly executed" branch first
(whether negative or "positive").  At one time, this lead to run-time
efficiencies - but now just seems (semi-)more logical to me.  Sometimes this
DOES mean that the first path is just a "CONTINUE".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and Scope-terminators - again (was: IGYPS2112-E

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-01-04T08:05:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A542ECF.F5E65F93@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<B672B640.1E6%jeffbaynard@home.com> <20001230100611.23172.00006692@ng-fv1.aol.com> <3A51E2D4.7B25E6ED@brazee.net> <B6782DD6.22E%jeffbaynard@home.com> <josephk-7E0ECC.22210603012001@news.m.iinet.net.au>`

```
Joe Katnic wrote:

> > IF ( A NOT = B ) AND ( C NOT = D )
> >    YADDA YADDA
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> transformation to determine when the desired code is to be executed.

Most people can cope with the second because it is easier to deal with
positives (equal) than with conbinations of negatives (not equal). 
There was an article about this in New Scientist recently in the more
general context.

A compromise might be:

   IF NOT ( (A = B) OR (C = D) )
       YADA YADA
   END-IF
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and Scope-terminators - again (was: IGYPS2112-E message after WRITE

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-02T07:21:17-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A51E3DC.A2B590A0@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<91odm0$sql$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <20001222231219.03963.00006052@ng-ct1.aol.com> <3A49FB13.269D2DD3@brazee.net> <92da66$fj2$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <92em29$7ua$1@sshuraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <92ifn7$2kl2$1@news.hitter.net> <B672B640.1E6%jeffbaynard@home.com>`

```


Jeff Baynard wrote:

> Amen.
>
> I have coded a handful of GO TOs in my career, but never have I coded a NEXT
> SENTENCE.  NEXT SENTENCE is a 'hand-delivered' invitation for errors.

Until CONTINUE was available, I have coded innumerable NEXT SENTENCE clauses.
So far, I haven't seen that this invites errors.  Of course, I haven't worked in
a shop which believed in one period per paragraph.

Now code which avoids this by putting in NOT logic has invited errors.  Lots of
programmers apparently have troubles with NOTs, but I haven't come across
programmers who had troubles with NEXT SENTENCE.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and Scope-terminators - again (was: IGYPS2112-E message

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** rjones1005@aol.com (RJones1005)
- **Date:** 2001-01-04T02:58:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010103215845.19919.00006168@ng-ft1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3A51E3DC.A2B590A0@brazee.net>`

```
>Until CONTINUE was available, I have coded innumerable NEXT SENTENCE clauses.
>So far, I haven't seen that this invites errors.  Of course, I haven't worked
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>programmers who had troubles with NEXT SENTENCE.


Based on my experience in rewriting our "program from hell" I believe in the
one-period per paragraph thing.  My shop clearly is not of the same mind.

My larger point is that my shop (and it's not that unique apparently) is locked
into "COBOL 74 think."  That is, the way one programs, indeed must program
given the constructs available in that version of the ANSI standard.  Further,
I get the impression many COBOL programmers only think in "the COBOL way"
because they know nothing else.  

My boss's boss was clearly somewhat perplexed when I said I had done away with
GO TOs in the new program. "How in the world did I 'abort' further processing
in the case of an error?"

The programmer in my shop I most admire was flummoxed by inline PERFORMS and
EVALUATE TRUE statments, among others - commenting that "the code is difficult"
as if the old code was comprenensible and maintainable.

All I can say is that if I han never learned assembler, basic, fortran, C, C++
that my thinking also would be limited to "the COBOL way."   I strongly believe
my success in rewriting this program, where many others had failed, is due in
large part to the fact that I had learned other programming languages. 

So I simply don't understand how one-period-per-paragraph leads to NOT logic
(which I agree comes across as stilted); or is that CONTINUE that does that?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IGYPS2112-E message after WRITE

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Tom McFarland" <tmcfarland@uturn.home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-06T04:26:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<28x56.103817$w35.18286809@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com>`
- **References:** `<91odm0$sql$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <20001222231219.03963.00006052@ng-ct1.aol.com> <3A49FB13.269D2DD3@brazee.net>`

```
I've been writing IDMS code for 20 years and made the transition to scope
terminators/no periods without a hitch.  There's no mystery in what the IDMS
precompiler inserts in your program when you use an AUTOSTATUS protocol.  In
my experience, it will only insert NEXT SENTENCE if you tell it to.

Example:

OBTAIN CALC CUSTOMER
ON DB-REC-NOT-FOUND
    my error handling code
END-IF

pre-compiles to:

CALL 'IDMS' USING bunch of arguments
IF NOT DB-REC-NOT-FOUND
    PERFORM IDMS-STATUS
ELSE
  my error handling code
END-IF

If you terminate your IDMS ON statement with a period rather than an END-IF,
the resulting precompiler code will likewise terminate with a period.

So if you code:

OBTAIN CALC CUSTOMER
ON DB-REC-NOT-FOUND
    NEXT SENTENCE.

You'll get:

CALL 'IDMS' USING bunch of arguments
IF NOT DB-REC-NOT-FOUND
    PERFORM IDMS-STATUS
ELSE
  NEXT SENTENCE.

In what circumstances has your pre-compiler inserted NEXT SENTENCE or
CONTINUE?

Tom McFarland


Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:3A49FB13.269D2DD3@brazee.net...
>
> RJones1005 wrote:
>
> > IMHO don't go mixing scope terminators and periods.  I use
scope-terminators
> > exclusively and use periods only at the end of paragraph names and at
the end
> > of paragraphs (COBOL 85 ANSI STANDARD requirement).  The problem with
periods
> > is that you will inadvertentaly end up terminating everything, and they
may not
> > be what you intend.  And I guarantee you will get all mucked up if you
go
> > mixing these with IFs.
>
> I've seen you say that a few times.  I have an environment where I have to
use
> periods because I don't know for sure what my pre-compiler will stick in
for my
> IDMS code.  (I also feel comfortable with full stop periods within
paragraphs).
> But there is no way I am going to give up my scope terminators.
>
> So far, I haven't come across any problems by using scope terminators in
an
> environment where everybody uses periods.
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Get file size in Realia?

- **From:** Support@ScreenIO.com (Kevin J. Hansen)
- **Date:** 2001-01-02T19:01:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3a522700.5571231@news>`
- **References:** `<3A4B8316.8BC6623F@arkansas.net> <3A4D3892.FE85086E@bellsouth.net> <n5e36.46274$59.13209352@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
On Sat, 30 Dec 2000 05:08:35 GMT, "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
wrote:

>You might be able to get a byte count using DOS_LSEEK twice, once with
>SEEK-FROM-START set to TRUE and again with you set SEEK-FROM-END to TRUE and
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>nFileSizeHigh. I ain't never called a Win32 API from Realia though...
=================
Nice idea, but the size of the file in bytes is only a vague
approximation of the number of records in the file. 

As I understand it, the Realia file system allocates blocks that
contain both indexes and data space, and these may of course be empty.


What you REALLY need is to obtain the number of records in a given
keypath, and the percentage along the keypath of the current key.
Btrieve (and other data managres) provide this, but it takes some
hocus-pocus to obtain it from ISAM.  

About the only way you can get anything meaningful about your
CA-Realia ISAM file is to ask them how it's done.

Or, convert to Btrieve or some other similar package...

Kevin

Norcom - COBOL Programming Tools
GUI ScreenIO - A Windows UI for COBOL
http://www.screenio.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Get file size in Realia?

- **From:** godch01@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2001-01-09T17:37:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<93fi8n$8p$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<3A4B8316.8BC6623F@arkansas.net>`

```
In article <3A4B8316.8BC6623F@arkansas.net>,
  Terry Grider <tgrider@arkansas.net> wrote:
> Does anyone know how to get either the record count or file size from
a
> Realia indexed file from within a cobol program?  I'm hoping for an
API
> or something.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
There is no API available to determine the number of records in a
Realia indexed file. this is true for both the 16 bit and 32 bit
versions. There is no method of reading a specific location in the file
to determine this value. The only way to determine the number of
records in a Realia indexed file is to sequentially read the file and
count the records.

Charles W. Godwin
Development Manager - CA-Realia Products
Computer Associates International, Inc.
MailTo:Charles.Godwin@ca.com
http://www.ca.com


Sent via Deja.com
http://www.deja.com/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Get file size in Realia?

- **From:** croxbo@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2001-01-10T00:28:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<93gac6$j2t$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<3A4B8316.8BC6623F@arkansas.net> <93fi8n$8p$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
When you use the REALIF utility, you can redirect the output into a
file and read it. Realif says a lot about indexed files without
completely reading it. It reads the header of the file, which contains
all the information.

The format is:

REALIF filename.ext > realif.out

The file realif.out will contain the info of filename.ext.

I hope this is helpful.



Sent via Deja.com
http://www.deja.com/
```

<a id="t-312c47eca9"></a>
## Thread: Computing the difference between two times. — 5 msgs

### Computing the difference between two times.

- **From:** acimo@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2001-01-02T16:25:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<92sve5$36c$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In 'Program A', I am calculating the amount of time it has been since
'Program B' has previously executed.

Every time 'Program B' starts, I write that time to a file.  I read this
file and move the time to WS-PREV-RUN-TIME.  I move the system time to
WS-SYS-TIME.  I convert both times from hhmmss into seconds.  I then
subtract WS-PREV-RUN-TIME-SECS from WS-SYS-TIME-SECS.  I take the
difference a convert it back to hhmmss.

I've tested the code and I know it works.  Is there a better calculation
I can use?  I'm also open to any suggestions regarding my style of code.

Thank you.

Below is a sample of my code :

 01  WS-WORK-FIELDS.
     05  WS-TEST-DATE            PIC 9(08) VALUE 20001229.
     05  WS-CICS-FIELDS.
        10  WS-ABSTIME           PIC S9(15) COMP.
        10  WS-RESPONSE-CODE     PIC S9(08) COMP.
        10  WS-RESPONSE-CODE2    PIC S9(08) COMP.
        10  WS-DAY-OF-WEEK       PIC S9(08) COMP.
            88 SUN-ISS           VALUE 0.
            88 MON-ISS           VALUE 1.
            88 TUE-ISS           VALUE 2.
            88 WED-ISS           VALUE 3.
            88 THU-ISS           VALUE 4.
            88 FRI-ISS           VALUE 5.
            88 SAT-ISS           VALUE 6.
******SYSTEM TIME
        10  WS-SYS-TIME          PIC 9(06).
        10  WS-SYS-TIME-R        REDEFINES WS-SYS-TIME.
            15  WS-SYS-TIME-HH   PIC 9(02).
            15  WS-SYS-TIME-MM   PIC 9(02).
            15  WS-SYS-TIME-SS   PIC 9(02).
        10  WS-DATE              PIC 9(08).
        10  WS-RIDFLD            PIC S9(09) COMP.
****PREVIOUS RUN TIME
     05  WS-PREV-RUN-TIME        PIC 9(06).
     05  WS-PREV-RUN-TIME-R      REDEFINES WS-PREV-RUN-TIME.
         10  WS-PREV-RUN-HH      PIC 9(02).
         10  WS-PREV-RUN-MM      PIC 9(02).
         10  WS-PREV-RUN-SS      PIC 9(02).
****DIFFERENCE BETWEEN PREV-RUN-TIME AND SYS-TIME
     05   WS-DIFF-TIME           PIC 9(06).
     05   WS-DIFF-TIME-R         REDEFINES WS-DIFF-TIME.
         10  WS-DIFF-TIME-HH     PIC 9(02).
         10  WS-DIFF-TIME-MM     PIC 9(02).
         10  WS-DIFF-TIME-SS     PIC 9(02).
***MISCELLANEOUS TIME FIELDS.
     05  WS-PREV-RUN-TIME-SECS   PIC 9(05).
     05  WS-SYS-TIME-SECS        PIC 9(05).
     05  WS-REMAINDER-MM         PIC 9(05).
     05  WS-REMAINDER-SS         PIC 9(05).
************************************************************

 READ-LAST-START-ADS0600.
**OBTAIN PREVIOUS RUN TIME.
     MOVE 'ADIT' TO COD-KEY.
     PERFORM GIT-COD-REC.
     MOVE COD-NBR (2) TO WS-PREV-RUN-TIME.

 READ-LAST-START-ADS0600-EXIT.     EXIT.

 COMPUTE-TIME-DIFF.
**OBTAIN SYSTEM TIME.
     PERFORM CICS-ASK-TIME.
**CONVERT PREV-RUN-TIME INTO SECONDS.
     COMPUTE WS-PREV-RUN-TIME-SECS =
        (WS-PREV-RUN-HH * 3600) + (WS-PREV-RUN-MM * 60) +
        WS-PREV-RUN-SS.
**CONVERT WS-SYS-TIME INTO SECONDS.
     COMPUTE WS-SYS-TIME-SECS =
        (WS-SYS-TIME-HH * 3600) + (WS-SYS-TIME-MM * 60) +
        WS-SYS-TIME-SS.
**CALCULATE WS-DIFF-TIME.
     SUBTRACT WS-PREV-RUN-TIME-SECS FROM WS-SYS-TIME-SECS.
     DIVIDE WS-SYS-TIME-SECS BY 3600
        GIVING WS-DIFF-TIME-HH
        REMAINDER WS-REMAINDER-MM.
     DIVIDE WS-REMAINDER-MM BY 60
        GIVING WS-DIFF-TIME-MM
        REMAINDER WS-REMAINDER-SS.
     MOVE WS-REMAINDER-SS TO WS-DIFF-TIME-SS.

 COMPUTE-TIME-DIFF-EXIT.         EXIT.
======================= B O T T O M ========================






Sent via Deja.com
http://www.deja.com/
```

#### ↳ Re: Computing the difference between two times.

```
result:

24 hours    ===> 86,400,000ms
01 hour      ===> 3,600,000ms
01 minute  ===> 60,000ms

HTH....

Bill
```

### Current COBOL job market status?

- **From:** Warren Porter <wbport4@bellsouth3.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-02T15:02:36-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A5241EC.123B3F75@bellsouth3.net>`

```
I took early retirement from a mostly COBOL position in late 1999 after
months of Y2K conversions.  Currently I am thinking about getting back
into the swing of things, but ads in local paper (central Miss.) have
virtually no COBOL positions for months on end unless that is just one
of many skills an applicant must have.  I've looked at dice and monster
as well and have an application/resume on file at a local recruiter.

How does the COBOL job market look nowadays?  What other key skills does
the IT need and what are the best ways to acquire them?

TIA
```

#### ↳ Re: Current COBOL job market status?

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2001-01-03T02:19:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
In article <3A5241EC.123B3F75@bellsouth3.net>,
Warren Porter  <wbport4@bellsouth3.net> wrote:

[snippage]

>How does the COBOL job market look nowadays?

Stinks, *big*-time... but that is to be expected right now.  The holidays
are finished; during the first week in January folks are busy recovering
from them, trading stories about ol' drunken (relative) and shipping the
kids back off to college.

The second week in January is the Plague of Great Meetings... last year we
did (thing), this coming year we'll do (thing better).

The *third* week in January people begin to wake up and say 'Wholly
fertiliser... I haven't done *anything* since Thanksgiving!'... and *then*
the pimps should start getting orders and things should start moving.

This, at least, has been my experience for a few years now... last year,
however, was a complete violation of this schedule; last year was the Year
Without Summer (1816) with absolutely *no* action *all* *year* *long*
(except for a small flurry just before Thanksgiving... the flurry during
which I got off the miserable pig-of-a-contract I'd been on for the
*entire* **stinking** *year* with *no* *alternatives* *at* *all* showing
up).

>What other key skills does
>the IT need and what are the best ways to acquire them?

The same name as the corner-office boss; marry into the family.

DD
```

<a id="t-dc3abb3c40"></a>
## Thread: Your thoughts on insy education and beyond — 1 this month / 2 total

_Thread started [2000-12](2000-12.md#t-dc3abb3c40)._

#### ↳ Re: Your thoughts on insy education and beyond

- **From:** Susan Allen <susan.a.allen@boeing.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-02T22:36:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A5257F5.1B220130@boeing.com>`
- **References:** `<rllowrie-E09CBC.09425523122000@news.alntn1.tx.home.com>`

```
Lynn Lowrie wrote:
> 
> I would like your thoughts on a matter.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> should pursue.

Congratulations on the A.  I have a BABA (Bachelor Arts Business Admin.)
and for a living I am a Systems Analyst (& yes I do a great deal of
programming, sometimes in COBOL). There were no Computer Science degrees
in those far off days (although the program was in development at the
time)

The Business Degree gave me a profound understanding of Financial
systems, I could chat comfortably with Accountants and Financial
Statements folk, I could also easily understand the Estimating Community
(how much did it cost, how much will it cost next time).  I found I have
no personal inclination to be a manager and prefer the more technical
tasks.

YOur degree will give you some framework to decide what you enjoy doing
and a VISA into the business world, where you will with time, find your
true calling (be it technical or managerial or something else).

	Best wishes		Susan A
```

<a id="t-42a2956763"></a>
## Thread: Problem with manuel record lock — 8 msgs

### Problem with manuel record lock

- **From:** "Ralph Leiherr" <leiherr@vwda.de>
- **Date:** 2001-01-03T14:30:50+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<92v9if$k4s$02$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
Hi all,

my Cobol Compiler ist MicroFocus 4.032 and my OS is Windows NT 4.0.

I need help about a problem with manuel record lock.

I have a file with 10 records

PrimKey  AlternateKey with duplicates (Name)
-------  -----------------------------------
0000001  Mayer
0000002  Mayer
0000003  Mayer
0000004  Mayer
0000005  Mayer
0000006  Schulze


User (1) locked the Record with Key 00003 for update.

User (2) search about the AlternateKey. At record 00003 the system gives me
the StatusCode 068 who tells this record is locked. So I will read the next
record
but they have the same alternate Key with the locked record so I canï¿½t
start with key is greater !

Is there everybody who can me help about this problem ?

Thanks

Ralph Leiherr
```

#### ↳ Re: Problem with manuel record lock

- **From:** Frank Pohlmann <Frank.Pohlmann@baeurer.de>
- **Date:** 2001-01-03T15:20:04+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A533514.2ECF440E@baeurer.de>`
- **References:** `<92v9if$k4s$02$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
What we did is to use only unique alternate keys. So we put the primary key at
the end of the alternate key to get a unique key.
I don't know another way to solve the problem.

Frank Pohlmann

Ralph Leiherr schrieb:

> Hi all,
>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ralph Leiherr
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Problem with manuel record lock

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-01-04T08:35:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A5435CD.23D16D38@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<92v9if$k4s$02$1@news.t-online.com> <3A533514.2ECF440E@baeurer.de>`

```
> Ralph Leiherr schrieb:
> >
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Is there everybody who can me help about this problem ?

MF Cobol has the +B switch which changes the behaviour to how you want
to work.  With the +B run-time switch OFF (as it is by default) then
after a READ NEXT has found a locked record the following READ NEXT on a
file opened in I-O mode will re-read the same record again (as you have
found).

If the +B switch is ON then the READ NEXT will get the actual next
record and not re-read the locked record.

Both behaviours are useful in different circumstances.

This +B switch can be set on the COBSW environment variable, on the
program run command or programatically.

Alternately use the NORETRYLOCK compiler directive.

If the file is open in INPUT mode the lock won't be detected nor will it
re-read the locked record.  If user 2 is not updating then you could
close and re-open as appropriate so that it is in INPUT mode when
reading through alternate key.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Problem with manuel record lock

- **From:** 098821430@doar.net
- **Date:** 2001-01-04T06:25:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<931518$k4c$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<92v9if$k4s$02$1@news.t-online.com> <3A533514.2ECF440E@baeurer.de> <3A5435CD.23D16D38@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

  Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

read the next

can't

> > >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> after a READ NEXT has found a locked record the following READ NEXT
on a
> file opened in I-O mode will re-read the same record again (as you
have
> found).
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> If the file is open in INPUT mode the lock won't be detected nor will
it
> re-read the locked record.  If user 2 is not updating then you could
> close and re-open as appropriate so that it is in INPUT mode when
> reading through alternate key.
>

Instead of dealing with B switch or reopening the file in INPUT mode.
You can use the IGNORE LOCK syntax.


Sent via Deja.com
http://www.deja.com/
```

#### ↳ Re: Problem with manuel record lock

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2001-01-03T14:30:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WHG46.7655$Sl.399507@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<92v9if$k4s$02$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
In article <92v9if$k4s$02$1@news.t-online.com>,
Ralph Leiherr <leiherr@vwda.de> wrote:
>Hi all,
>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>Is there everybody who can me help about this problem ?

You still have the primary key (someplace) from your last good read.  Try
incrementing this and issuing a START NOT LESS THAN based on the
incremented value; this should return the record following the locked one.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Problem with manuel record lock

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-01-04T08:24:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A543344.10E834B1@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<92v9if$k4s$02$1@news.t-online.com> <WHG46.7655$Sl.399507@iad-read.news.verio.net> <92vk2n$lk7$1@news.igs.net> <vQI46.7676$Sl.400864@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
> >>>PrimKey  AlternateKey with duplicates (Name)
> >>>-------  -----------------------------------
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> >>>record

> >>You still have the primary key (someplace) from your last good read.  Try
> >>incrementing this and issuing a START NOT LESS THAN based on the
> >>incremented value; this should return the record following the locked one.

That fails the test.  It happens, in this particular case, that the next
primary key is also the next 'Mayer' to be read.  In any real case
'reading the next primary key' would be less than useful.  Ie your
'solution' won't work. 

> >That is going to change which key you are reading by ...
> 
> Only for the first READ NEXT; after that one has the alternate key of the
> record following the locked record and can switch back to that path.

That fails the test too.

Aside from the fact that the 'solution' is specific to the set of data
(and thus is not a solution), how would you suggest 'switching back to
that path' in a way that would actually read the record after the lock ?

A START Perhaps ?  A READ .. KEY IS .. ?

Won't work.
```

```
We are persuing Stored Procedures returning Resultsets on DB2 v6 or v7.

I know, this can be done on DB2/OS390. I understand that we have to
declare the data type SQL TYPE RESULT-SET-LOCATOR VARYING & then
ASSOCIATE the Locator with the Procedure.

But, I am not able to compile the code. Any thoughts appreciated. Thx.

Siva


Sent via Deja.com
http://www.deja.com/
```

#### ↳ Re: Resultset in Cobol Stored Procedures

- **From:** "Randy Zimmerman" <rzimmerman@wi.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-03T19:07:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YLK46.11$Pi.1428@typhoon.mw.mediaone.net>`
- **References:** `<92vpgr$eg3$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
What SQL database software are you using and what COBOL product are you
using?  Quite a few SQL pc vendors (including IBM) do not yet support result
set locators with Cobol precompilers.

MERANT does supports result set locators in our Mainframe Express V2.5 and
Net Express V3.1 products when used with the SQL Option (XDB) sql database
engine.  One nice feature of this support is the ability to animate both the
client and server programs.

Randy Zimmerman
MERANT
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Resultset in Cobol Stored Procedures

- **From:** "Randy Zimmerman" <rzimmerman@wi.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-09T18:53:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<M6J66.3099$nU4.218030@typhoon.mw.mediaone.net>`
- **References:** `<92vpgr$eg3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <YLK46.11$Pi.1428@typhoon.mw.mediaone.net> <93csim$rof$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Siva,

Sorry - there isn't much you can do for the time being.  There is a small
glimmer of hope since I believe DB2 UDB v8 will probably support result set
locators (this is a rumor I've heard and not an IBM preannouncement so treat
this comment for what it's worth) and then you could you probably then use
the IBM Cobol precompiler with WB 4.0.38.   DB2 UDB v7 can talk to JAVA
stored procedures which can return objects like result sets but WB 4.0.38
doesn't work too good with JAVA.

MFE/Net Express/Server Express all use a new MERANT precompiler technology
called an ecm which I'm responsible for so if and when IBM does support this
function in DB2 UDB I would look to implement support for it.

We basically only do bug fixes for classic products such as WB 4.0.38.

Randy Zimmerman
MERANT
```

<a id="t-1125600752"></a>
## Thread: number of days between dates — 26 msgs

### number of days between dates

- **From:** "mike" <mikman@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-03T18:03:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bQJ46.4569$jo.56099329@tomcat.sk.sympatico.ca>`

```
We have used the following chunk of code for years to do
this calculation, but it seems to be missing a day for the
transition period between 2000/12/XX to 2001/01/XX.  You run
it on the 2 dates and then subtract the results to give the
number of days between the dates.   (eg 2000/12/30 and
2000/12/31 results in 1 day as does 2001/01/01 and
2001/01/02.)  The problem arises when we pass it something
like 2000/12/30 and 2001/01/01 both show up as having 146098
days since Dec 31,1600 thus resulting in zero days which is
incorrect.  I think it has something to do with it being
2001 for the year.   But I can't see the Forest for the
trees here. Can anyone see any problems with the logic?

 MOVE ZERO TO INT-OUT.
******* compute # of days to passed year.
    SUBTRACT 1601 FROM DATE-YEAR GIVING THIS-YEAR.
    COMPUTE LEAP-ADJUST = THIS-YEAR / 100 - THIS-YEAR / 400.
    COMPUTE LEAP-DAYS = THIS-YEAR / 4 - LEAP-ADJUST.
    COMPUTE INT-OUT = THIS-YEAR * 365 + LEAP-DAYS.
******* add in # of days in preceeding months
           ADD DAYS-TO-MONTH( DATE-MONTH ) TO INT-OUT
    MOVE DATE-YEAR TO LEAP-YEAR-TEST.
    IF IS-LEAP-YEAR AND DATE-MONTH > 2
       ADD 1 TO INT-OUT.
******* add in day within month
    ADD DATE-DAY TO INT-OUT.
       A000-EXIT.
           EXIT.
```

```
01  LEAP-DAYS  PIC 9(4).

                                                        32,
36, 40, 44, 48, 52, 56, 60,

68, 72, 76, 80, 84, 88, 92,
                                                        96.
```

#### ↳ Re: number of days between dates

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-03T12:21:23-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A537BB2.B807C025@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<bQJ46.4569$jo.56099329@tomcat.sk.sympatico.ca>`

```
I don't know how your algorithm is supposed to work.  How does it
determine how many days are in between January 1, 1900 and January 1,
2000 with all of the leap years in betwen?   If you have a sneaky
algorithm, make sure to DOCUMENT it in your code.  If you don't care,
document that as well.

I also don't know what your picture clauses are like?  What is stored
when you do the following:
COMPUTE LEAP-ADJUST = THIS-YEAR / 100 - THIS-YEAR / 400. where
THIS-YEAR=399?    399/100 - 399/400 = ???? depends upon picture.  It may
even be dependent upon your compiler's handling of intermediate values,
I'm not sure.

When I do leap year checking, I use the REMAINDER clause to determine
leap year.

Here's one way of doing the program:
- Verify last-date > This-date
- If either date > February, perform leap-year-check
- Convert dates to YYYY and DDD, adding in leap day if necessary

Loop until last-yyyy = first-yyyy
- perform leap-year check for last-yyyy.
- add 365 or 364 to last-ddd
- subtract 1 from last-yyyy

Subtract first-yyyy from last-yyyy

mike wrote:

> We have used the following chunk of code for years to do
> this calculation, but it seems to be missing a day for the
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>            EXIT.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: number of days between dates

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-03T15:17:19-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9304tj$92n$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<bQJ46.4569$jo.56099329@tomcat.sk.sympatico.ca> <3a538214$0$57191$272ea4a1@news.execpc.com> <ZHL46.4584$jo.52232273@tomcat.sk.sympatico.ca>`

```
What compiler are you using? (we may be able to help you "get at" the
functions).

Also (probably more important),

I find the following statements (in your original code) to be suspicious
(especially with the results that you received),

 COMPUTE LEAP-ADJUST = THIS-YEAR / 100 - THIS-YEAR / 400.
 COMPUTE LEAP-DAYS = THIS-YEAR / 4 - LEAP-ADJUST.


The *full* rule for Leap Years is -

If divisible by 4 then it is a leap-year
  Unless it is divisible by 100 in which case it is NOT a leap year
      Unless it is also divisible by 400 in which case it IS a leap year

My guess is that your "algorithm" is treating 2000 as NOT a leap-year -
which it was.
```

```
I think a two table approach would be far simpler.

        if year is greater than 2050 or year is less than 1950
            display "table in module x needs updating" on console
            else
                subtract 1949 from year giving year-subscript.
        COMPUTE BASEDATE = year-table (year-subscript) + month-table (month)
+ day.

Placing it in a subroutine would isolate it easily.  That is what I have
been doing for years, and it will work with very slight modifications
for any precision, including two digit years.  Today, I would place it
in an object, so I would not need to recompile the programs using it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: number of days between dates

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-03T17:59:28-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<930apl$ccu$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<bQJ46.4569$jo.56099329@tomcat.sk.sympatico.ca> <3a538214$0$57191$272ea4a1@news.execpc.com> <ZHL46.4584$jo.52232273@tomcat.sk.sympatico.ca> <9304tj$92n$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
The algorithm is fine. It's just not very clear.

First, THIS-YEAR means the end of the previous year.

Second, replace LEAP-ADJUST in the second COMPUTE,
with the right -side of the equation from the first COMPUTE
(be sure to change the sign), the algorithm is:

1. Total all years divisible by 4 as leap years.
2. Subtract all years divisible by 100 as NOT leap years.
3. Add all years divisible by 400 as leap years.

The result is the number of leap days between 31 Dec 1600
and 31 Dec of the prior year.

The problem seems to be that the first COMPUTE is
rounding instead of truncating the intermediate results.

When I ran the code using MF 3.2, I got the same error.
After adding $SET ARITHMETIC"VSC2" the results were
correct. The default for MF 3.2 is to round. The directive
forces truncation of intermediate results.
------------------
Rick Smith

William M. Klein wrote in message <9304tj$92n$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>...
>What compiler are you using? (we may be able to help you "get at" the
>functions).
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com
[...]
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: number of days between dates

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-03T19:00:54-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<930ecr$gem$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<bQJ46.4569$jo.56099329@tomcat.sk.sympatico.ca> <3a538214$0$57191$272ea4a1@news.execpc.com> <ZHL46.4584$jo.52232273@tomcat.sk.sympatico.ca> <9304tj$92n$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <930apl$ccu$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
Some additional results:

1. Replacing the first COMPUTE with two DIVIDE instructions
GIVING integer values and combing those values works.

2. The second COMPUTE did not show any problem
(using MF 3.2) in tests for dates in 2001 through 2005.

3. The number of days calculated with the existing code is
one day greater than the number from FUNCTION
INTEGER-OF-DATE. (With the corrections, the numbers
are the same.) If the result was only used to find the
difference in days this would not be a problem. If the result
is stored anywhere, correcting the program will cause an
error of one day when comparing against the previously
stored result.

4. The routine will not work correctly for dates in the year
2100.
------------------
Rick Smith

Rick Smith wrote in message <930apl$ccu$1@news.hitter.net>...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: number of days between dates

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-04T08:44:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A543753.AC98DD36@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<bQJ46.4569$jo.56099329@tomcat.sk.sympatico.ca> <3a53f389.173019735@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> That's funny. I use (used) similar code -- the problem is that 2001 is
> exactly 400 years from 1601.  I didn't try to figure it out - I just
> shifted to some other code I was given by Arnold Trembly.

Thane, you are too kind.

The very first COBOL program I ever wrote professionally, back in 1979,
was a routine to determine day-of-week for a date.  I believe it used
Zeller's congruence, and I didn't understand why it worked.  I had to
translate it from an old DOS/VS 360 assembler program in ANSI-74 COBOL
for an IBM Series/1 minicomputer.  I noticed that it needed a 4-digit
year in order to determine the day of week.  Even in 1979 we knew there
would be some issues with the year 2000.

I am no mathematician, but I can (usually) do arithmetic correctly.  The
problem of determining the number of days between two dates becomes
difficult when you cross a calendar year boundary or a century
boundary.  So I eventually developed my own routine.  It worked, but I
doubt that it is as efficient as a more elegent mathematical routine. 
The only advantage my routine had was that I understood how it worked.  

Basically, the way I determined the number of days between two dates was
to convert each date into an absolute number of days from a base date. 
I used January 1st, 0001 as the base date, assuming the current
gregorian calendar rules.  Then it's easy to determine the number of
days between two dates.  It's also easy to get the day of week.  Just
convert to an absolute number of days from the base date, divide by 7,
and convert the remainder.  January 1, 0001 would have been a Monday,
and day 000001 of the absolute day count.  A 400-year cycle has exactly
146,097 days and is evenly divisible by 7.  So January 1, 2001,
(absolute day count 730,486) is also a Monday (730,486 mod 7 = 1, or
Monday).   

Converting a yyyy-mm-dd date into an absolute day count is not too bad. 
Converting an absolute day count back into a yyyy-ddd date or a
yyyy-mm-dd date was considerably harder!

If you have COBOL intrinsic functions, you can save yourself a lot of
work by using them instead. 
```

```
Arnold Trembley wrote:

> The very first COBOL program I ever wrote professionally, back in 1979,
> was a routine to determine day-of-week for a date.  I believe it used
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

the 400 year rule, their code still works.
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Timothy" <timhillock@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-05T03:00:09+00:00

```
Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:3A54A3ED.B5FE38FC@brazee.net...
> Arnold Trembley wrote:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> What's funny is that the people who were completely ignorant about the
year
> 2000 leap year issues were OK.  If they left out both the 100 year rule
and
> the 400 year rule, their code still works.

In about 80 to 98 years they better get it right.  For now the 4 year rule
is good enough for most programs.   I bet that the only programs that would
have the 100 and 400 year rule, are those where they are looking at dates
over 100 years on either side of Y2K.  Of those, I predict that a few of
these don't have the 100 and 400 rule.

The thing is, have we learned from Y2K or not.   I know that I won't be
around by 2100 (something to do with aging) but I bet that by then there
will be a tiny Y2K.  Not involving the size of the date field but the leap
year rules.  Of course, the debate of when the next century begins will
still be around.   The next predicted major date change will be in about
7980 years(based on the current calendar) excluding the Unix date problem in
about 35 years (I think).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: number of days between dates

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-04T19:38:44-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A5541C4.4977@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<bQJ46.4569$jo.56099329@tomcat.sk.sympatico.ca> <3a53f389.173019735@news1.attglobal.net> <3A543753.AC98DD36@worldnet.att.net> <3A54A3ED.B5FE38FC@brazee.net> <ZMa56.86227$59.23811760@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
Timothy wrote:

> In about 80 to 98 years they better get it right.  For now the 4 year rule
> is good enough for most programs.   I bet that the only programs that would
> have the 100 and 400 year rule, are those where they are looking at dates
> over 100 years on either side of Y2K.  Of those, I predict that a few of
> these don't have the 100 and 400 rule.

Lots of people use Julian dates (number of days since Jan 1, 4713 BC) internally in 
their programs.  It actually makes life simpler, since date arithmetic is just like any 
other arithmetic.  You just have a "to-julian" and a "from-julian" conversion routine.  
Those programs will be OK, because the standard Julian conversions will work for any 
date since the Pope messed with the calendar.  I actually included this in some 
corporate programming standards I wrote in 1975, along with the two conversion 
routines, so that everybody's dates would be usable by anybody else.
```

```
birth.
```

#### ↳ Re: number of days between dates

- **From:** Keith Odam <0dam-k@iname.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-13T10:47:54+10:30
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<I_M76.2068$65.14080@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`
- **References:** `<bQJ46.4569$jo.56099329@tomcat.sk.sympatico.ca>`

```
I may have missed it amongst the discussion of programming style,
but has anyone noticed that the "days since Dec 31,1600" is correct
for 1 Jan 2001, and wrong for 31 Dec 2000 and before?
  (Probably one day wrong from 1701 to 2000).

A couple of people mentioned that they were suspicious of the
COMPUTE statement(s), but I suggest that the first one:
>     COMPUTE LEAP-ADJUST = THIS-YEAR / 100 - THIS-YEAR / 400.
is maintaining precision until the final delivery of a result.
  In year 2000:
truncate( 399/100-399/400=3.99-0.9975=2.9925) = 2
and not the 3-0 = 3 that you might have expected.
  In 2001, 400/100-400/400=4-1=3 is the result you expected.

  If this is the case, then you could either separate the divisions
to ensure truncation occurs as you expect, although I would prefer
to make one simpler multiplication.
COMPUTE INT-OUT = 365.2425 * THIS-YEAR.

 If you don't really need to handle dates before the twentieth
century though, a base of 1 Jan 1901 and using 365.25 would be
more obvious.  After all, you can ignore the century rules for
all years from 1901 to 2099 inclusive.

Keith

mike wrote:
> We have used the following chunk of code for years to do

…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
```

#### ↳ Re: ILBOWAT0 Information

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 2001-01-04T08:07:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<931avf$o8k$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<3A53E516.68F15E2B@attglobal.net>`

```
In article <3A53E516.68F15E2B@attglobal.net>,
  iuvo@attglobal.net wrote:
> Hi:
> I understand that the IBM supplied routine ILBOWAT0 can be called by a
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

AFAIK, ILBOWAT0 dates back to old OS/VS COBOL days. You might find a
documentation in some 20 years old manuals. I never found descriptions
about ILBOWAT0 in the current IBM documentation. The module resides in
the COB2LIB as well as in the most current SCEERUN.

A disassembly of ILBOWAT0 shows SVC 47, so I would not use it in a CICS
environment. SVC 47 is the ASSEMBLER macro STIMER, you find it
documented in the manual 'OS/390 V2R10.0 MVS Assembler Services
Reference', Document Number GC28-1910-11, online at:
http://www.s390.ibm.com:80/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/IEA1A741


BTW, ILBOWAT0 is an ALIAS of ILBOWAT:

ILBOWAT  CSECT
         ENTRY ILBOWAT0
         DC    H'0'
ILBOWAT0 BC    15,4(0,R15)
         STM   R14,R12,96(R13)
...

If you call ILBOWAT instead of ILBOWAT0, you get an abend S0C1 because
of the invalid instruction H'0'!

I once wrote the following COBOL sample program (note the DATA(24)
compiler option, ILBOWAT0 has AMODE(24)):

       CBL DATA(24)
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. WAIT.
      ***
      *** DELAY IN COBOL VIA CALL TO ILBOWAT0
      ***
      *** USAGE:
      *** //S1 EXEC PGM=WAIT,PARM=NNNN
      *** NNNN IS A NUMBER IN THE RANGE OF 0001 TO 9999 SECONDS
      ***
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  DELAY-AMT               PIC S9(9) COMP.
       01  ILBOWAT0                PIC X(8) VALUE 'ILBOWAT0'.
       01  CURRENT-TIME            PIC 9(8).
       LINKAGE SECTION.
       01  PARAMETER-TO-MAIN.
         05 PARM-LENGTH PIC S9(4) COMP.
         05 PARM-TEXT   PIC X(100).
         05 PARM-REDEF REDEFINES PARM-TEXT.
          10 PARM-NUMERIC PIC 9999.
          10 FILLER       PIC X(94).
       PROCEDURE DIVISION USING PARAMETER-TO-MAIN.
       MAIN SECTION.
           MOVE PARM-NUMERIC TO DELAY-AMT
           ACCEPT CURRENT-TIME FROM TIME
           DISPLAY 'TIME OF DAY BEFORE ILBOWAT0: ', CURRENT-TIME
      *
           CALL ILBOWAT0 USING DELAY-AMT
      *
           ACCEPT CURRENT-TIME FROM TIME
           DISPLAY 'TIME OF DAY AFTER ILBOWAT0 : ', CURRENT-TIME
           GOBACK.
```
