###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: number of days between dates

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-01-16T10:56:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A6428C8.7DC31226@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<bQJ46.4569$jo.56099329@tomcat.sk.sympatico.ca> <3a538214$0$57191$272ea4a1@news.execpc.com> <ZHL46.4584$jo.52232273@tomcat.sk.sympatico.ca> <9304tj$92n$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <aTQ46.1354$Er.229814@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net> <3A54304E.EFC47152@worldnet.att.net> <938dom$1q0$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A5967AD.2724D2B0@Azonic.co.nz> <3a5919c6.2444248@nntp.sprynet.com> <3A6327F5.1ED9DD79@Azonic.co.nz> <3a634de8.5032339@nntp.sprynet.com>`

```
Frank Swarbrick wrote:
> >>
> >> This way you can easily search for your variable by name, and yet have value
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> 88-level is used.

Yes of course, that is why I suggested the alternatives (where available
and suitable) of 78s or equivalents or of symbolic constants.

Using a literal value in code _may_ (depending on compiler) create a new
entry in the literal pool for each usage.  So:

	AT END MOVE "Y"		TO EoF
	..
	IF ( EoF = "Y" )

may indeed cost 2 literal table entries.  Whereas use of a descriptive
variable with this value will not create any literal table entry but
will have a variable declaration.  Thus the variables will save space
compared to using literals.

Whether an 88 level generates a separate literal entry for each usage or
uses some other mechanism depends on how the implementation has been
done.  for example it may be that a particular compiler could convert:

        SET EoF-Detected TO TRUE
  into  MOVE "Y"      TO EoF-Flag

and then compile this code giving a new literal table entry for each
reference.		

Which code 'wastes' more space is not a simple matter of asserting that
one or the other will.


> Also, in the case of C enumerated types I don't think you can set the variable
> to a value of anything other than the specified enumerated types.

Yes of course, that is why I suggested the alternatives (where available
and suitable) of 78s or equivalents or of symbolic constants.

Decades ago I used a compiler that had a 'CONSTANTS SECTION' that
prevented changing the values of the decalrations within it.

> Anyway, no big deal.  I'm only stating my personal bias/preference.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: number of days between dates

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-01-10T13:46:40+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<93hlco$gb1$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<bQJ46.4569$jo.56099329@tomcat.sk.sympatico.ca> <3a538214$0$57191$272ea4a1@news.execpc.com> <ZHL46.4584$jo.52232273@tomcat.sk.sympatico.ca> <9304tj$92n$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <aTQ46.1354$Er.229814@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net> <3A54304E.EFC47152@worldnet.att.net> <938dom$1q0$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`

```
Well,

That certainly evoked some passionate responses.

I had no idea you guys cared so much...<G>

It was interesting that while many of you, quite properly, disputed my
opinions, nobody actually said they didn't like the suggested code.

Actually, I don't care what people use as flag and switch values...as long
as it is documented with an 88 level so that it is never referenced. I would
agree that coding:

if switch = '1' ...

is every bit as bad as coding:

if switch = 'Y' ...

or coding:

if switch = [anything else]

None of it tells us anything about the purpose of switch.

As for Upper/Lower case, again, my personal preference is for Mixed and a
number of studies support this view, but, hey, if you prefer something else,
good.

One of the problems is that when people first start out in COBOL (and my
message was aimed at newcomers) there is no way of knowing what is good
style or what isn't, and why.

Style is necessarily based on opinion and this will differ.

My opinion is based on 35 years of using the Language on all kinds of
platforms and environments all over the planet...so I feel qualified to
offer an opinion.

However, what is offered is just that: an opinion. (albeit, an informed one)

I will gladly give the reasons which caused me to reach this opinion, but it
is no more valid than anybody else's and you will notice that the code I
posted was "suggested".

Now, anybody like to change the "suggested" code?

Further comments below...


Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote in message
<938dom$1q0$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>...

>Arnold Trembley wrote in message <3A54304E.EFC47152@worldnet.att.net>...
><Snipped>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I like this solution very much. It will run on just about ANY machine and
compiler options are not going to make any difference to it.

However, it is NOT a good example of  modern COBOL code and, as there are a
number of new COBOL programmers who frequent this forum I suggest it be
amended as follows:

01   year-yyyy.
       12  year-cc pic 99.
       12  year-yy pic 99.
[there is no need for the redefinition, and usage defaults to DISPLAY...]

01   leap-year-flag  pic x value space.
        88 its-a-leap-year    value '1'.
        88 its-NOT-a-leap-year value '0'.

01  leap-year-test pic 9.
       88 no-remainder  value zero.

01  useless-junk    pic 99.

...

if year-yy = zero
   divide year-cc by 4
               giving useless-junk
               remainder leap-year-test
else
   divide year-yy by 4
              giving useless-junk
               remainder leap-year-test
end-if
if no-remainder
   set its-a-leap-year to TRUE
else
   set its-NOT-a-leap-year to TRUE
end-if

if its-a-leap-year
      [etc....]


I shall now annotate the opinions expressed below...[annotation in square
brackets]
>>>
"Please, please, newbies, resist the urge to set flags to 'Y' or 'N'. Use 88
levels; that's what they're for. "

[This should be updated to say "resist the urge to TEST flags with 'Y or
'N'". The values can be anything you feel comfortable with]


COBOL is intended to be a Self-Documenting
Language... Besides, it is arrogant to assume the whole world uses "Yes" and
"No"  (it doesn't) and, in terms of pure logic, "1" and "0" are better
Boolean representations of a truth value.)

[This cannot be argued, as anyone who has studied Boolean Algebra and
Propositional Calculus will understand. You CAN represent values with
ANYTHING, but it is then no longer Boolean (George Boole based his Algebra
on a Binary system, using 1 and 0. Despite the Poison Dwarf's attempt to
muddy the water, 1 is TRUE and 0 is FALSE. (Sorry, Doc, it's defined that
way and is therefore axiomatic, SO LONG as you want to call it
"Boolean"...<G> I also realise that you may well decide not to do so, in
which case whatever you want is OK...<G>))]

Unfortunately, not all implementations of COBOL allow a value to be set to
FALSE, so it is necessary to provide for both TRUE and FALSE with 88 levels,
then set the required one to TRUE, as shown above.

[No comment. Bill Klein might like to comment on where the COBOL standard is
with Set ...to FALSE?]

Also, where your environment supports it, use lower case ("there's no need
to SHOUT") as it is easier on the eyes than Upper Case.
[However, if you are working on a site or in an environment where this is
not the norm, adhere to what is usual.]

Pete.
```

This sounds like a homework question - so rather than given answers, I'll
give resources - but that is what you ask for.  

Most COBOL texts would give information on data types.  You could also
download the Fujitsu COBOL v3 compiler and look at the reference manual
provided with the compiler.

An article on "Data Structures and Algorithms: Data Structures in
COBOL" was recently published on the COBOL Reports website
(http://cobolreport.com) by Leif Svalgaard.  The copy I have is dated
18 September 2000.  I don't know if it is still archived or not.

Hope that helps.

Floyd Johnson

#### ↳ Re: help on.... data types, data structures and string please!!!!!

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-04T09:02:50-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xg056.340$4Z.21939@news2.atl>`
- **References:** `<931tvi$sr$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
'Nightfly' also posted this question in a BASIC newsgroup.  So either
it is a troll, or 'Nightfly' is interested in the subject generally.

I recommend any or all of the following books:

    "Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours", Thane Hubbell

    "COBOL Unleashed", Jon Wessler, et al.

    "Algorithms + Data Structures = Programs", Niklaus Wirth

    "Algorithms in (pick language)", Robert Sedgewick

    "The Art of Computer Programming", Donald Knuth

The above books all cover data structures and basic algorithms.  The
Knuth books (there are several in the series) are considered by many
to be 'The Programmer's Reference'.  However, only the first two are
specifically COBOL books.  "Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours" is an
excellent introduction to COBOL.  "COBOL Unleashed" is a more advanced
book for those who already know at least some COBOL.  Both books come
with free COBOL compilers on CD-ROM, and the authors (several for the
'Unleashed' book) frequent this newsgroup.  Sedgewick has written
several books in the "Algorithms in ..." series (e.g. "Algorithms in C"
and "Algorithms" which uses Pascal), but I don't believe there is one
specifically for COBOL.  "Algorithms + Data Structures = Programs" is
a classic that I consider a 'must read' for any serious programmer.
The language used is Pascal naturally, for Wirth (pronounced 'Veert')
is the inventor of Pascal.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: help on.... data types, data structures and string please!!!!!

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-04T13:09:35-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<932e3q$818$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<931tvi$sr$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk> <xg056.340$4Z.21939@news2.atl>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote in message ...

>The language used is Pascal naturally, for Wirth (pronounced 'Veert')
>is the inventor of Pascal.
>--

Way back in 1968-69, I was taking an undergraduate course in compiler
design.  We wrote a compiler for a new language in Fortran IV, the idea
being that it would compile a bootstrap compiler written in a new language.

The test was to compile twice, once with the bootstrap, then with the actual
code, and compare object.  Since there were only four of us taking this new
fangled computer science stuff, and we had five professors to teach us four
courses (including one in Fortran IV), we just sorta smoked a lot of dope
and
set it up as one big project with seven workers, and four undergraduate
credits
at the end of it.

The project was done on a PDP-10 with a Univac 9300 RJE station (card reader
printer) and a trans-Atlantic line for ten minutes per night.  I think it
was to the
University of Zurich.

About five years later, I was teaching a course in Pascal, and I checked
some
old listings.  I came to the stunned realisation that we had written the
bootstrap
for Pascal.  So here I am, having written a Pascal compiler, taught several
Pascal
courses, and I have never written anything but trivial stuff in the
language. I do
not even like the language particularly, though I have worked in lots worse.

If I remember correctly, one of the profs for that venture was Key Dewdney.
Key
took over the Mathematical Games column of Scientific American from Martin
Gardner.  Interesting man.  I did an art show with him where we morphed a
fat
lady into an Ice-cream cone as a series of prints, done on a PDP-8, during
the same period.  Had some good times playing with equipment back then.

Donald <wandering about while a half million lines re-compile>
```

#### ↳ Re: help on.... data types, data structures and string please!!!!!

- **From:** lcccpiasabird@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2001-01-04T16:25:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<932865$g73$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<931tvi$sr$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
In COBOL the variables or fields are described in the top part of the
program.  To describe them they use a Picture Clause which has the
letters PIC a space then the data type followed by parentheses(spell)
with the size of the field in it.  The programs I have done have fields
with a set field length.

EXAMPLE:  01 ENE-OF-FILE PIC X(03) VALUE "YES".

X is for an Alpha-Numeric field.  In COBOL there is no string class
like JAVA has or some variations of C++.  However there is a STRING
command that allows concatanation, etc. "A" would be for strictly
Alphabetic fields and isnt used much, and "9" is for numeric fields.
There are variations for Packed numbers, hex characters, and binary
data, but I wont go in that direction.  In COBOL you can create tables
or arrays using the "OCCOURS" statement creating a series of fields
that can be referred to with a sub-script method.  For isntance if
TOTAL OCCURS 5 TIMES, then TOTAL (1) is the first one, etc.  In C++
that would be TOTAL[0].  In COBOL the subscripts must start at 1 and be
whole numbers.  SOME compilers let you choose part of a field in the
following manner:

FIELD-1 (5,3)

This translates to the variable "FIELD-1" STARTING AT THE FIFTH ELEMENT
USE 3 CHARACTERS.  This is similar to ASSEMBLY; in fact a Mainframe's
operating system uses ASSEMBLY.  This is a type of referential
addressing.

Pardon my spelling.  As the others stated I suggest you get a
referrence book for complete COBOL SYNTAX.  Another suggestion is a
book on Structured Programming Logic.  If they offer a class in this
subject at your school someone may have one they could let you use.

Charles



In article <931tvi$sr$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>,

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help with Fujitsu Cobol 4.0

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-01-05T12:41:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3a55c0a7.83351926@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<1empzr9.64vfgj1l4g7jqN%f.regnier@worldonline.fr> <yS156.81$t6.7714@news.corpcomm.net> <1emrfmu.1s2841k1s3eimsN%f.regnier@worldonline.fr>`

```
It looks like your program is probably in the "My Documents" folder.
Early versions of Fujitsu COBOL had trouble with directory names that
had embedded spaces.  Create a new folder without spaces in the name
and move your source code there, then try compiling.

<a id="t-45e981e49f"></a>
## Thread: WARNING! — 23 msgs

### WARNING!

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-01-05T10:12:54+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<932qk2$ort$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`

```
There is a virus around called MTX and my system is currently infected with
it.

I cannot establish beyond doubt where it came from but I have reason to
believe it may have been from private posts out of this Newsgroup.

DO NOT OPEN ANY ATTACHMENTS TO E-MAIL WHERE THERE IS NO MESSAGE, AND
ESPECIALLY, IF YOU HAVE RECEIVED MAIL FROM ME WITH ATTACHMENTS IN THE LAST
FEW DAYS IT MAY HAVE BEEN INFECTED.

I can only say sorry, it wasn't done deliberately...

The virus manifests itself by attaching to outgoing e-mails. If you open the
attachment, you are likely to become infected.

DO NOT OPEN any attachments to this message! Simply delete the second
message.

This is a particularly nasty virus as it actually has three components: the
virus, a worm, and a Trojan Horse. Although it is not especially malicious,
it opens a port on your system from which it can update itself from the
Internet. It has another nasty aspect in that it monitors what sites you are
accessing on the net and will not allow you to access any virus protection
sites which may have an anti virus.

It is difficult to remove (although, believe me I am working on it...!)
because it installs into and corrupts certain system .dlls.

If you are sure your system is clean, you would be well advised to update
your anti-virus software (mine was 41 days out of date...) and treat any
e-mails you are not expecting (even from trusted sources) with suspicion if
they have attachments.

Sadly,

Pete.

PS If you are currently expecting private response from me, please be
patient. I am not sending any e-mails until this problem is sorted
(hopefully within the next 12 hours)
```

spread of virii via VBS scripting is largly an OUTLOOK problem and not
a Windows problem.  It's software on the OS, not the OS itself.  I run
Pegasus email and I don't have a problem with these types of virii.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: WARNING!

- **From:** Calvin Crumrine <Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us>
- **Date:** 2001-01-05T12:43:38-09:00

```
Don't count yourself safe just because you don't use IE & OUTLOOK. In my
experience most people who don't use these simply ignore them vs. removing
them. There are now some viruses (virii? Technically I think they're
worms.) that use IE/OUTLOOK to run, but will execute this quite well in
background after being launched from an email received in another client.
(Won't guarantee all clients do this, specifically I don't know about
Pegasus.) I'm probably not explaining this well-I can't remember/find the
details but I remember the alert saying that we needed to either install
the security path from Microsoft or remove the insecure controls. (Don't
recall whether these were .dlls or Active X, but they aren't essential to
IE/OUTLOOK even though they're installed with them.)

Thane Hubbell wrote:

> Not that I'm a big Windows fan, because I'm not - but the rampant
> spread of virii via VBS scripting is largly an OUTLOOK problem and not

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-06T10:35:22-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<932qk2$ort$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A5575B7.12FEE8E1@Josella-Simone-Playton.de> <3a55c027.83223476@news1.attglobal.net> <yFv56.2520$Ps.110858@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3a573633.47507492@news1.attglobal.net>`

"Thane Hubbell" <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote:
> Outlook installs itself with every MS product you install - but it has
> an uninstall, unlike IE (Which I like).  However, even if one cannot
> remove Outlook - you don't have to ever use it.

Just want to clarify a point.  It is 'MS Outlook Express' that installs
with Windows.  'MS Outlook' is a distinct and much more elaborate app.
that is part of 'MS Office'.  Some problems with 'Outlook' do not apply
to 'Outlook Express', and probably vice-versa. :-)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: WARNING!

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-01-06T02:09:09+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<934ime$raj$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<932qk2$ort$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A5575B7.12FEE8E1@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>`

```
I have noticed a growing smugness amongst UNIX (and UNIX-flavoured) users.

There are viruses which can attack UNIX systems just as easily; in fact UNIX
was more famous in its infancy for the holes in its security than for its
processing power.

Mainframes may be less prone to viruses (I believe this is because, in
general, they are less open to attack from the Internet by not being
connected to it, and also the majority of people working on them are
computer professionals who have nothing to prove, as well as the
architecture being more secure), but mischievious programs have been written
for the Mainframe since I was a junior programmer (and that is a VERY long
time ago...<G>).

I well remember being carpeted because I wrote a program which emulated
mainframe DOS system messages and caused an Engineering call to be made to
IBM...My defence that it was April the First cut very little ice and I kept
my job by the skin of my teeth and the fact that in those days programmers
were in very short supply... I never wound up the Operations people again...

The point is that we should all be on our toes regarding malevolent idiots
who derive some pleasure in their sad lives from  this kind of endeavour.

In fact it took me 6 hours to research, isolate, and remove the virus, worm,
and Trojan and clean up the infected system .dlls. This is not so bad, and I
learned a great deal in the exercise... I also found out how it slipped
through the protection I had in place which should have trapped it.  (this
was caused by taking a default in a configuration when I should've thought
about it...)

So, on the positive side it was a learning experience, but not one I would
want to have every day...

If anyone else has this virus and needs help, mail me. (yes, infected mail
is OK...no problem...<G>)

Pete.



Herwig Huener & Josella Simone Playton wrote in message
<3A5575B7.12FEE8E1@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>...
>2001-01-05 08:20:00 MET
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>GruberStrasse 10 A / D-85655 GrossHelfenDorf / Bayern / EU
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: WARNING!

```
In article <934ime$raj$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>,
Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

[snippage]

>Mainframes may be less prone to viruses (I believe this is because, in
>general, they are less open to attack from the Internet by not being
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>time ago...<G>).

Weeelllllll... I recall, a few years back, a fellow I knew who was doing
some maniframe work as a student in New York State - either Union College
or RPI, I cannot recall which - back when state o' the art was twin,
slaved 360s.  The goal then was to write a program which would use up
enough resources that it would cause the system to crash before Ops could
find and terminate it; such programs were called 'wabbits'.

When such a program was discovered the cry would go up amongst the
operators of 'Catch dat scwewy wabbit!'... and if the system went down the
perpertrator then had to walk past the Ops Desk while they were trying to
bring the system back up and *not* crack a smile.

Ahhhhh, for the Oldene Dayse...

DD
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: WARNING!

- **From:** "Cliff Peterson" <cliffpeterson@spamcop.net>

```
"Herwig Huener & Josella Simone Playton"
<webmaster@Josella-Simone-Playton.de> wrote in message
news:3A5575B7.12FEE8E1@Josella-Simone-Playton.de...
>
> > There is a virus ...
>
> A new case for Linux or any UNIX-brand, isn't it?

From the Wall Street Journal Interactive this morning...
----------------------------------------------------------
WASHINGTON -- Computer experts discovered a flaw in widely used software
that could let hackers hijack corporate and government Web sites and steal
sensitive e-mail.

The flaw in software that controls most of the world's Internet traffic was
quickly deemed a "critical" threat. It affects a popular software known as
BIND (Berkeley Internet Name Domain) that operates the Internet's equivalent
of global telephone directories. Experts warned that hackers could change or
delete entries in those directories at their whim, reroute Internet traffic
or shut down Web sites.

Researchers at the federally funded Coordination Center -- formerly known as
the computer emergency response team -- said the flaw "threatens the
Internet's integrity" because the BIND software is "arguably the Internet's
single most important software package." The federal government also issued
urgent warnings Monday to its civilian agencies.

The flaw affects the latest version of so-called BIND software created by
the Internet Software Consortium for Unix and Linux computers used by
companies. BIND is arguably the Internet's single most important software
package and the flaw threatens the Internet's integrity, say experts.
----------------------------------------------------------

Hmmm...

Linux and UNIX invulnerability appears to have been based on their
obscurity.  Who wanted to attack an operating system with three users?
Maybe we all need to move to BeOS now... ;-)

Cliff
```

- **References:** `<932qk2$ort$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A5575B7.12FEE8E1@Josella-Simone-Playton.de> <Oizd6.4565$mA1.402631@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <956fuh$ntg$1@dackel.pdb.sbs.de>`

```
"Herwig Huener" <Herwig.Huener!@!fujitsu-siemens.com> wrote in message
news:956fuh$ntg$1@dackel.pdb.sbs.de...
> 2001-01-30 14:30:00 MET
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> be removed in no time.

...so, as a hacker, I have the source code to examine when I look for
weaknesses.  I like that...

I use Linux (a little).  I have Red Hat installed under System Commander.
It's okay, but it reminds me of the old joke - "a camel is a horse built by
a committee".  Each piece is written by a different, sometimes eccentric
programmer - there's Bob's this, and Ralph's that, and Joe's the other.  If
you don't have a driver for a peripheral, you can write one or search around
to see if anyone has stopped hang gliding long enough to write it.

But - any operating system becomes more vulnerable as more people use it -
its very popularity sparks the interest of computer vandals.  Bad guys don't
want to write destructive software for a system used by fifty people.

Cliff
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: WARNING!

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Herwig Huener <Herwig.Huener!@!fujitsu-siemens.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-30T16:32:26+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<956mvk$rnf$1@dackel.pdb.sbs.de>`
- **References:** `<932qk2$ort$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A5575B7.12FEE8E1@Josella-Simone-Playton.de> <Oizd6.4565$mA1.402631@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <956fuh$ntg$1@dackel.pdb.sbs.de> <cSzd6.4583$mA1.404389@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
2001-01-30 16:21:34 MEZ

Cliff Peterson schrieb:
> 
> ...

> ...so, as a hacker, I have the source code to examine when I look for
> weaknesses.  I like that...

Same thing as with cryptography: The security *must not* rely in the
secrecy of algorithms. - It is highly desired that crackers may
peek into the source code. You have a testing crew for free that
way!

> I use Linux (a little).  I have Red Hat installed under System Commander.
> It's okay, but it reminds me of the old joke - "a camel is a horse built by
> a committee".  Each piece is written by a different, sometimes eccentric
> programmer - there's Bob's this, and Ralph's that, and Joe's the other.

The central tools which make up the productivity for a
programming job are Posix-compliant and are the same on
all UNIX-boxes. (Oftenly, the Linux-flavour of such tools
is brighter than Posix requires, but in
most cases, an additional option enforces strict Posix.)

Then, all often used things (Kernel itself, server daemons,
programming tools) are peered by many reviewers.
No too excentric programming style can survive. - Well, that's
the theory, also in the open source scene, programmers
age or loose interest, and the entropy of some pieces
of code raises unnecessarily, or code splits occur.

However, in some cases, you are right. I peeked into
source-code of some *rarely used* software, and I found a
terrible mess. Everybody can write a program and claim
it's open source.

> If you don't have a driver for a peripheral, you can write one or search
> around to see if anyone has stopped hang gliding long enough to write it.

Yes. True. BTW you can write the animal yourself!

> But - any operating system becomes more vulnerable as more people use it -

No. See above.

> its very popularity sparks the interest of computer
> vandals. Bad guys don't want to write destructive software
> for a system used by fifty people.

Yep. By exactly that reason Linux has to be safer in
order to survive.

Herwig
```

Herwig Huener wrote:
> 
> > I use Linux (a little).  I have Red Hat installed under System Commander.

Of course most large software is like that.  Did anyone think that
Microsoft wrote all (or even most) of its software ?  DOS was from SCP
(or via SCP from DRI), MS C was Lattice, IE was Mosaic, Front Page was,
ummmm, Front Page Inc.  VB was bought in.  Much Windows utility software
is 'Norton' or 'Central Point'.
 
> No too excentric programming style can survive. - Well, that's
> the theory, 

In the case of closed software no one would ever know, except the
in-house programmers.

> > No too excentric programming style can survive. - Well, that's
> > the theory,
>
> In the case of closed software no one would ever know, except the
> in-house programmers.

It's all relative.  We don't want our programs to look the same as what we did 20
years ago, even if we were still in the same closed shop.  So how much variation is
optimal?   I don't know.  How much is survivable?   Obviously quite a bit - in our
time frame.  (Is that closed shop going to survive significantly longer than a more
open environment?)

- **References:** `<932qk2$ort$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`

```
On Fri, 5 Jan 2001 10:12:54 +1300, "Peter E. C. Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> enlightened us:

>There is a virus around called MTX and my system is currently infected with
>it.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
<<snippage>>

Here's some information on that virus which is officially known as
W95.MTX:



Discovered on: August 17, 2000

 W95.MTX has a virus component and a worm component. It propagates
using email. Also it infects some Win32 executables in specific
directories. The virus also has the capability to block access to
certain web sites. This may prevent users from downloading new virus
definitions. 

Also known as: W95.Oisdbo, W95.MTX.dr, W95.MTX (.dll)

WORM COMPONENT
The worm component makes a copy of Wsock32.dll and names it
Wsock32.mtx. The Send export function of this .mtx file is then
modified to point to its own code. This allows the virus to mail a
copy of the worm infected with this virus to the same person to whom
the user sends an email (using the same program).

Here are a list of file names that this virus might use when it sends
the infected worm to other people. For those files with .pif
extensions, the .pif extension might not be visible in your mail
program.

                     I_wanna_see_you.txt.pif
                     Matrix_screen_saver.scr
                     Love_letter_for_you.txt.pif
                     New_playboy_screen_saver.scr
                     Bill_gates_piece.jpg.pif
                     Tiazinha.jpg.pif
                     Feiticeira_nua.jpg.pif
                     Geocities_free_sites.txt.pif
                     New_napster_site.txt.pif
                     Metallica_song.mp3.pif
                     Anti_cih.exe
                     Internet_security_forum.doc.pif
                     Alanis_screen_saver.scr
                     Reader_digest_letter.txt.pif
                     Win_$100_now.doc.pif
                     Is_linux_good_enough!.txt.pif
                     Qi_test.exe
                     Avp_updates.exe
                     Seicho_no_ie.exe
                     You_are_fat!.txt.pif
                     Free_xxx_sites.txt.pif
                     I_am_sorry.doc.pif
                     Me_nude.avi.pif
                     Sorry_about_yesterday.doc.pif
                     Protect_your_credit.html.pif
                     Jimi_hendrix.mp3.pif
                     Hanson.scr
                     F___ing_with_dogs.scr
                     Matrix_2_is_out.scr
                     Zipped_files.exe
                     Blink_182.mp3.pif

Wininit.ini is created by this component, which causes Wsock32.dll to
be deleted and Wsock32.mtx to be renamed to Wsock32.dll. Wininit.ini
executes after the computer is restarted. After Wininit.ini is
created, this component runs the virus component.

VIRUS COMPONENT
The virus component searches for specific antivirus programs running.
If the virus finds one, the virus does not run. If the virus continues
to run, it decompresses the worm component, drops a copy of it into
the user's Windows directory (typically C:\Windows), and runs it. The
name of this dropped file is Ie_pack.exe. After Ie_pack.exe is
executed, it is renamed to  Win32.dll.

The virus also drops Mtx_.Exe and runs it. This is a downloader
program that goes to a specific Web site (i.am/[MATRIX]) where
plug-ins for the virus are downloaded and executed. It searches for
Win32 executables in the current directory, Windows directory, and the
Temp directory. The file to be infected needs to have a size that is
not divisible by 101, is greater than 8K in size, and has at least 20
import call instructions. If not, the file is not infected by the
virus.

The virus also adds a registry entry that lets the downloader run
automatically every time the system is started. The downloader is
invisible in the Task List. 

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Help Save The Rainforest
For more info, please see:  
http://www.ran.org/ran/

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: WARNING!

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-01-09T17:04:40+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<93e4c9$as7$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<932qk2$ort$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <t5kjbtcuq8fbdc@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Thanks Jason,

I appreciate the offer of help.

However, I managed to get rid of it within 8 hours of it being brought to my
notice, and I mailed everyone (at least I hope I got them all...) with an
apology and an offer of help.

In actual fact, of 9 people mailed, only 1 actually needed some help. One of
them (a relative newcomer to computing) had re-formatted his hard drive and
re-installed everything. I explained to him that that was an effective
(although drastic) solution, and  also told him that as time goes by and we
accumulate more software and more configurations for it, this option becomes
less attractive until it is really no longer viable. Had to give the guy
credit though, he ignored all the hype from the local computer guru who said
he should surrender the system immediately for detox (at a hefty fee of
course) and thought it through himself.

Elsewhere in this thread I wrote:

"In fact it took me 6 hours to research, isolate, and remove the virus,
worm,
and Trojan and clean up the infected system .dlls. This is not so bad, and I
learned a great deal in the exercise... I also found out how it slipped
through the protection I had in place which should have trapped it.  (this
was caused by taking a default in a configuration when I should've thought
about it...)

So, on the positive side it was a learning experience, but not one I would
want to have every day...

If anyone else has this virus and needs help, mail me. (yes, infected mail
is OK...no problem...<G>)"





Jason Helfrich wrote in message ...
>I feel your pain. However, don't stress. I have read other replies and
>found a excellent solution.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: OT - New Millennium — 37 this month / 44 total

### OT - New Millennium

- **From:** "Timothy" <timhillock@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-05T03:27:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0bb56.86331$59.23867380@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
I was in New York City for New Years Eve, along with 500k others.  The next
day I went to the Empire State Building.  There was a gentleman with one of
those machines where you put in a coin and it gets bent out of shape and a
design is made out of it.   This machine produces on the coin, the design of
the building and has the Millennium on it.  I forgot to mentioned that he's
on the observation deck on the north/east side, in the inside portion.
Anyhow he is hawking "Get your new Millennium Coin here".   Some one had
asked him how can the Millennium be new when it was last year.  The guy
answered back saying the  Millennium really begins this year.  People were
trying to cash in on the confusion on when the New Millennium begins, last
year (or words to that effect).  Good sales man he was.   I didn't get a
good look to see if it indeed had a date of 2000 or 2001 on the sample
pressed coins.  If there is no date, well he's got the rest of the year to
make money.

I just don't know how long the booth will be there.  I don't plan to be back
in NYC this year to find out.

Oh yes in case some of you haven't seen them this year, there were glasses
with the 2001 design.  I think that they could be making them up to the year
2009.
```

much to do there.  One of my favorites is the Empire State building.  Been
to the top like 3 times.  What a sight.  It was probably a mob in Times
Square on New Years Eve, but I dunno if you were there.

Jeff

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - New Millennium

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-25T21:37:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<I01c6.10082$KJ3.607187@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<0bb56.86331$59.23867380@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <B67BF648.292%jeffbaynard@home.com> <3A6EFA37.4F91BFE2@freenet.uk.com>`

```

"Paul Barnett" <
>
> I do agree about the true start of the 3rd Millenium, there never
was a year zero
> which means that simple mathematics tells you that it starts 1st Jan
2001.

There wasn't a year '1' either. Nor a year '2' or '3' and so on up
until about 323 when the approximate date of Jesus' birth was
calculated and a new calendar implemented. Even so, the calculation
was off by (scholars now say) 3 to 8 years (for example, Herod is
known to have died in 4 B.C.).

Further, measurement of years, in Biblical Times, was based on a
zeroth numbering scheme: "In the first year of the reign of King
So-And-So implies sometime before the first anniversary of the king's
reign.
```

Jerry P wrote:

So the question is what is a meaningful way to measure a millennium.  From
a couple of weeks after the winter when Jesus was about 5 years old?   Is
that meaningful?   From when we have to change our computer data storage?
That's meaningful.   At least we don't have calendars based upon the
coronation of Hirohito (fortunately neither do the Japanese anymore).

- **Message-ID:** `<yc3c6.243655$59.61338952@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<0bb56.86331$59.23867380@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <B67BF648.292%jeffbaynard@home.com> <3A6EFA37.4F91BFE2@freenet.uk.com> <I01c6.10082$KJ3.607187@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3A70A431.7524BDC6@brazee.net>`

Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:3A70A431.7524BDC6@brazee.net...
>
>
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>
 --

First of all - Belated Happy New Year  for the Chinese.  It's the year 4699
for them.

Now onward and upward.....

1) Of course there was no year 1 (as a recorded date) for the people who
were living at the period of time around Christ's birth.  It didn't exist
until around 323 when the current calendar (and the slight changes around
1583) came into being.  The Monk who came up with calendar format didn't
have the concept of zero.  It came later from the Arabs.  Thus no year 0.

2) "In the first year of the reign of King..." to me implies that they are
talking about something that happened between the day that the King was put
on the throne to the last day of what was considered the length of a year at
that time (IE 364 days maybe).

3) "what is a meaningful way to measure a millennium"?   A millennium is
still 1,000 years no matter which calendar is used.  The question could be
stated as "When/what event does a millennium start counting from".   Chinese
have been counting for 4,699 years.  For those of the Jewish faith, I think
that their year is 57 something something.  For those of us of the Christian
faith, our year is 2001.   This is not the place to talk about why most of
the world is using this calendar.   Don't forget that there are other
calendars from other faiths as well.   Each of those have been counting from
some event important to them.

4) Lastly -  Who knows but maybe in the far future (500 -1000 years) if we
survive and expand to other solar systems,  our current calendar(used as the
main one) may be replaced by a new one.  Whatever calendar is used, a
millennium is still 1,000 years.   The length of a year...... well.....
that's something else.


Tim
Day  25 of the 21st Century and 3rd Millennium.
```

"Timothy" <timhillock@home.com
>
> 1) Of course there was no year 1 (as a recorded date) for the people
who
> were living at the period of time around Christ's birth.  It didn't
exist
> until around 323 when the current calendar (and the slight changes
around
> 1583) came into being.  The Monk who came up with calendar format
didn't
> have the concept of zero.  It came later from the Arabs.  Thus no
year 0.
>
> 2) "In the first year of the reign of King..." to me implies that
they are
> talking about something that happened between the day that the King
was put
> on the throne to the last day of what was considered the length of a
year at
> that time (IE 364 days maybe).

You're right. As an interesting aside, scholars have, for hundreds of
years, thought the chronology in Kings and Chronicles was so much
jibberish. It just didn't track. Then a British serving officer, and
amateur Biblical scholar (I forgot his name), sat down (presumably in
a tent with plenty of Gin) in India and tried to make sense of it all.
He found that the Bibilical chronology WAS consistent when three
heretofore asides were taken into consideration:

1. The kingdoms of Israel and Judea started their years at different
times: one at Rosh Hashanna (in the fall) and the other at Passover
(in the Spring).
2. Some kings took over before a 'new' year: sometimes this period was
counted as a 'year' and sometimes not.
3. Co-Regency: A served for 10 years, B served for 8 years, for a
total of 12 years.

>
> 3) "what is a meaningful way to measure a millennium"?   A
millennium is
> still 1,000 years no matter which calendar is used.  The question
could be
> stated as "When/what event does a millennium start counting from".
Chinese
> have been counting for 4,699 years.  For those of the Jewish faith,
I think
> that their year is 57 something something.  For those of us of the
Christian
> faith, our year is 2001. ....

Which brings up an interesting point: Why has (almost) everyone
scrapped the perfectly good Jewish calendar? There haven't been any
adjustments (or Y2K problems) in, oh, maybe 2000 years. Of course
there's an extra month (Adar II) thrown in twice every 18 years, but
what's art without suffering.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - New Millennium

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-26T15:13:41-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A71F695.D9204C68@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<0bb56.86331$59.23867380@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <B67BF648.292%jeffbaynard@home.com> <3A6EFA37.4F91BFE2@freenet.uk.com> <I01c6.10082$KJ3.607187@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3A70A431.7524BDC6@brazee.net> <yc3c6.243655$59.61338952@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <otmc6.11240$k%3.295753@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Jerry P wrote:

> Which brings up an interesting point: Why has (almost) everyone
> scrapped the perfectly good Jewish calendar? There haven't been any
> adjustments (or Y2K problems) in, oh, maybe 2000 years. Of course
> there's an extra month (Adar II) thrown in twice every 18 years, but
> what's art without suffering.

Most people never used it in the first place.   We used to use the
calendars of the conquerors, and now we use the calendars of our business
partners.

What types of clocks did everybody have?  Certainly the 24 hour division
of the day is not natural.  But the sailors who needed that type of
precision sailed around the world with their culture's time conventions.
```

Imagine living without any real concept of time, only the seasons and

years old" today, how would you have communicated that 100,000 years ago.

years, so it seems our calendar sort of gives a more limited view of how old
we really are.

Jeff

lived through.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - New Millennium

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-29T10:49:41-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Bahd6.2386$MA5.25906@news2.atl>`
- **References:** `<0bb56.86331$59.23867380@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <B67BF648.292%jeffbaynard@home.com> <3A6EFA37.4F91BFE2@freenet.uk.com> <I01c6.10082$KJ3.607187@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3A70A431.7524BDC6@brazee.net> <yc3c6.243655$59.61338952@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <otmc6.11240$k%3.295753@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <E%nc6.251673$59.62946983@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <B6979C02.1DE%jeffbaynard@home.com> <9681B3CB30378C74.B90E5AFED35F8370.EDA095B6F3B58168@lp.airnews.net>`

```
"SkippyPB" <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam> wrote
>
> Only in America...do drugstores make the sick walk all the way to the
> back of the store to get their prescriptions.

And hospitals are the only business I know of where a customer who is
about to spend $100k+ with the business is required to park farther
away than the employees, and the employees convenience is always put
ahead of the customer.  And this is when you are *really* sick!  Any
other business I know of where you are about to spend that much money,
the salesman would meet you at 3AM in the morning and buy you a meal
in the best restaurant in town, just hoping to get your business.
Even Wal-Mart employees park farther away than the customers!

While I'm on my soapbox, yesterday I went to my usual Doc-In-A-Box
with sinus problems.  They asked me to give them $100 'deposit' up
front.  I told them I and my family had been coming there for over
20 years and had always paid them before leaving the building.  "It
doesn't matter, that's the new policy."  "Not for me" I said, and
asked for my medical records to move to another doctor.  I walk out
the door without paying and they lose maybe $100, but if they give
me the wrong medicine, I could die.  And *they* want the deposit?!?
```

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<53gc6.1826$OP4.79748@news1.atl>`
- **References:** `<0bb56.86331$59.23867380@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <B67BF648.292%jeffbaynard@home.com> <3A6EFA37.4F91BFE2@freenet.uk.com> <I01c6.10082$KJ3.607187@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

"Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com> wrote:
>
> There wasn't a year '1' either. Nor a year '2' or '3' and so on up
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> known to have died in 4 B.C.).

Just because it wasn't *called* "Year 1" at the time, doesn't mean
there wasn't a "Year 1"!  Zero existed since the beginning of the
time, but man didn't recognize and name such it until relatively
recently. ;-)

P.S.- Just because the actual year of Jesus' birth was miscalculated
does not invalidate the calendar, or Jesus.  Both have had enormous
impact on the world we live in, and will continue to do so, whether
many people are too ignorant to know that 2001 is the first year of
the new millenium, or not. :-)

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - New Millennium

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-29T10:09:48-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A75A3DB.19BD677E@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<0bb56.86331$59.23867380@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <B67BF648.292%jeffbaynard@home.com> <3A6EFA37.4F91BFE2@freenet.uk.com> <I01c6.10082$KJ3.607187@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <53gc6.1826$OP4.79748@news1.atl> <3A727B8F.4046C815@Azonic.co.nz> <RpDc6.3779$Xc5.102706@news2.atl> <3A73CF8A.8801658B@Azonic.co.nz> <Nfhd6.2387$MA5.25977@news2.atl>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:

> Also true, but the remark I was replying to was in essence "Year 1
> does not and did not exist, because the Gregorian Calendar was not
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Calendar did, in fact, occur?  :-)

I have questions on when it started and when it ended.   In the history of
our calendar, things have moved around quite a bit.  If we go back by
precise one year increments, we won't always be in January.  I suppose when
we go back before the calendar, we pick our current iteration of the
calendar as what we are using (when is the first leap year before year 3?).

How do we program a calendar, with leap year rules and everything for eras
before our current calendar's rules existed?

And there is a philosophical question of whether a year actually exists at
all.  If there is a calendar in the middle of the forest, but nobody to see
it...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - New Millennium

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Support@ScreenIO.com (Kevin J. Hansen)
- **Date:** 2001-01-29T20:08:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3a75ce00.84004912@news>`
- **References:** `<0bb56.86331$59.23867380@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <B67BF648.292%jeffbaynard@home.com> <3A6EFA37.4F91BFE2@freenet.uk.com> <I01c6.10082$KJ3.607187@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <53gc6.1826$OP4.79748@news1.atl> <3A727B8F.4046C815@Azonic.co.nz> <RpDc6.3779$Xc5.102706@news2.atl> <3A73CF8A.8801658B@Azonic.co.nz> <Nfhd6.2387$MA5.25977@news2.atl> <3A75A3DB.19BD677E@brazee.net>`

```
On Mon, 29 Jan 2001 10:09:48 -0700, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>I have questions on when it started and when it ended.   In the history of
>our calendar, things have moved around quite a bit.  If we go back by
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>before our current calendar's rules existed?
============
It's worse than you think, because the Gregorian calendar was adopted
in different times in different countries.

In Rome, it was adopted in 1582.

Great Britain and its colonies adopted the Gregorian Calendar in 1752.

Russia didn't adopt it until 1918 (after the Revolution).

And, Alaska came under it in 1867 when it was purchased by the United
States; prior to that, of course, it used the Russian calendar.

FWIW, it's no real surprise that at least some of the Microsoft
Windows date functions only go back to 1601.  

If you really want to be a stickler on dates for international
applications, you must do quite a bit of research.

We have some information on leap-year rules and calendar history (plus
some links to calendar-information sites) on our site.

Kevin
Norcom - COBOL Programming Tools
GUI ScreenIO - A Windows UI for COBOL
http://www.screenio.com
```

> >before our current calendar's rules existed?

…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.screenio.com

I don't remember the exact dates, but it was in 1582, September when Rome
adopted the current calendar.  I think it went from September 12 (say a
Monday) to the next day (Tuesday) of September 23, 1582.   People had
thought that they had lost 10 days of their life.  Our daylight savings time
pales in comparison.

-- Tim
Day 25 of the 21st Century and 3rd Millenniun.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - New Millennium

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-01-30T07:34:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A766E76.18B8443F@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<0bb56.86331$59.23867380@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <B67BF648.292%jeffbaynard@home.com> <3A6EFA37.4F91BFE2@freenet.uk.com> <I01c6.10082$KJ3.607187@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <53gc6.1826$OP4.79748@news1.atl> <3A727B8F.4046C815@Azonic.co.nz> <RpDc6.3779$Xc5.102706@news2.atl> <3A73CF8A.8801658B@Azonic.co.nz> <Nfhd6.2387$MA5.25977@news2.atl> <3A75A3DB.19BD677E@brazee.net>`

Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> I have questions on when it started and when it ended.   In the history of
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> calendar as what we are using (when is the first leap year before year 3?).

There is also the problem that year numbers did not always start on Jan
1.  New year was often in March.  This still exists in such archaic
schemes as the tax year which starts April 1 here.

I am not even sure that year 1583 started or ended Jan 1, though this
can be checked, so, given this is the year that was 'correct' you should
be doing any unwinding from whenever that was.
```

The new year starting in March was for the old Roman Calendars.  With the
creation of our current calendar (in 353 AD), the new year was put to
January.

As for your tax year starting on "APRIL 1".......need I say more?

> I am not even sure that year 1583 started or ended Jan 1, though this
> can be checked, so, given this is the year that was 'correct' you should
> be doing any unwinding from whenever that was.

Day 29 of the 21st Century and 3rd Millenniun.

## Thread: [Q]Difference of Online and Batch in CICS? — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### [Q]Difference of Online and Batch in CICS?

- **From:** ChungKil Kim <chkim@initech.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-05T12:55:57+09:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A5545CD.C0394BD5@initech.com>`

```
i use COBOL to C call.
in batch it run ok.
but in online it does not properly run.
sometime run ok, and the other time not.

when it run ok, it receive some outdata(this data is setted by C func).
when it does not work, it does not receive outdata.

C function do encryption or decryption operation.

why sometime properly work, the others not work?

is there any difference online and batch mode?

i am new to COBOL.
my customer use C func that i make. he is COBOL user.
his ENV is OS/390 and CICS.

thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: [Q]Difference of Online and Batch in CICS?

- **From:** ChungKil Kim <chkim@initech.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-05T14:52:33+09:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A556121.9810CD10@initech.com>`
- **References:** `<3A5545CD.C0394BD5@initech.com>`

```
Online is CICS.
Batch is MVS.

ChungKil Kim wrote:

> i use COBOL to C call.
> in batch it run ok.
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: [Q]Difference of Online and Batch in CICS?

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 2001-01-05T16:19:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<934s5s$ncq$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<3A5545CD.C0394BD5@initech.com>`

```
In article <3A5545CD.C0394BD5@initech.com>,
  ChungKil Kim <chkim@initech.com> wrote:
> i use COBOL to C call.
> in batch it run ok.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> when it run ok, it receive some outdata(this data is setted by C
func).
> when it does not work, it does not receive outdata.
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

ILC calls between COBOL an C should work both in CICS and in batch mode.
Could you please give us more information about the error? Does it
depend on the calling COBOL program or does it occur with the same
program that runs OK sometimes? Does it run in one CICS, but not in
another CICS?

Have you debugged your programs? You could DISPLAY the parameter in
COBOL before and after the call to C and printf() it in your C program
at the start and the end of the program (assuming you use IBM C for
OS/390).

What levels of OS/390, CICS, C, COBOL and Language Environment are you
using?

If you give us more details, we can try to help you, but it's difficult
to diagnose an error without any informations.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: [Q]Difference of Online and Batch in CICS?

```
following is COBOL side code i use:

//YYENDEC  JOB S00907,'Y.B LEE',CLASS=S,MSGCLASS=X,
//             MSGLEVEL=(1,1)
//PROCLIB  JCLLIB ORDER=IGY.V1R2M0.SIGYPROC
//COB      EXEC IGYWCL,PARM.COBOL='OPT(FULL)'
//COBOL.SYSIN   DD *
       ID                              DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.                     COBTEST.
       ENVIRONMENT                     DIVISION.

       CONFIGURATION                   SECTION.
       SOURCE-COMPUTER.                IBM-9121.
       OBJECT-COMPUTER.                IBM-9121.

       DATA                            DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE                 SECTION.
       01  WS-RC                          PIC S9(04) COMP VALUE ZERO.
       01  WS-TYPE                        PIC  X(03) VALUE 'ENC'.
       01  WS-KEY                         PIC  X(16) VALUE
                                       '1234567890123456'.
       01  WS-INDATA                      PIC  X(32) VALUE
                                       'ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOP'.
       01  WS-INLEN                       PIC S9(04) COMP VALUE 16.
       01  WS-OUTDATA                     PIC  X(32) VALUE SPACE.
       PROCEDURE                       DIVISION.
       0000-START.
           DISPLAY 'BEFORE CALL***************'
           DISPLAY 'WS-RC        =' WS-RC '***'
           DISPLAY 'WS-TYPE      =' WS-TYPE '***'
           DISPLAY 'WS-KEY       =' WS-KEY '***'
           DISPLAY 'WS-INDATA    =' WS-INDATA '***'
           DISPLAY 'WS-INLEN     =' WS-INLEN '***'
           DISPLAY 'WS-OUTDATA   =' WS-OUTDATA '***'

           MOVE    'DEC'               TO WS-TYPE
           CALL 'BCOENDEC'             USING
                 WS-RC WS-TYPE WS-KEY WS-OUTDATA WS-INLEN WS-INDATA
           DISPLAY 'AFTER  DEC ***************'
           DISPLAY 'WS-RC        =' WS-RC '***'
           DISPLAY 'WS-TYPE      =' WS-TYPE '***'
           DISPLAY 'WS-KEY       =' WS-KEY '***'
           DISPLAY 'WS-INDATA    =' WS-OUTDATA '***'
           DISPLAY 'WS-INLEN     =' WS-INLEN '***'
           DISPLAY 'WS-OUTDATA   =' WS-INDATA '***'
           GOBACK.

/*
//LKED.SYSLIB   DD DSN=CEE.SCEELKED,DISP=SHR                            00240000
//              DD DSN=S02526.NONEXEC,DISP=SHR                          00240000
//LKED.SYSIN DD *                                                       00240000
  NAME GO(R)                                                            00240000
/*
//S1       EXEC PGM=*.COB.LKED.SYSLMOD,REGION=4M                        00240000
//SYSOUT   DD  SYSOUT=*                                                 00250000
/*


follwing is c code:

#pragma  linkage(bcoendec,COBOL)
#include <stdio.h>

int k_encrypt(char *out, char *in, int in_len, char *key)
{
...
}

int k_decrypt(char *out, char *in, int in_len, char *key)
{
...
}

void bcoendec(short *RC, char *TYPE, char *KEY, char *INDATA, short *INLEN, char *OUTDATA)
{
   *RC = -1;
   if (strcmp(TYPE, "ENC") == 0) {
      *RC = k_encrypt(OUTDATA, INDATA, *INLEN, KEY);
   }
   else if (strcmp(TYPE, "DEC") == 0) {
     *RC = k_decrypt(OUTDATA, INDATA, *INLEN, KEY);
   }
}

sometime it work, the other not work.
i can't find the difference when it work and not work.
i use same input always for test.

when it work, *RC value(it was displayed by COBOL side) is large than zero.
when it not work, *RC has garbage value(before c call it init by zero).
when debuging mode it same.

there are no error messages which was catch by CICS.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [Q]Difference of Online and Batch in CICS?

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 2001-01-09T08:33:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<93eide$8ft$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<3A5545CD.C0394BD5@initech.com> <934s5s$ncq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A5674C8.588A693E@initech.com>`

```
OK, let's try...

1. The linkage between COBOL and C is OK. Depending on the Compiler
level of both compilers, you could even omit the #pragma linkage in the
C Code.

2. There is no error in the calling COBOL program. Since you do nothing
else than call the C-module, you cannot have a mistake here.

3. Ergo: there must be an error in the C-module, I'd guess a storage
overlay.

It is not so seldom that a program containing some errors runs OK in one
environment (batch), but not in another (CICS). You can even see a
problem in a program that 'runs OK since years and has never been
changed' if you have e change in the environment (COBOL runtime to LE or
even another level of LE runtime). The error is mostly in the user
program and the real error was that it did run for some time instead of
abending.

This kind of error is very hard to find. You could try:

- have a code inspection of your program by an experienced programmer
- debug your code using a tool like Xpediter or Debug Tool
- comment part of your code until the cropped part runs OK, then
uncomment half of the incative code until you find the error
- call for IBM support which may cost some $, but might be cheaper than
search many day without finding the error

Good look!

In article <3A5674C8.588A693E@initech.com>,
  ChungKil Kim <chkim@initech.com> wrote:
> following is COBOL side code i use:
>
…[46 more quoted lines elided]…
> //LKED.SYSLIB   DD DSN=CEE.SCEELKED,DISP=SHR
00240000
> //              DD DSN=S02526.NONEXEC,DISP=SHR
00240000
> //LKED.SYSIN DD *
00240000
>   NAME GO(R)
00240000
> /*
> //S1       EXEC PGM=*.COB.LKED.SYSLMOD,REGION=4M
00240000
> //SYSOUT   DD  SYSOUT=*
00250000
> /*
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> void bcoendec(short *RC, char *TYPE, char *KEY, char *INDATA, short
*INLEN, char *OUTDATA)
> {
>    *RC = -1;
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [Q]Difference of Online and Batch in CICS?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 2001-01-10T11:29:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<93hh29$gis$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<3A5545CD.C0394BD5@initech.com> <934s5s$ncq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A5674C8.588A693E@initech.com> <93eide$8ft$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A5AD2E6.570F8C1D@initech.com>`

```
In article <3A5AD2E6.570F8C1D@initech.com>,
  ChungKil Kim <chkim@initech.com> wrote:

>
> how differ C from COBOL(in CICS)?
>

There is a part of the COBOL runtime (CEE.SCEECICS) which is different
in CICS than in batch. C is C in both environments.

>
> thanks for your comments. :-)
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> thanks.

The main difference between batch and CICS (from a technical view) is
that under CICS, programs are used reentrant. If you have static storage
in your C-Module, you have to set the compiler-option 'RENT' both in the
C and the COBOL compile. If it is not the current C/C++ for OS/390
compiler, but rather a C/370 compiler, you should omit static storage.

You could also try a dynamic call from COBOL to C:


WORKING-STORAGE                SECTION.
01 BCOENDEC PIC X(8) VALUE 'BCOENDEC'.
....

PROCEDURE                      DIVISION.
....
CALL BCOENDEC            USING
                 WS-RC WS-TYPE WS-KEY WS-OUTDATA WS-INLEN WS-INDATA

If you do it like that, you have to define BCOENDEC to CICS (Language
Le370). So you give CICS the possibility to initiate the C-program. In
you shop, there might be a restriction which forbids dynamic calls in
CICS. If so, tell the CICS-peaople that this restriction is outdated and
that IBM recommends dynamic calls rather than EXEC CICS LINK.

There might be other problems if CICS is not configured correctly for
using Language Environment like mixing of old and new runtime in STEPLIB
and DFHRPL. You could also try to define the COBOL programs as Language
Le370 instead of COBOL.

Really hard to find the bug from here... All I can say is: It should
work ;-(
```

#### ↳ Re: [Q]Difference of Online and Batch in CICS?

>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>> Ursprüngliche Nachricht <<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<

Am 05.01.01, 03:55:57, schrieb ChungKil Kim <chkim@initech.com> zum 
Thema [Q]Difference of Online and Batch in CICS?:


> i use COBOL to C call.
> in batch it run ok.
> but in online it does not properly run.
> sometime run ok, and the other time not.


You need the LE370 Runtime - actual LE1.8 or 2
batch and cics or ims

Inside the C-Program one statement must be:

	#pragma linkage(....COBOL) ... i think

load-module must include something like C2CO as main entry point, this 
will be done automatic by prelinker and linkage-editor


You can call all hll-programs from any other hll-language include 
hll-assembler

see: IBM OS390 Language Environment for MVS VM

	inter language communication


all this Info - be carefull. This info is only by mind not by paper
	cobol call c
	c call cobol

see you

- **Date:** 2001-01-07T01:30:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CEP56.4223$m01.347269@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<F1O56.59$g87.132630@nnrp2.sbc.net>`

```
The DISPLAY verb goes to SYSOUT , so....

SYSOUT DD DSN=MY.DSN,DISP=....


Of course, ALL SYSOUT output goes with it, but that's what you have in the
output queue now anyway.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: can we display to a file?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-06T19:40:11-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<938h9f$416$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<F1O56.59$g87.132630@nnrp2.sbc.net> <CEP56.4223$m01.347269@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
*actually* you can (but don't have to) change where DISPLAY output goes.
This is done by

A) Using the "UPON" phrase in the DISPLAY statement
   and/or
B) Associating another name with "SYSOUT" in the Special-Names paragraph.
   and/or
C) using the OUTDD compiler option.

Having said that, Michael's answer (below) will certainly work
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: can we display to a file?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-07T13:53:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vx_56.6066$m01.647684@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<F1O56.59$g87.132630@nnrp2.sbc.net> <CEP56.4223$m01.347269@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <938h9f$416$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill, I had not thought of SPECIAL-NAMES / UPON or OUTDD. This could be a
really useful tool. I will be checking the current manuals next week and
playing around.

Sheesh, it would be handy to 'direct' DISPLAY output on a selective basis.

Thanks for the lead/tip/hint...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: can we display to a file?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-08T07:05:13-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A59C918.DBB543F3@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<F1O56.59$g87.132630@nnrp2.sbc.net> <CEP56.4223$m01.347269@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <938h9f$416$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <vx_56.6066$m01.647684@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
The normal CoBOL DISPLAY in my shop goes to SYSOUX, not SYSOUT.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: can we display to a file?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-08T09:44:00-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<93cn3o$5nv$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<F1O56.59$g87.132630@nnrp2.sbc.net> <CEP56.4223$m01.347269@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <938h9f$416$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <vx_56.6066$m01.647684@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <3A59C918.DBB543F3@brazee.net>`

```
When/if you see "SYSOUX" in an IBM Mainframe shop, this is (usually) a
"historical" left-over from OS/VS COBOL days - when you could ONLY change
the last character of "SYSOUT".  It wouldn't surprise me if Howard's shop
has set the *default* (possibly not even modifiable by application
programmers) for the OUTDD compiler option to SYSOUX.  If you/he looks at a
compiler listing (assuming he is using VS COBOL II or later), my guess is
that this is what OUTDD will show.

*EVEN* when you use the OUTDD statement, it is still possible to over-rider
where DISPLAY output goes - by using the "UPON" phrase of the DISPLAY
statement.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: can we display to a file?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-08T11:44:07-07:00

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:

> When/if you see "SYSOUX" in an IBM Mainframe shop, this is (usually) a
> "historical" left-over from OS/VS COBOL days - when you could ONLY change
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> statement.

I just checked, it is a compile option which will automatically be in place in
production.  I believe that most of my IBM shops have used SYSOUX.
```

<a id="t-70695a29d8"></a>
## Thread: Numeric field definition — 3 msgs

### Numeric field definition

- **From:** Joe <joejones9270@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-08T03:06:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<93baqu$kdv$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I've been given the job of converting an EBCIDIC database to ASCII.
The records include numeric fields. There are several types of numeric
fields. I'm not sure what the following definitions mean:

S9
S9(9)V9(2)
S9(5)V9(6)

Any help will be appreciated!

Thanks,

Joe


Sent via Deja.com
http://www.deja.com/
```

#### ↳ Re: Numeric field definition

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-07T22:14:06-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<93beqm$1m1c$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<93baqu$kdv$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
S9:                  Signed numeric, 1 digit (+1 = C1)
S9(9)V9(2):    Signed numeric, 11 digits, 2 decimal places (-123456789.12 =
F1F2F3F4F5F6F7F8F9F1D2)
S9(5)V9(6):    Signed numeric, 11 digits, 6 decimal places (+12345.123456 =
F1F2F3F4F5F1F2F3F4F5C6)
The decimal places are implied, not explicit in the above.  The COBOL
manuals at www.mvshelp.com can give you more information about data types.
If the above were packed decimal (COMP-3) fields, the storage
representations would be very different than the above.
```

#### ↳ Re: Numeric field definition

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-08T14:03:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uMj66.326$PT.74585@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<93baqu$kdv$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
There is a freeware text and graphics tutorial on COBOL datatypes at
http://www.flexus.com/softwaredownload.html.
Get file COBDATA.ZIP.

For information on converting COBOL datatypes to IEEE as well as EBCDIC to
ASCII conversions, read the article at http://www.flexus.com/ebd2asc.html.

For a Windows (32-bit) DLL to convert COBOL datatypes to IEEE datatypes AND
convert EBCDIC to ASCII, write to me and explain your development
environment a little (i.e., platform, tools).
```

<a id="t-da11d575aa"></a>
## Thread: PC Based COBOL vs Mainframe COBOL Employment — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### PC Based COBOL vs Mainframe COBOL Employment

- **From:** merhottin@aol.com (Merhottin)
- **Date:** 2001-01-08T04:02:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010107230209.09910.00000411@ng-fr1.aol.com>`

```
Hi all,

      I got a job out of school writing COBOL in Realia, and we are suppose to
be switching to Fujitsu soon.
     I have been there about 6 months, and love my job, and love writing COBOL,
here is my question:  I see alot of jobs for mainframe COBOL, JCL, DB2, CICS (
all of which I learnt in school) but havent used those skills in about a year
plus.  I know I need the experince writing COBOL, but in 2 years, will it hurt
me not using those other skills?
    I have thought about using this time to learn COBOL, and going back and
taking classed in HTML, JAVA, etc.  
     Just curious if I will still be 'inexperinced' in two years.  Should I
start looking for a mainframe cobol job?  
     What are your thoughts about Fujitsu COBOL? I know that can go directly to
the web, and we can write 'windows' based programs in it.  I love programing,
just dont want to miss the ship.  Thanks in advance for your thoughts, and
comments.

John
```

#### ↳ Re: PC Based COBOL vs Mainframe COBOL Employment

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2001-01-08T05:44:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010108004410.11561.00000351@ng-cf1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<20010107230209.09910.00000411@ng-fr1.aol.com>`

```
You will be in a sort of limbo John.  While you can claim experience in COBOL
your practical experience in all the other skills is nill and that can be a
show stopper. On the other hand if you've worked with any kind of SQL then you
can relate that to DB2 and maybe can talk your way into that.

A lot of shops want people to have at least a year in all the skills listed. 
You can get away with having just CICS and no DB2 or vice versa but neither
will hurt.

You may be able to find an entry level job that will train you in CICS, DB2 and
JCL but don't expect to go into a mainframe shop any higher than that with out
practical experience.  Also don't expect to do much but grunt maintenance for a
while as you aquire the skills you're lacking.

Eileen
```

<a id="t-bdcb29aa10"></a>
## Thread: Cobol Software Options For Aix — 4 msgs

### Cobol Software Options For Aix

- **From:** "Alistair Nelson" <aldoofbanga@yahoo.com.au>
- **Date:** 2001-01-08T16:14:36+11:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.unix.aix
- **Message-ID:** `<93bibt$82a$1@perki.connect.com.au>`

```
Hi,

Does anyone know what cobol  compiler's are available for AIX? I aren't a
cobol coder, I just have an urgent need to compile some code.

Is there any "free" versions? I couldn't find a GNU version. Or is there any
versions that give you
a free run-time version for distribution to customers. Apparently the IBM
AIX compiler doesn't facilitate this.

Cheers

Alistair
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol Software Options For Aix

- **From:** Syed Faisal Akber <faisal@SERVER1.AKBER.NET>
- **Date:** 2001-01-08T18:58:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.unix.aix
- **Message-ID:** `<bnlc39.le.ln@SERVER0.AKBER.NET>`
- **References:** `<93bibt$82a$1@perki.connect.com.au>`

```
Alistair Nelson <aldoofbanga@yahoo.com.au> wrote:
> Hi,

> Does anyone know what cobol  compiler's are available for AIX? I aren't a
> cobol coder, I just have an urgent need to compile some code.

> Is there any "free" versions? I couldn't find a GNU version. Or is there any
> versions that give you
> a free run-time version for distribution to customers. Apparently the IBM
> AIX compiler doesn't facilitate this.

> Cheers

> Alistair

Alistair,

There are two major COBOL compilers available on AIX.  The first is,
as you mentioned, IBM COBOL for AIX.  The other option you have is the 
Merant MicroFocus COBOL Workbench.  MF COBOL does allow you to distribute the
run-time with your application freely.  Just double check the licence before 
you do so.  

IBM claims that IBM COBOL is faster than MF COBOL on AIX.  I cannot confirm 
this, but I do know that MF COBOL is faster than IBM VA COBOL on NT, and 
other PC platforms.  


Hope this helps,
Faisal Akber
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Software Options For Aix

- **From:** "Alistair Nelson" <aldoofbanga@yahoo.com.au>
- **Date:** 2001-01-10T15:09:38+11:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.unix.aix
- **Message-ID:** `<93gna7$lfm$1@perki.connect.com.au>`
- **References:** `<93bibt$82a$1@perki.connect.com.au> <bnlc39.le.ln@SERVER0.AKBER.NET>`

```
Thanks a million for the info!
```

- **Date:** 2001-01-10T22:05:24-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.unix.aix
- **Message-ID:** `<3a5d3176$0$14651@wodc7nh0.news.uu.net>`
- **References:** `<93bibt$82a$1@perki.connect.com.au> <bnlc39.le.ln@SERVER0.AKBER.NET>`

On Mon, 8 Jan 2001 15:43:08 -0000, "Paulo" <paulo@NOSPAM100limite.com>
wrote:

>Hi !
>
>I'm having problems with the keys in linux, using conf.r85, specialy with
>'home', 'end' keys, does anyone has a conf.r85 file working perfectly ?

Hi.


In order for us to be able to answer this question we need to have
more information such as

What is the emulation you are using.
If using a terminal emulator which one is it.
What is the exact problem you have with the keys.
What is the software you are using. (if you have RM/PANELS the keys
may work in a certain way, if you have other product then they may
work diferently.
What is the content of your configuration file.
What is the content of your termcap/terminfo entry for the specific
emulation you are using.


Best regards

Frederico Fonseca
Memosis - Liant Products Technical Support in Portugal
```

## Thread: Object Model problems - NetExpress — 2 msgs

### Object Model problems - NetExpress

- **From:** "Neil Hayes" <NeilH@syspro.co.za>
- **Date:** 2001-01-09T07:04:21+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3a5a9b80$0$234@helios.is.co.za>`

```
I think I'm just looking for 1 or 2 lines of code to answer this frustrating
problem which I don't seem to find any ole examples anywhere.

I originally attached a zip file containing the project, but I was unable to
post it. So parts of the code appear at the end.

Object.

To create one dll that contains multiple classes which contain methods and
properties

In my example vb project I want to be able to define one root class and
access the rest.or the classes in the manor below.

    Dim myobj as TestClass1.TestClass1    'Define the base class
    set myobj = CreateObject("TestClass1")    'Create the object

   myobj.Connect                                            'Do a method in
TestClass1 or set properties

  myobj.TestClass2.Colour  = "Red"              ' Set the colour in
TestClass2 to Red


Problem.

I animated this code to see what happens, the connect function was called OK
and any code would have run, but now what about the colour property ?

What do I need to do to get  myobj.TestClass2.Colour to run ?

What code should be in this property or is my structure incorrect or perhaps
my VB ?


TestClass2 could have multiple properties and methods so it would not be
correct to

    invoke TestClass2 "SetColour'' using xyz

instead it must return some sort of pointer but what and where does it go ?

Notes.

I've modified the TestClass1.if_idl file the original line commented out
The testclass1.idl file also has been modified to include

         importlib("stdole32.tlb");
        interface TestClass2;                     - This line was added.

This is the project code (NetExpress) if you want the zipped version email
me and I'll send with pleasure.


TestClass1.idl
*****************************************************
// typelib filename: testclass1.tlb

[
  // OCWIZARD - start library attributes
  uuid(E381676D-A25C-4188-89EB-10690EBC9B2B),
  version(1.0),
  helpstring("Library description for TestClass1")
  // OCWIZARD - end library attributes
]
library testclass1
{
    // TLib : OLE Automation : {00020430-0000-0000-C000-000000000046}
    importlib("stdole32.tlb");
    interface TestClass2;
    // OCWIZARD - start #include lines
    #include "TestClass1_if.idl"
    #include "TestClass2_if.idl"
    // OCWIZARD - end #include lines

TestClass1_if.idl
*****************************************************
    #include "TestClass1_if.h"
    [
    // OCWIZARD - start interface attributes
      dual,
      uuid(9FA6DACE-7C63-49AB-8265-F690D82E162D),
      helpstring("Interface description for TestClass1")
