```
      *> OLE server trigger (for in-process servers only)
      *> WARNING: Do not add further entry points to this file
      $set case
       linkage section.
       01 loadReason    pic x(4) comp-5.
       procedure division.
       entry "DllOleLoadClasses" using by value loadReason.
      *> OCWIZARD - start classes
      *> Calling the class registers it as available to OLE clients
           call "testclass1"
           call "testclass2"
      *> OCWIZARD - end classes
           exit program.

TestClass1.cbl
*****************************************************
      $set ooctrl(+p)

      *>-----------------------------------------------------------
      *> Class description
      *>-----------------------------------------------------------
       class-id. TestClass1
                 inherits from olebase.

       object section.
       class-control.
           TestClass1 is class "testclass1"
      *> OCWIZARD - start list of classes
           olebase is class "olebase"
           oleSafeArray is class "olesafea"
           oleVariant is class "olevar"
      *> OCWIZARD - end list of classes
      *>---USER-CODE. Add any additional class names below.
           TestClass2 is class "testclass2"

           .

      *>-----------------------------------------------------------
       working-storage section. *> Definition of global data
      *>-----------------------------------------------------------
      *01 anInterface           object reference.

      *>-----------------------------------------------------------
       class-object.   *> Definition of class data and methods
      *>-----------------------------------------------------------
       object-storage section.

      *> OCWIZARD - start standard class methods
      *>-----------------------------------------------------------
      *> Return details about the class.
      *> If you have a type library, theClassId and theInterfaceId
      *> here MUST match.
      *> theProgId must match the registry entry for this class
      *>   (a zero length string implies using the class file name)
      *> theClassId must match the CLSID stored in the registry.
      *> theVersion is currently ignored (default 1 used).
      *>-----------------------------------------------------------
       method-id. queryClassInfo.
       linkage section.
       01 theProgId             pic x(256).
       01 theClassId            pic x(39).
       01 theInterfceId         pic x(39).
       01 theVersion            pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 theDescription        pic x(256).
       01 theThreadModel        pic x(20).
       procedure division using by reference theProgId
                                by reference theClassId
                                by reference theInterfceId
                                by reference theVersion
                                by reference theDescription
                                by reference theThreadModel.
        call "cbl_debugbreak"
         move z"{DB257273-F0BE-4849-9DB5-55BAABEF647D}" to theClassId
         move z"{9FA6DACE-7C63-49AB-8265-F690D82E162D}" to theInterfceId
         move z"Description for class TestClass1"
              to theDescription
         exit method.
       end method queryClassInfo.

      *>-----------------------------------------------------------
      *> Return details about the type library - delete if unused.
      *> theLocale is currently ignored (default 0 used).
      *> theLibraryName is a null terminated string used for auto
      *> registration, and supports the following values:
      *>     <no string> - Library is embedded in this binary
      *>     <number>    - As above, with this resource number
      *>     path        - Library is at this (full path) location
      *>-----------------------------------------------------------
       method-id. queryLibraryInfo.
       linkage section.
       01 theLibraryName        pic x(512).
       01 theMajorVersion       pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 theMinorVersion       pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 theLibraryId          pic x(39).
       01 theLocale             pic x(4) comp-5.
       procedure division using by reference theLibraryName
                                by reference theMajorVersion
                                by reference theMinorVersion
                                by reference theLibraryId
                                by reference theLocale.
         move 1 to theMajorVersion
         move 0 to theMinorVersion
         move z"{E381676D-A25C-4188-89EB-10690EBC9B2B}" to theLibraryId
         exit method.
       end method queryLibraryInfo.

       procedure division returning ReturnCode.


       exit method.
       end method "Connect".
      *>---------------------------------------------------------------


      *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       method-id. "getTestClass2".
       local-storage Section.
      *>---USER-CODE. Add any local storage items needed below.
       linkage Section.
       01 anInterface       Object Reference.
       procedure division returning anInterface.

       end object.

Visual Basic Project.

Create a new VB project, add a reference to the TestClass1.dll, add a
command button. Add this code to the command button.

Private Sub Command1_Click()

'I modified the type library IDL files to achive this for intellisense
'The TestClass2 get property in TestClass1 returns the interface of
TestClass2

Dim myobj As testclass1.testclass1

Set myobj = CreateObject("testclass1")

'Do a connection
myobj.Connect

'set the colour code to Red
myobj.TestClass2.ColourCode = "Red"

'Finalize the object
Set myobj = Nothing


End Sub
```

#### ↳ Re: Object Model problems - NetExpress

- **From:** "David Sands" <david.sands@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-17T10:15:57
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<943vvu$rt7$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<3a5a9b80$0$234@helios.is.co.za>`

```
Hi Neil,

I think you will need an variable such as

01    mytestclass2    object reference.

in object stroage of testclass1.

In class section if testclass1

>            TestClass2 is class "testclass2"

should be

>            TestClass2 is class "$OLE$testclass2"

and then in Gettestclass2 of testclass you could have something such as:-

    if mytestclass2 = null
        invoke testclass2 "new" returning mytestclass2
    end-if
    move mytestclass2 to aninterface

I think that should work OK.

Regards
David
```

#### ↳ Re: problem with opening mutiple files

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-09T08:37:40-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<93f7na$ooo$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<93ep8a$2u0$1@news1.skynet.be>`

```
I am taking a WILD guess on this - because "basically" the code looks
reasonable to me (up to the first OPEN syntax error - once you get that,
everything else is "bound" to fail).

Your two Select statements start

 SELECT inschryv ASSIGN TO disc, "inschryv"
    and
 SELECT tweede ASSIGN TO disc, "tweede"

is "disc" a documented word to use (with this compiler) at this place in the
SELECT statement? If not, I think that it is TRYING to assign both files to
the SAME external medium - and this means that it couldn't be opened in both
Sequential and Indexed organization at the same time.

Depending on that (old) RM compiler, I would consider either taking out
"disc" or POSSIBLY changing it to "disk" - check your documentation to see
which the compiler is expecting.
```

```
   46    000002           HOOFD.
   47

********************PB-START

                             OPEN INPUT INSCHRYV.
REM   *          IF NOT-EXIST THEN PERFORM END-PROGRAM.

********************PB-END

   48    000005              OPEN I-O inschryv.
RM/COBOL-85 (VER 2.01)  FOR DOS 2.00+          01/09/01  11:20:19 PAGE   2
SOURCE FILE: EERSTE.CBL                 OPTIONS: L
LINE   DEBUG     PG/LN
A...B.......2.........3.........4.........5.........6.........7..ID.....8
EXAMEN-1

********************PB-START

                             OPEN I-O TWEEDE.
REM   *          IF NOT-EXIST THEN OPEN OUTPUT TWEEDE
REM   *                                                CLOSE
TWEEDE
REM   *                                                 OPEN     I-O
TWEEDE.

********************PB-END

   49    000012              Open output tweede.
                                         $     $

********************PB-START

       START-LEZEN.
            READ INSCHRYF NEXT
                        AT END MOVE  1 TO SW
                                       GO TO EINDE-PROGRAMMA.
            PERFORM WEGSCHRIJVEN-TWEEDE.
            GO TO START-LEZEN.
       EINDE-PROGRAMMA.
            CLOSE INSCHRYV   TWEEDE.
       END-RUN.
REM   *    perform wegschrijven until eof. KAN NIET !!!

********************PB-END

   50    000015              Read inschryv at end move 1 to sw.
   51    000030              perform wegschrijven until eof.
   52    000042              close inschryv
   53    000049              close tweede
                                   $
   54    000052              STOP RUN.
                             $
   55    000055          Wegschrijven.
   56
   57    000058              move naam to naam-s
   58    000065              move geb-datum to geboortedatum
   59    000072              move klas to klas-s
   60    000079              write tweede invalid key perform fout.
                                   $                  $
   61    000087          fout.
   62    000090              display "Er is iets mis"
   63    000096              accept x.
Does someone knows the problem?

Does dit help you een beetje verder?
mvg.
Piet van 'Het Compulaat'
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: problem with opening mutiple files

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-10T11:03:58-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<93i4ju$u04$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<93ep8a$2u0$1@news1.skynet.be> <3a5c6090$0$30008$4156c999@news.netdirect.net>`

```
The person who asked the original question received a private email reply -
but just so people know the "actual" problem was that they had alternate
keys starting at the same position as the primary key  - and this is a
"no-no" in Standard COBOL.

FYI,
  An "FD" may begin ANYWHERE in the A-Margin (if your compiler still
requires A-/B-margin checking).  They do NOT need to start in the same
place.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: problem with opening mutiple files

- **From:** dxmixxer@netdirect.net (Douglas Miller)
- **Date:** 2001-01-12T22:13:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3a5f839a$0$1499$7ea90a65@news.netdirect.net>`
- **References:** `<93ep8a$2u0$1@news1.skynet.be> <3a5c6090$0$30008$4156c999@news.netdirect.net> <93i4ju$u04$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
In article <93i4ju$u04$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:
>The person who asked the original question received a private email reply -
>but just so people know the "actual" problem was that they had alternate
>keys starting at the same position as the primary key  - and this is a
>"no-no" in Standard COBOL.
>
And how would this affect the compiler's ability to recognize the name of the 
FD as a valid identifier? No, I think you're on the wrong track here.

>FYI,
>  An "FD" may begin ANYWHERE in the A-Margin (if your compiler still
>requires A-/B-margin checking).  They do NOT need to start in the same
>place.
>
You missed my point entirely -- which was precisely that in this program the 
keyword FD does *not* begin in Area A. Even in compilers that don't enforce 
A/B margin checking, there is still a continuation column, is there not? And I 
think the original poster has his FD beginning in the continuation column.

I tried compiling the program myself. My compiler (Tandem Cobol85) never says 
a thing about a conflict between the primary and alternate key offsets, but it 
complains like hell when the FD begins in the continuation column.

Obviously his record definition is faulty. But I still think the biggest part 
of his problem is that the FD begins one column left of where it should.

I'd be interested in seeing a followup from the original poster.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: problem with opening mutiple files

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-12T18:09:01-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<93o6ao$k81$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<93ep8a$2u0$1@news1.skynet.be> <3a5c6090$0$30008$4156c999@news.netdirect.net> <93i4ju$u04$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <3a5f839a$0$1499$7ea90a65@news.netdirect.net>`

```
The reply that I reported came from the vendor of the compiler in question.
You are correct that if the FD began in column 7 - that could WELL cause a
serious problem.  However, that was NOT how the code actually "appeared" (in
the text that was passed back and forth via private email).

There are a VARIETY of compilers that do different things when an FD, a
Select/Assign, or an I/O statement "has a problem".  There is NOTHING in the
Standard that makes the type of compiler error that you receive "portable".

Once the Select/Assign clause pointed to "incorrect" Alternate Keys "all
bets were off" for the rest of the source code.

Personally, I believe that the message that was issued was NOT very
user-friendly - but it certainly was a "legitimate" recognition that
SOMETHING was wrong.
```

## Thread: JCL Return Code (RC) — 10 this month / 12 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### JCL Return Code (RC)

- **From:** "Curious" <leng1@bigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-10T22:49:01+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<93hsgv$d6d$1@clematis.singnet.com.sg>`

```
Hi,

May I know

(1) What is RC in the JCL ?
(2) And the reason as to why do we need to put RC code in the JCL ?
     Is there any implication of this code.

Tks
```

#### ↳ Re: JCL Return Code (RC)

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2001-01-10T17:00:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010110120043.10182.00000722@ng-ca1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<93hsgv$d6d$1@clematis.singnet.com.sg>`

```
Curious writes ...

>Hi,
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>     Is there any implication of this code.

1) Every batch program run under MVS, OS/390, or z/OS has the ability to return
an integer, called the Return Code or Condition Code to the JCL stream; the
value must be in the range 0-4095 decimal; the convention is (although you are
not obligated to follow it) that a return code of zero says the program ran
successfully and that non-zero return codes indicate some kind of error, with
larger values implying more sever errors

2) The JCL for a step in a job can test the return code value from any previous
step in the same job, to determine if the current step should run or not; if
you don't do return code checking, the current step runs, by default, unless a
prior step abends. So you might have steps that you only want to run under
certain conditions.

A common example is a job to do a compile and link. All the compilers (and the
Assembler) put out return codes of  0, 4, 8, 12, or 16 (and maybe some do 20),
where '4' generally implies warning level compile errors. So you could set up
the link step to test the return code from the compile step, and if the RC is
greater than 4, do not run the link step because there were serious compile
errors. You can do the same thing for your own application batch steps.

The way to set RC in a program varies depending on the language you are coding
in.

There are two ways to test RC values in JCL. 1) use the COND parameter (not a
good idea any more); 2) use the IF/THEN/ELSE/ENDIF JCL statements (the
recommended way).

<ad>
All this and more is covered in our two JCL classes. For information on these
classes check out our website at http://www.trainersfriend.com

Follow the links to Topics, then JCL. From there you can get to course
descriptions, objectives, and outlines for these courses.
</ad>

Hope this helps,



Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: JCL Return Code (RC)

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-10T10:35:20-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A5C9D58.25609E22@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<93hsgv$d6d$1@clematis.singnet.com.sg> <20010110120043.10182.00000722@ng-ca1.aol.com>`

```


S Comstock wrote:

> Curious writes ...
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> larger values implying more sever errors

I have sometimes passed values which determine which following steps I want to skip
or run.  These don't have to be errors - they might indicate that we need to do an
FTP step (or who gets the FTP), or an update step, or whether the program produced
a file etc.

To do this though, one should know what condition codes are interpreted as errors.
A standard set of values for your shop which can be recognized as program control
codes is recommended.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: JCL Return Code (RC)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-10T14:26:33-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<93ighh$89h$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<93hsgv$d6d$1@clematis.singnet.com.sg> <20010110120043.10182.00000722@ng-ca1.aol.com>`

```
Just one other "warning" about RC (Return-Code) and COBOL.

As has been discussed in this NG before, there are some products (such as
DB2 - and even infrequently CICS) that are not "nice" about clearing R15 all
the time.  What this means is that if you have a COBOL program that CALLs
(uses) these facilities (and they don't all have to be in-house or IBM
products/applications) and then your COBOL program "exits", the SYSTEM will
use whatever is "left" in R15 (register 15) as the Return-Code that is
"reported" back to the system.

This *may* cause SEVER problems for subsequent programs in your job stream -
and even if this is a "stand-alone" program, will be QUITE misleading.

When/If this happens, then you should add a "Move Zero to Return-Code"
statement in your application program just before doing a Stop Run or final
GoBack.  I do *not* recommend doing this "in general" - but only as a
"solution" for applications with "misbehaving" subprograms. (On the other
hand, I can't think of any "problem" in adding it in programs that don't
need it - when you are POSITIVE that "all is ending well")
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: JCL Return Code (RC)

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-11T01:21:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uU776.3231$eT6.288701@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<93hsgv$d6d$1@clematis.singnet.com.sg> <20010110120043.10182.00000722@ng-ca1.aol.com> <93ighh$89h$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill, what I do is carry my own WS-RETURN-CODE in my programs, MOVEing it to
RETURN-CODE immediately prior to exiting.

If I call a service and want to use its RC as this program's RC, I store it
in WS-RETURN-CODE myself. (usually only if its higher than the current
value).

Gets around the problem nicely.

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: JCL Return Code (RC)

- **From:** Support@ScreenIO.com (Kevin J. Hansen)
- **Date:** 2001-01-10T17:05:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3a5c94d6.53193107@news>`
- **References:** `<93hsgv$d6d$1@clematis.singnet.com.sg>`

```
On Wed, 10 Jan 2001 22:49:01 +0800, "Curious" <leng1@bigfoot.com>
wrote:

>May I know
>
>(1) What is RC in the JCL ?
>(2) And the reason as to why do we need to put RC code in the JCL ?
>     Is there any implication of this code.
============
If you have a batch job that consists of many programs, the return
code is very useful, because it allows you to run or suppress
subsequent job steps (programs) depending on whether a program ran
successfully.

In the IBM mainframe world, you can set the return code within your
program, and then write JCL that evaluates that return code.  You can
then take different actions for different return codes.

In addition, the system (IIRC) will set a unique recturn code if your
job terminates abnormally.  Normally, this causes the system to flush
the balance of your job.  (It abended, right?)

HOWEVER, in our mainframe batch job days, my partner and I discovered
that you could code your JCL so that it would run a program (job step)
even if a program abended.

What was that good for?  Suppose you have a job that consists of 60 or
80 programs that takes hours to complete.  Suppose, also, that there
are points along the way where the files are updated in such a way
that they are valid.

Now:  Suppose that, at those points, you take a quick backup of your
disk files before continuing with your job.  Then, if a program
following your backup terminates abnormally (leaving the files in an
inconsistent state of being updated), you can code your JCL to restore
them from the latest tape backup so that the online portion of the
system will come up properly the next day.

--> This also means that you don't need to get up and go to work at
2AM to restore the files yourself. <--

No one at the State of Alaska data center knew you could even do this,
and the first couple of times it happened, we STILL got calls from the
operators wondering why the job that just bombed was requesting a
tape.  "Just load the tape and let it do its restore," we told 'em,
"and don't call me next time!"

Worked like a champ.  We'd fix the bug the next morning, knowing that
the online system would be up and running the next day in spite of the
abend.

Amazing what you can learn if you CAREFULLY read the JCL manual!

Kevin
Norcom - COBOL Programming Tools
GUI ScreenIO - A Windows UI for COBOL
http://www.screenio.com
```

#### ↳ Re: JCL Return Code (RC)

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-10T11:37:00-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<93i6kh$kllc$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<93hsgv$d6d$1@clematis.singnet.com.sg>`

```
(R)eturn (C)ode is one of the ways a program can communicate with JCL.  Job
steps can be executed/bypassed based on the RC of previous steps.
Communication in the opposite direction (from JCL to program) can be done
via the PARM option of the execute statement.  See the JCL manuals at the
www.mvshelp.com site for more details about RC.

To reply, remove extra "r" in e-mail address.
....Terry


----- Original Message -----

Sent: Wednesday, January 10, 2001 8:49 AM
Subject: JCL Return Code (RC)


> Hi,
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Tks
```

```
enlightened us:

>Hi,

RC is Return Code.

You may check RC from job step to job step and alter the flow of JCL
processing accordingly.
```

## Thread: ADV: Do you want to give Python a try? — 1 msgs

### ADV: Do you want to give Python a try?

- **From:** Shawn Gordon <shawn-gordon@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-10T20:26:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A5CC4FE.C1323D7F@home.com>`

```
theKompany.com is pleased to announce the release of its Windows/Linux
GUI development environment for Python, BlackAdder.  BlackAdder combines
a visual design environment with debugging, syntax highlighting, ODBC
interfaces and extensive HTML documentation into a comprehensive
platform for developing Python applications. 

theKompany.com worked with Trolltech, the creators of the Qt windowing
toolkit on which BlackAdder is based on and eGenix, creators of the
multi-platform ODBC interfaces for Python known as mxODBC, and were able
to derive a license arrangement to bring the most power and flexibility
to BlackAdder". 

What is especially exciting that BlackAdder not only runs on Linux and
Windows, it generates applications that will run on either system as
well. This protects your investment in development by enabling you to
develop and deploy your applications almost anywhere. Unlike similar
products, BlackAdder includes all the elements needed for developing
complete applications and the rights to re-distribute the necessary
run-time elements, highlights of the BlackAdder are:

- Organizes your Python scripts and GUI designs into easy to manage
projects.
- An editor that includes highlighting of Python keywords and code
folding.
- An interactive Python interpreter (v2.0) that allows you to execute
any Python commands while your application is running.
- The Qt GUI toolkit (v2.2.3) from Trolltech.
- A GUI designer with all the features of Qt Designer from Trolltech
that generates Python code.
- A debugger that supports single stepping and breakpoints.
- ODBC database connectivity (using mxODBC v2.0 from eGenix).
- Over 50Mbytes of HTML documentation.

BlackAdder is available in two editions, "Personal" aimed more at the
home hobbyist and "Business", both editions produce the same high
quality, professional applications the only restriction is the ability
to create proprietary applications for resell.  The pricing and
differences are described below, note the special discount pricing
during the initial beta period:

BlackAdder Personal Edition 
Includes: 
- Python 
- PyQt 
- Qt 
- Comprehensive HTML documentation 
- mxODBC (ODBC for Python) 
- IDE (including GUI designer, editor and debugger)
- 20% discount voucher for any book from O'Reilly 
License: For personal home use only, not allowed to re-distribute any
BlackAdder components. 
Cost: $79.99 ($49.99 if purchased during the Beta period). 
Support: Informal mailing list. 
Updates: $59.99 per year for up to 4 updates per year ($39.99 during the
Beta period)

BlackAdder Business Edition 
Includes: Same as the Personal Edition. 
License: For commercial use. Those BlackAdder components needed to
provide a run-time environment (ie. Python, PyQt, Qt) may be
re-distributed at no extra cost. One mxODBC license is included in the
purchase price, valid mxODBC licenses must be obtained for any
additional installations of generated software using the mxODBC
component.
Cost: $399.99 ($249.99 if purchased during the Beta period). 
Support: First month of support is included, extended support contracts
are available on the web site. 
Updates: $299.99 per year for up to 4 updates per year ($199.99 during
the Beta period)

Detailed information and screenshots can be seen at
http://www.thekompany.com/products/blackadder/
```

```
>From: Binyamin Dissen postingid@dissensoftware.com 
>Date: 1/11/01 8:19 AM Eastern Standard Time
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>Director, Dissen Software, Bar & Grill - Israel

EXEC CICS WRITE
     FILE (YOUR-FILE)
     FROM (THE-RECORD)
     RIDFLD (THE-KEY)
     LENGTH (THE-LENGTH)
END-EXEC
```

```
>> From: Binyamin Dissen postingid@dissensoftware.com 
>> Date: 1/11/01 8:19 AM Eastern Standard Time
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…

Assuming COBOL II & up:

  EXEC CICS WRITE
  FILE (YOUR-FILE)
  FROM (THE-RECORD)
  RIDFLD (THE-KEY)
  END-EXEC

Let the translator do the work for you,
LiamD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL/VSAM Example

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2001-01-13T15:26:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010113102618.13189.00001155@ng-ce1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3A5FCA13.3020300@optonline.net>`

```
I have to smile at the responses to the CICS post.

If a HANDLE CONDITION is not coded and no checking of the response code is done
the program will abend.  There is no need to explicitly use the NO HANDLE
unless one has been coded earlier for a particular condition.  As for the RESP
well if you want the reponse code in a WORKING-STORAGE field ok - but most
times where I'm at now they use IF EIBRESP.  Just the following the flow of the
current location ::shrug::.

LENGTH - I use several variable length VSAM files - habit - not necessary, just
habit.  Most of the programs are from way back in the days of love beads and
peace signs - and show it :).  I'm not sure how much original code is left and
how much is 'repairs'. 

Compiler is the latest and greatest COBOL for OS/390 and CICS is 4.1.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL/VSAM Example

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** infocat@sprynet.com (Frank Swarbrick)
- **Date:** 2001-01-14T05:14:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3a613233.21251379@nntp.sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<3A5FCA13.3020300@optonline.net> <20010113102618.13189.00001155@ng-ce1.aol.com>`

```
On 13 Jan 2001 15:26:18 GMT, yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama) wrote:

>I have to smile at the responses to the CICS post.
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>Compiler is the latest and greatest COBOL for OS/390 and CICS is 4.1.   

You know what I would really love?  In addition to NOHANDLE, also a
HANDLE(condition).  In other words:

EXEC CICS READ
    FILE(FILNAM)
    INTO(MY-REC)
    RIDFLD(MY-KEY)
    HANDLE(NOTFND)
END-EXEC

IF EIBRESP = DFHVALUE(NORMAL)
    SET RECORD-FOUND TO TRUE
ELSE
    SET RECORD-NOT-FOUND TO TRUE
END-IF

Why?  Because this would allow an abend for any condition other than NORMAL or
the specific conditions I've chosen to handle (NOTFND, in this case).  I realize
I could put an IGNORE CONDITION but if I don't put a HANDLE CONDITION (with no
label) after it it will continue to be ignored, which is probably a bad thing.
Plus it's ugly.

And yes, I could (and do) have some generic error checking, but I don't like it.
You can't imagine (well, maybe you can) how many programs at my shop end with
such helpful messages as "ABNORMAL TERMINATION -- CONTACT PROGRAMMING" if a CICS
file is disabled or some such thing.  I much prefer an abend with a dump using
ABEND-AID.  In programs I write I've ended up doing a divide by zero in order to
force an ASRA, but I'd much prefer the method I've shown above.

(Please no one tell me to go back to HANDLE CONDITION with labels.  Yikes!)

Frank Swarbrick
home: infocat@sprynet.com / work: frank.swarbrick@efirstbank.com
"I'm very seldom naughty." --Willow Rosenberg "Buffy the Vampire Slayer"
```

```
using
>ABEND-AID. 

Oh I can imagine.  Must be the same group that coded the copybook for the
procedure division with all the HANDLE CONDITIONS in it for all errors you can
think of and another with all the obscure messages you can think of I see in
programs I maintain.  Gives me great pleasure to rip those puppies out and put
in something sane like 'The xyz file is closed - call the Help(less)desk'.  Off
the wall abends will cause a dump so I can track it down.
```

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>

- **Message-ID:** `<93u3ug$hb4$1@news.igs.net>`

```

"YukonMama" <yukonmama@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20010114235016.19091.00000250@ng-fh1.aol.com...
> >From: infocat@sprynet.com  (Frank Swarbrick)
> >Date: 1/14/01 12:14 AM Eastern ma) wrote:
>
> >You can't imagine (well, maybe you can) >how many programs at my shop end
with
> >such helpful messages as "ABNORMAL >TERMINATION -- CONTACT >PROGRAMMING"
if a
> CICS file is >disabled or some such thing.  I much >prefer an abend with a
dump
> using
> >ABEND-AID.
>
> Oh I can imagine.  Must be the same group that coded the copybook for the
> procedure division with all the HANDLE CONDITIONS in it for all errors you
can
> think of and another with all the obscure messages you can think of I see
in
> programs I maintain.  Gives me great pleasure to rip those puppies out and
put
> in something sane like 'The xyz file is closed - call the Help(less)desk'.
Off
> the wall abends will cause a dump so I can track it down.

This is one of those places that OOP Cobol can pay off.  We are now using a
standardized OBJECT that handles all program termination. It has properties
right down to the source code paragraph name, and places them on the
operator's "console" (in our case, a windows screen). We are gradually
updating our existing source with it as we do maintenance. Once we have
everything converted, we will probably jazz it up a bit with logging to
disk, etc.  Maybe even have it send email to the appropriate account. Once
everything is nicely separated, we should be able to introduce improvements
by simply replacing a single DLL.
```

```
interrogate
the EIBRESP? I don't like using HANDLE because it can (and often does) lead

less than clear programming. <grin>

However, I use RESP a LOT, and if there is a faster alternative, well, even
a few cycles here and
there help. :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL/VSAM Example

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** infocat@sprynet.com (Frank Swarbrick)
- **Date:** 2001-01-15T19:16:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3a634b2d.4333803@nntp.sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<33cr5t8cdi8gdhcttl95bsrpoul4a9e9v8@4ax.com> <20010111233605.00557.00001193@ng-cm1.aol.com> <llbt5t46inal11jeksqelbfifct2ssfhhe@4ax.com> <p7mu5to3mv45l3ngol2j0hiiemc0kdni2o@4ax.com> <3A5FCA13.3020300@optonline.net> <3a61319d.21101552@nntp.sprynet.com> <3a61db0e$0$23014@wodc7nh7.news.uu.net>`

```
On Sun, 14 Jan 2001 10:58:09 -0600, "Paul Raulerson" <praul@isot.com> wrote:

>
>"Frank Swarbrick" <infocat@sprynet.com> wrote in message
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
>there help. :)

I'm only guessing, but I would think that EIBRESP is "faster".  It is a linkage
section field that points to the CICS area that holds the actual response field.
We don't see this, but I would guess that to get using RESP(MY-RESP) would
entail the assembler equivilant of "MOVE EIBRESP TO MY-RESP".  Therefore by
simply using NOHANDLE and interrogating EIBRESP you are saving at least one
instruction.  Not to mention the two bytes of working storage necessary to hold
MY-RESP.

Frank Swarbrick
home: infocat@sprynet.com / work: frank.swarbrick@efirstbank.com
"I'm very seldom naughty." --Willow Rosenberg "Buffy the Vampire Slayer"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL/VSAM Example

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-16T01:47:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A63A80B.DE62BA49@optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<33cr5t8cdi8gdhcttl95bsrpoul4a9e9v8@4ax.com> <20010111233605.00557.00001193@ng-cm1.aol.com> <llbt5t46inal11jeksqelbfifct2ssfhhe@4ax.com> <p7mu5to3mv45l3ngol2j0hiiemc0kdni2o@4ax.com> <3A5FCA13.3020300@optonline.net> <3a61319d.21101552@nntp.sprynet.com> <3a61db0e$0$23014@wodc7nh7.news.uu.net> <3a634b2d.4333803@nntp.sprynet.com>`

```
Frank Swarbrick wrote:

> On Sun, 14 Jan 2001 10:58:09 -0600, "Paul Raulerson" <praul@isot.com> wrote:
>

(snip)

>
> I'm only guessing, but I would think that EIBRESP is "faster".  It is a linkage
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> MY-RESP.

It's a fullword, i.e., 4 byte, field; but I'm having trouble imagining that anyone's
seriously having a problem with 2 vs. 4 bytes nowadays. If you code the RESP(...)
parameter, the translator generates exactly the move you put in quotes.

LiamD :-)
```

```
Frank Swarbrick wrote:

> On Sat, 13 Jan 2001 03:23:10 GMT, Liam Devlin <liamddd@optonline.net> wrote:
>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> the response past the next statement after the EXEC.

Which two bytes? My point was that the RESP(..) parameter makes the translator assume NOHANDLE.
I personally like having EIBRESP in a W-S field (so I can issue other commands, if I want -
sometimes necessary).

LiamD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL/VSAM Example

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** infocat@sprynet.com (Frank Swarbrick)
- **Date:** 2001-01-15T19:18:48+00:00

```
On Sun, 14 Jan 2001 19:56:59 GMT, Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.net> wrote:

>Frank Swarbrick wrote:
>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>sometimes necessary).

The two bytes of MY-RESP when using RESP(MY-RESP).  Of course, if you find it
necessary to have the response in working storage then RESP(..) is perfectly
acceptable.  I simply have never found it necessary.

And yes, I'm probably making a big deal out of nothing.  It's simply I've found
some people use RESP(..) because they don't know that it's not necessary; you
can simply use NOHANDLE.

Frank Swarbrick
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ CICS EIBRESP (was Re: COBOL/VSAM Example)

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-16T03:51:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A63C49E.64C72CA3@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<33cr5t8cdi8gdhcttl95bsrpoul4a9e9v8@4ax.com> <20010111233605.00557.00001193@ng-cm1.aol.com> <llbt5t46inal11jeksqelbfifct2ssfhhe@4ax.com> <p7mu5to3mv45l3ngol2j0hiiemc0kdni2o@4ax.com> <3A5FCA13.3020300@optonline.net> <3a61319d.21101552@nntp.sprynet.com> <3A620483.12B87313@optonline.net> <3a634c7b.4667620@nntp.sprynet.com> <3A63A8AF.1F440B6B@optonline.net>`

```
Liam Devlin wrote:
> 
> Frank Swarbrick wrote:
…[45 more quoted lines elided]…
> LiamD

In a performance-intensive CICS environment, using either NOHANDLE or
EIBRESP(my-eibresp) is more efficient than using handle conditions or
defaulting to a CICS action.  When you do an EXEC CICS command it must
search a table to locate the address of the handle condition routine,
even if you have previously set IGNORE CONDITION.  Using either NOHANDLE
or EIBRESP(my-eibresp), the table search is bypassed completely.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL/VSAM Example

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-11T12:58:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e6i76.775$793.28889@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<3A5D0852.3AC9B71D@ix.netcom.com>`

```
Andy, there's nothing magical about VSAM files, nor is special COBOL code
required.

VSAM is a file-managment system used to create files of all types in an IBM
Mainframe environment.

In your COBOL program, VSAM files are just files - you OPEN, READ, WRITE and
CLOSE just like any other file. You'll need to use VSAM (through IDCAMS) to
create files of ORGANIZATION INDEXED or ORGANIZATION RELATIVE. You can use
VSAM to create ORGANIZATION SEQUENTIAL, but a lot of shops just use the QSAM
files allocated through the DISP parameter of JCL for sequential files.

MCM
```

## Thread: Infoworld: COBOL outdated and losing support — 64 msgs

### Infoworld: COBOL outdated and losing support

- **From:** theodp@aol.com (Theo DP)
- **Date:** 2001-01-11T02:49:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010110214928.29798.00001900@ng-cs1.aol.com>`

```
--> Snipped From
http://www.infoworld.com/articles/op/xml/01/01/08/010108opborck.xml

Enterprise Strategies | James R. Borck

Cobol programmers, face it: Your favorite code is outdated and losing support
...
Since its introduction 41 years ago, Cobol has undergone three major revisions.
Although the updates in 1974 and 1985 were essentially published for the
introduction of new features, it was not until the final revision, Cobol 97,
that object-oriented constructs were appended to the ANSI standard.

So for the throngs of you who wrote to chide me for suggesting Cobol code was
not object-oriented, the 41 years of legacy Cobol code in the field did not
magically "become" object-oriented three years ago just because the
specification was updated.

Many of you took exception when I suggested Cobol is no longer taught in the
majority of our nation's institutes of higher learning. You have let me know
that you've heard of and taken courses, you have offered lists of universities
with courses, and have even let me know that you have read books about Cobol.

U.S. News and World Report recently published a list of the top colleges and
universities in the United States. Among them were the likes of MIT and Cal
Tech. We polled the top 10 institutions on the list and found that in the
classroom Cobol is mentioned only anecdotally in context of the history of
programming, but not a single school currently offers coursework in Cobol. Not
one.
...
To the many who have cited the open-source movement as the catalyst thrusting
Cobol into a "full-throttled resurgence," to quote one reader, you will be
disappointed to learn that none has produced a Cobol-compliant compiler.
...
Finally, if anyone is hiring Cobol programmers, I have rï¿½sumï¿½s from people
blaming me for their layoffs. Let me know at james_borck@infoworld.com.
```

```
james_borck@infoworld.com.

The reason that there is no open source COBOL compiler is because it is
such a powerful language.  It is very difficult to write a compiler for
it.  The progress made on open COBOL compilers is really making good
progress.  It just doesn't appear to be.

The C language has only about 40 odd function points.  It is a
primitive language.  COBOL has about 300 or more function points.  Some
of those function points are very complicated.  In fact, the COBOL
standards committee keeps bringing up "function overload" as a
problem.  The INVOKE function point was created in order to not
overload the CALL function point.  My rough estimate is that COBOL is
20 to 50 times more powerful that C, C++, C# or any homologs like
Java.  Maybe 100 times more powerful.
Hence it takes 50 times more effort to produce a COBOL compiler.

People like Bork remain ignorant of this fact.  Businesses are
only now beginning to realize that COBOL is a powerful tool that
increases developer productivity, especially in business areas.  Over
the long term COBOL centered businesses are more competitive that C
centered businesses.  Bork focuses on academia.  Business focuses on
practical productivity.





Sent via Deja.com
http://www.deja.com/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Infoworld: COBOL outdated and losing support

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-11T09:34:39-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A5DE09F.7A2BE345@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<20010110214928.29798.00001900@ng-cs1.aol.com> <93khhs$4cu$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
With schools dropping CoBOL from their curricula, companies may need to
drop CoBOL from their choice of languages as well.

But if CoBOL goes away from enterprise environments, it won't be because it
is replaced by OO languages.   Enterprises cannot afford to have all of
their systems and programs integrated in that way.

If it goes away, it will be because business rules have migrated from the
applications programs.  Database administrators will control most of the
business rules.   Whether you access your data via a Java program, XTML,
CoBOL, or even your word processor won't be very important.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infoworld: COBOL outdated and losing support

- **From:** luke <luke_bryan@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-11T23:04:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<93le5k$10u$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<20010110214928.29798.00001900@ng-cs1.aol.com> <93khhs$4cu$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A5DE09F.7A2BE345@brazee.net>`

```
In article <3A5DE09F.7A2BE345@brazee.net>,
  Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> With schools dropping CoBOL from their curricula, companies may need
to
> drop CoBOL from their choice of languages as well.
>
> But if CoBOL goes away from enterprise environments, it won't be
because it
> is replaced by OO languages.   Enterprises cannot afford to have all
of
> their systems and programs integrated in that way.
>

the
> applications programs.  Database administrators will control most of
the
> business rules.   Whether you access your data via a Java program,
XTML,
> CoBOL, or even your word processor won't be very important.
>

Academia shuns COBOL.  So what?  Here is a quote from Steve McConnell,
editor-in-chief of IEEE Software magazine,

"We only have 40 percent of developers graduating with computer science
degrees or MIS degrees.  So right off the bat, we have 60 percent who
don't graduate with a relevant degree.  They're picking up their sole
training on the job or through training that their employers offer.  Of
that 40 percent, I think a lot of the education they receive is not all
that practical."  Intelligent Enterprise August 1, 2000 p. 58.

It's a lot harder to teach a powerful language like COBOL.
That academia wishes to teach the "fun" stuff at the expense of
the "practical" stuff doesn't change the business bottom line.

Businesses have been training their own COBOL developers since 1959.
I don't know the percentages of on-the-job trained COBOL developers
today vs. 30 years ago.  But I would bet they are about the same.

If academia takes a flying leap into the Atlantic business won't
notice much.  (Except businesses that sell them office supplies.)



Sent via Deja.com
http://www.deja.com/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infoworld: COBOL outdated and losing support

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-12T05:32:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WFw76.135234$59.36670624@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<20010110214928.29798.00001900@ng-cs1.aol.com> <93khhs$4cu$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A5DE09F.7A2BE345@brazee.net> <93le5k$10u$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Now let's be fair to academia. Their primary job is to teach people how to
learn, not to give them a specific skill. Nor is the curriculum based on
'fun', it is based on 'fundamentals'. As others have said, if you want to
learn a trade go to a trade school.

My first computer-related course, more years ago than I care to admit, was
called 'Numerical Methods'. Assignments were to be done in Fortran. No class
time was spent on the language. We were expected to muddle through that on
our own, with some help from the overworked teaching assistant. The
following semester there was a 'Digital Systems' course which involved
writing algorithms in M6800 assembler, but learning the language, or the
VM/CMS operating system so we could use the cross-assembler,  was not part
of the curriculum either. I had to teach myself another 3 or 4 languages and
as many operating systems before graduation. Anybody else coded DART
applications for a Zilog Z80?

I also take exception to the term 'powerful' as an adjective to COBOL. I
would support the use of 'effective' or 'verbose'. COBOL relies on verbs.
Most modern languages rely on functions. COBOL waits on the whim of compiler
vendors, and a standards committee that moves slowly to adopt change.

Pragmatic organizations will continue to use COBOL regardless of what some
confrontational news article says. However, as organizations adopt packaged
solutions, or migrate to newer database technologies, the relevance of COBOL
will diminish. In the meantime, COBOL still has a long life ahead.


- Karl Wagner





"luke" <luke_bryan@my-deja.com> wrote in message
news:93le5k$10u$1@nnrp1.deja.com...

>   Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
OK - but they also have a job in teaching people about the world.   Someone
graduating with a MIS degree needs to know why he learned Pascal and why the
business world uses CoBOL.  He should know that there is no such thing as "a
CoBOL" file, and what he needs to do to interface with legacy business
applications.

And he shouldn't have to attend community college at night in order to be ready
to get a job when he graduates!
```

```
In article <WFw76.135234$59.36670624@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>,

> Now let's be fair to academia. Their primary job is to teach people
how to

We agree.  Using academia's decision to shun COBOL is not a
fair barometer of what business finds useful.  Bork is unfair in
trying to use academia as the barometer.

Speaking of trade school, check out www.learningtree.com.  They have 13
COBOL courses scheduled at various places around the country for
this year.  I don't have their schedule for last year so I can't
compare the increase.  People are signing up for these at $2,345.00 a
pop.  Is that a fair barometer?

> My first computer-related course, more years ago than I care to
admit, was
> called 'Numerical Methods'. Assignments were to be done in Fortran.
No class
> time was spent on the language. We were expected to muddle through
that on
> our own, with some help from the overworked teaching assistant. The
> following semester there was a 'Digital Systems' course which involved
> writing algorithms in M6800 assembler, but learning the language, or
the
> VM/CMS operating system so we could use the cross-assembler,  was not
part
> of the curriculum either. I had to teach myself another 3 or 4
languages and
> as many operating systems before graduation. Anybody else coded DART
> applications for a Zilog Z80?
>
> I also take exception to the term 'powerful' as an adjective to
COBOL. I
> would support the use of 'effective' or 'verbose'. COBOL relies on
verbs.
> Most modern languages rely on functions. COBOL waits on the whim of
compiler
> vendors, and a standards committee that moves slowly to adopt change.
>

I intentionally use the adjective "powerful" as it is appropriate.
COBOL does decimal arithmetic.  C does not.  In C, one has to write
their own subroutine.  Try explaining to a CFO why the numbers don't
come out exact on his statements because C uses floating point
processing.

To sort a file in COBOL, simply put:
SORT FILE-SORT
    INPUT PROCEDURE BRING-IN-FILE-A
    OUTPUT PROCEDURE WRITE-OUT-FILE-A.
Notice that this allows one to dink around with the records as they are
coming and going.  What can one do in C besides calling an operating
system command to sort the file?  Can one dink with the records?
Write a sort subroutine in C and it could cost one weeks of development
time.  In COBOL one does it in minutes.  One also has the advantage in
COBOL that most all COBOL compiler manufacturers use the most
sophisticated sort algorithms known to man.  Does every C developer
know these when they write their own sort routines?

COBOL allows one to put keys into records and retrieve them
dynamically.  In C one calls a relative database package to do that
sort of thing.

COBOL allows one to search a table by typing
SEARCH ALL TABLE-A
    AT END DISPLAY "NOT FOUND"
    WHEN TABLE-A-KEY(INDEXER) = "ABCD"
        DISPLAY "FOUND IT".
When COBOL programmers see this they instantly know a table is being
searched and how it is being searched.  In C, one would see a call to a
subroutine that a developer wrote.  One would have to check the
subroutine to see what is going on.

COBOL has built in numeric formatting features.  In C, one must write
their own.

I could go on and on.  In the interest of bandwidth I'll stop.

COBOL does an abundance of work for the developer.
C just gives one the frame of a development environment.

COBOL is much more powerful than C.  Always has been.

> Pragmatic organizations will continue to use COBOL regardless of what
some
> confrontational news article says. However, as organizations adopt
packaged
> solutions, or migrate to newer database technologies, the relevance
of COBOL
> will diminish. In the meantime, COBOL still has a long life ahead.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > With schools dropping CoBOL from their curricula, companies may
need
> > to
> > > drop CoBOL from their choice of languages as well.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > is replaced by OO languages.   Enterprises cannot afford to have
all
> > of

> > >

from
> > the

of

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

science

who
```

```
Let me introduce you folks to my pets... peeves that is....

First meet "packaged" - it's a MISTAKE to assume that a packaged
solution is NOT COBOL, but everyone assumes that.  One of the most
successful packaged solutions is PeopleSoft - customized using COBOL.

Second -- Legacy.  The Hearst family is PROUD of their Legacy.  We all
talk about what a "Great Legacy" I will leave my children.  However,
when it comes to applications - "LEGACY" has a negative connotation.
Let's strike it from the COBOL enthusiasts' vocabulary shall we?
These are "Strategic" applications -- not "Legacy".
```

```
"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net

> >
>
> Howe about we use "legacy" only for applications known to be nearing
the end
> of their useful life, regardless of source lanaguage?

That, according to my dictionary, is the way it is supposed to be
used. That is, 'Legacy' is something from the past requiring a change
in process or technique to translate old data files to a new system.

So, if you are changing or about to change an old system, it's a
'legacy' system. Otherwise, it's 'current production.'
```

```
<snip>
Sorry, but I snipped out all threads to put the following statement:

Wasn't COBOL originally defined by DoD as follows:

- based on English language
- easy to read
- easy to write
- easy to maintain
- suitable for administrative problems
- usable on every computer

???

So look at the newer languages:
C, C++, Pascal, JAVA etc : who can easily read and write them?
Ever tried to make a simple printing routine in C(++)? Will cost you more
time then writing out the resultlist by hand. :)
Maintaining a C (or Pascal) program? Fisrt try to read it!
OK, C (and others) is not designed for administrative problems,
Using the mentioned languages on every computer? There is not a standard for
them!
Every manufacturer design its own implementation of the languages. C++ on a
PC (Windows or Unix/Linux) differs from the same language on, eg. an AS/400.

Right, so then use COBOL! The only thing that might be changed is the
ENVIRONMENT DIVISION, but nowadys even that is obsolete.

Jaap,
(programmed Cobol since 1976)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infoworld: COBOL outdated and losing support

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2001-01-26T09:26:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010126042603.12089.00001372@ng-fl1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3A64B768.52F74967@boeing.com>`

```
Ahh hocky-pucky! 

Somewheres round about 99 and 44/100 of all the posters to this thread are
wrong headed in their essential underlying unarticulated assumption.

It is not COBOL or C. It is COBOL _and_ C.

The world just keeps getting bigger and badder.

Despite the insistance of the physicist that entropy is increasing. I think
not. We keep getting more programming methods and nearly all of them persist
indefinitely.

If you want work in the new world there is not much reason to be preferential.
We will all have to work with organizational units with differing tool
emphases.

And we will all have to deal with the fact that some of the newer practitioners
do not understand COBOL, and (as has been amply illustrated) some COBOLers do
not know the history of C and UNIX.

But what is more, it is clear that neither the legacy methods, nor the markup
language oriented tools are handling web _input_ very effectively. So in the
not too distant future we will see more waves of innovation that will submerge
those who think there is such a thing as a single answer.



Robert Rayhawk
rayhawk@alum.calberkeley.org
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infoworld: COBOL outdated and losing support

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-01-27T08:18:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A728466.AC9D2991@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3A64B768.52F74967@boeing.com> <20010126042603.12089.00001372@ng-fl1.aol.com>`

```
RKRayhawk wrote:
> 
> Despite the insistance of the physicist that entropy is increasing. I think
> not. We keep getting more programming methods and nearly all of them persist
> indefinitely.

There was a comment made back ay the end of the eighties to the effect
that:

   In the 60s there was Cobol and 3000 other languages
   In the 80s there was Cobol and 300 other languages
   Now project that forward.

While we do get new languages and methods there is a significant drop
off of older ones.  The 3000 was the result of every manufacturer having
their own assemblers (one for each different machine) plus their own
business and scientific languages.

C has just about replaced all the assemblers and assembly code.  New
languages do arise, but mainly from new needs, such as scripting which
has given us Perl, PHP, Python, Ruby.

> And we will all have to deal with the fact that some of the newer practitioners
> do not understand COBOL, 

Newer practitioners often show that they understand very little. One
reason they do not understand Cobol is that they do not understand the
problem set that Cobol addresses.  Many think that learning programming
is about learning computers and get very computer centric about this.

When I started in computing most programmers were previously working in
businesses in accounting departments or warehouses where they understood
what the business did and they were sent off to learn programming and
join the new computer department.  They could relate to a 'packing slip'
or 'credit note' or any other business operation.

> and (as has been amply illustrated) some COBOLers do
> not know the history of C and UNIX.

History is not as important as the present and the future.  I wonder how
many many Cobolers know the history and current state of CGI.
 
> But what is more, it is clear that neither the legacy methods, nor the markup
> language oriented tools are handling web _input_ very effectively. So in the
> not too distant future we will see more waves of innovation that will submerge
> those who think there is such a thing as a single answer.

What we also need to see is Cobolers, or more importantly, business
programmers putting forward their input to these 'waves of innovation'
to ensure that they too are part of the future and that Cobol and
'boring accounting systems' are not marginalised.

In the current rush for e-Commerse it seems that the business model is
not being taken into account fully by the designers or programmers.  It
seems to be done by scripters (Perl, PHP, etc) who don't fully
understand business needs.  The latest example is large web ordering
systems where it is possible for the user (buyer) to change the price at
a certain point in the order cycle by right-clicking.

As the (Cobol) programming department is more and more taken out of the
cycle then this will happen more often.  The answer is not to decry the
loss of Cobol, but to make sure that the Cobolers can understand what is
happening in computing right now so they can be involved in it.

Stop rewriting the same batch programs over and over again since the 60s
and learn Python, XML, CGI, PHP, ..  just so you can see what's
happening and then contribute.

(of course some of us are).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infoworld: COBOL outdated and losing support

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Jeff Baynard <jeffbaynard@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-27T02:40:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<B6979F5C.1DF%jeffbaynard@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3A64B768.52F74967@boeing.com> <20010126042603.12089.00001372@ng-fl1.aol.com> <3A728466.AC9D2991@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
As has been said before, the bottom line is that It's The Artist, Not The
Paintbrush.  Our department just ripped off a very complex new retail
pricing system and the core code is all in COBOL.  The system immediately
improved sales of our company bigtime.  There are many other good languages
too that do things better than COBOL.  But when it comes to batch processing
and business logic, COBOL is certainly capable.

I sometimes send off code stubs in e-mails to all different kinds of people,
even people that have no business reading computer code at all.  But somehow
they all understand COBOL.  I wouldn't send out a C function or some
KornShell to try to help explain something.

I think COBOL will be around for as long as it helps provide a business
solution better than other products, and more importantly, that people can
use it well.  The basic goodness in COBOL is that it's so easy to be
productive in it, and to teach others how to use it.  Most changes to the
code are handled with ease too.

Just a little arrogance for the naysayers.

Jeff



in article 3A728466.AC9D2991@Azonic.co.nz, Richard Plinston at
riplin@Azonic.co.nz wrote on 1/27/01 3:18 AM:

> RKRayhawk wrote:
>> 
…[68 more quoted lines elided]…
> (of course some of us are).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Infoworld: COBOL outdated and losing support

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-01-12T08:06:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A5EBAF2.4F2EC39F@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<20010110214928.29798.00001900@ng-cs1.aol.com> <93khhs$4cu$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
> > Enterprise Strategies | James R. Borck
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> revisions.

C is 30 years old, C++ is 20 years old.

> > Although the updates in 1974 and 1985 were essentially published for
> the
> > introduction of new features, it was not until the final revision,
> Cobol 97,
> > that object-oriented constructs were appended to the ANSI standard.

The introduction of OO into C to make C++ was primarily to overcome the
limitations of the C language.  For example if you want a 'package' of
complex numbers in C you have to do it with functions.  

With built in types one can write expressions, ( a + b ) * c, where a, b
and c are of integer or float types, long or short.  If one creates a
'type' (usually a struct) for a complex number, or long decimal, then
these cannot be used in expressions and you have to use functions:

          cmplxaddint(complex1, integer);
          cmplxmultflt(complex1, float);
          ....

for _every_ combination.  This soon gets very tedious.

With C++ the complex or long decimal can be made an 'object' and the
expression operators can be overloaded so that these can be used in
expressions.

In COBOL a MOVE will work with every type that can be described.  All
numeric types can be used in any COMPUTE: packed, float, fixed decimal,
in any combination.

COBOL never had the limitations that C++ was developed to overcome in C.

Now it is true that OO has more general application such as information
hiding and inheretence.  These can be done just as well without OO
language support, whereas overloading cannot.  

The problem with OO is that C++ becomes a tool with which a programmer
can develop their own computer language.  They can define what the
operators are to mean, it is then that the language manuals will not be
able to tell you what a piece of code will do, it will be necessary to
refer to the documentation, or actual code, of several objects.  While
_superficially_ and in small projects it appears that C++ can simplify
the code, underlying this is all the complexity of the classes which may
be unique to each site or system.

I prefer to write in a deterministic language, such as Cobol.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infoworld: COBOL outdated and losing support

- **From:** Support@ScreenIO.com (Kevin J. Hansen)
- **Date:** 2001-01-11T23:02:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3a5e3bbf.161458334@news>`
- **References:** `<20010110214928.29798.00001900@ng-cs1.aol.com> <93khhs$4cu$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A5EBAF2.4F2EC39F@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
On Fri, 12 Jan 2001 08:06:10 +0000, Richard Plinston
<riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Now it is true that OO has more general application such as information
>hiding and inheretence.  These can be done just as well without OO
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>I prefer to write in a deterministic language, such as Cobol.

Richard,

Right on, particularly that part about "developing their own
language."

When you embed C functions deeply enough, it becomes practically
impossible for anyone - even the original developer - to figure out
what's going on.  God help you if (when?) he leaves.

One of the best parts about COBOL is that a beginner can become
productive (on maintenance, anyway) in a relatively short time.  With
C, I'd venture to guess that maintenance is far more difficult than
new program development.  I'd also guess that more than a little C
code gets rewritten from scratch for just that reason, although I have
no proof of it.

Since most shops do maintenance, it makes sense to use a language that
facilitates that task.

Incidentally (re the Infoworld article) I'd also surmise that
universities don't teach COBOL for the same reason they don't teach
welding, refrigeration, or auto mechanics; it's not an "academic"
subject.  Bet plenty of vocational schools are still teaching it,
though.

Kevin
Norcom - COBOL Programming Tools
GUI ScreenIO - A Windows UI for COBOL
http://www.screenio.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infoworld: COBOL outdated and losing support

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** epc2@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2001-01-12T02:44:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<93lr2h$c32$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<20010110214928.29798.00001900@ng-cs1.aol.com> <93khhs$4cu$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A5EBAF2.4F2EC39F@Azonic.co.nz> <3a5e3bbf.161458334@news>`

```

> When you embed C functions deeply enough, it becomes practically
> impossible for anyone - even the original developer - to figure out
> what's going on.

To say nothing of pushing and popping 37 stack frames just to put an
"A" in the upper left hand corner of the screen :-).

Seriously, what I think has hurt the maintainability of code written in
C style languages is that a lot of the work of writing the program
involves understanding someone else's function library, even more so in
object oriented programs. So now programming consists of spending 8
hours reading manuals, understanding an obscure API, then writing code.
And the more you know about the details of using their interface, the
more you get paid!

Language zealots persist in wishing to transform everything into a
Java like language, even COBOL. Why not learn something from Unix?
Write an intermediate text file and pass it off to another program!
Who says that old COBOL programmers are better replaced than retrained?
[see recent infoworld article discussing the views of the Gartner group
on this subject]. Perhaps CICS programmers might be better at
understanding client/server computing?

COBOL will survive because it performs well in doing its original jobs
- handling text and exact amounts of money.

When it comes to bank statements and utility bills, I would prefer
something reliable, even if it is 30+ years old!
------
Elliot



Sent via Deja.com
http://www.deja.com/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infoworld: COBOL outdated and losing support

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** epc2@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2001-01-12T15:30:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<93n7uk$fpj$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<20010110214928.29798.00001900@ng-cs1.aol.com> <93khhs$4cu$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A5EBAF2.4F2EC39F@Azonic.co.nz> <3a5e3bbf.161458334@news> <93lr2h$c32$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <TJC76.22$CM6.27294@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
  "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
> <epc2@my-deja.com> wrote in message
> > ... I would prefer something reliable, even if it is 30+ years old!
>
> Something reliable? You mean like me, you, and eighty-plus percent of
the
> people who post here?
>
> MCM
> (Genuine Baby Boomer)

I have vivid memories of being called upon to repair the damage created
by a poorly paid high school student who wrote the first version of an
accounting (+marketing) system (in dBase II, not in COBOL). No
documentation, etc. I spent 3 days looking at program listings before I
started on making the program run at a respectable speed. The owner of
the business moaned a bit about the bill, but was happy to pay and keep
his mortuary going!

When it comes to serious software, there is something to be said for
the wisdom of age. Consider the psychological theories of Piaget, who
said that the ability to do abstract thinking does not fully develop
until early adult life. Combine this with juvenile needs for self
expression, rebellion, and individuation, and you have a recipe for
software disaster. [Also imagine the cyber-vandel with a keyboard
instead of a spray can saying "Hey world, look at me", I've just
```

```
Another posted noted that the libraries change out from under

C++ system I have (which uses the current STL) do not match.  So
I spend lots of time running down functions that should work, but
don't.  I've just found that HEX(variable) was replaced by
stl::hex<<variable if I want it to print out in "hex" format
(don't quote me completely on this, that was as much as I
understood after scanning an answer to me on this problem).

Give me COBOL, FORTRAN, RPG, or ALC and I'll make a report
EXACTLY the way I want it and in less than a week.  After a month
with C++ I still haven't gotten far enough to do simple
formatting (and all I want to do is write a program to write
checks!).  All this is, is an add on to the old read a card,
write a line logic.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infoworld: COBOL outdated and losing support

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-12T23:30:56-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<93oljd$vkl$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<20010110214928.29798.00001900@ng-cs1.aol.com> <93khhs$4cu$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A5EBAF2.4F2EC39F@Azonic.co.nz> <3a5e3bbf.161458334@news> <93lr2h$c32$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A5F0DFD.5370938B@brazee.net> <73233685D2982B05.53F6234B9F65F6C7.74D9EB0E2122077B@lp.airnews.net> <3A601A8A.8A59FF39@Azonic.co.nz> <3A5F7848.F1596793@neo.rr.com>`

```
Since HEX(variable) was never part of C++, it was not
replaced by anything! It is most likely that HEX(variable)
was a macro that looked something like:

#define HEX(variable) {std::cout<<std::hex<<variable;}

It was probably in a file that wasn't included by the
programmer! :-)

If the absence of HEX(variable) was the case you found
to have shown the libraries were "changed out from under
you", then you are mistaken.

If your books on C++ were printed prior to 1997 there may
be differences between the books and the implementation.
Prior to 1997, the C++ standard was undergoing changes
to become an ANSI/ISO standard. The same applies to
books on OO COBOL prior to 2001 (or whenever).

I am one who thinks C is great and C++ has potential.
It seems I am also one of the few who understands that C
and C++ are two separate languages, each with their own
ANSI/ISO standards. (In other words, do not use C/C++
to identify a language! There is no language with that name!)

Bell Labs (now AT&T Labs) spent a lot of money on
computers and salaries to produce B, C, and C++. It is not
likely those involved were anything less than serious about
their work or the languages they produced.

Apparently, you also think that C++ is a serious language,
at least serious enough to deserve your attention.
------------------
Rick Smith

Steve Thompson wrote in message <3A5F7848.F1596793@neo.rr.com>...
[...]
>Actually, I think the author was fooled into thinking B and C
>were serious languages because Bell Labs bought into them.
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>write a line logic.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infoworld: COBOL outdated and losing support

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-01-14T09:42:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A61749F.5FFAA52B@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<20010110214928.29798.00001900@ng-cs1.aol.com> <93khhs$4cu$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A5EBAF2.4F2EC39F@Azonic.co.nz> <3a5e3bbf.161458334@news> <93lr2h$c32$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A5F0DFD.5370938B@brazee.net> <73233685D2982B05.53F6234B9F65F6C7.74D9EB0E2122077B@lp.airnews.net> <3A601A8A.8A59FF39@Azonic.co.nz> <3A5F7848.F1596793@neo.rr.com> <93oljd$vkl$1@news.hitter.net> <NM%76.1197$BQ6.63418@news4.atl>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> C was developed by collegiate people along with UNIX, and they were
> about as serious as Monty Python.  If you don't believe it, just
> look up the derivation of the names of the common utility programs. ;-)

The names of the original set of utility programs (rm, ls, cd, ed, ..)
derived directly from that functionality within the interactive sessions
of GE's Multix and was not 'invented' within AT&T.  

Many other Unix utilities were developed outside AT&T.  

C derived from B which was developed as an improvement on BCPL.  BCPL
was a 'Basic (fundemental not BASIC) CPL' a cut down version of
'Combined Programming Language', a major development effot of the Atlas
Laboritories to combine the functionality of several languages (mostly
APL, BPL and ACL) much as PL/I did  a decade later.

These weren't schoolboy projects, they were done by serious dedicated
computer engineers (both software and hardware) and your derisive
comments are unrequired.
```

```
I have to jump in here to clarify something. DOD did help develop COBOL via
the participation of Charles Phillips in an effort called CODASYL. Among the
companies/organizations involved in that group were:

AllState Insurance
Exxon
General Motors
Southern Railways
Westinghouse
Airforce Logisitics Command
Univac/IBM/Honeywell
US Steel
And no doubt I've forgotten to include someone who should be.

Yes, DOD, became involved in this effort, but the effort preceded elsewhere
first.

Warren Simmons
warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net
Canadian Federal Government
```

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>

- **Message-ID:** `<3A6203EE.D634606E@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<20010110214928.29798.00001900@ng-cs1.aol.com> <93khhs$4cu$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A5EBAF2.4F2EC39F@Azonic.co.nz> <3a5e3bbf.161458334@news> <93lr2h$c32$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A5F0DFD.5370938B@brazee.net> <73233685D2982B05.53F6234B9F65F6C7.74D9EB0E2122077B@lp.airnews.net> <3A601A8A.8A59FF39@Azonic.co.nz> <3A5F7848.F1596793@neo.rr.com> <93oljd$vkl$1@news.hitter.net> <NM%76.1197$BQ6.63418@news4.atl> <3A61749F.5FFAA52B@Azonic.co.nz> <7o386.4113$ag.135479@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Jerry P wrote:
> 
> Doesn't alter the facts that Unix was developed forty years ago by a
> money-losing subsidiary of the telephone company.

1970 was _thirty_ years ago or so, not 40.

Few R&D departments actually _make_ money, that is not part of their
function.  As at 1982 they had over 60 scientific and engineering awards
including 7 Nobel Prizes.

Why would you think that making or 'losing' money was significant ?
(given that it was not an objective).

> Whereas COBOL was developed fifty years ago by the Department of
> Defense.

1959 was much closer to 40 years ago than 50.

Cobol was developed by CODASYL (Conference on Data Systems Languages), a
group that consisted of computer manufacturers and users (in almost
equal proportions).  This group was _sponsered_ and _promoted_ by the
DoD.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infoworld: COBOL outdated and losing support

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-14T14:28:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gIi86.3732$Ep3.239708@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<20010110214928.29798.00001900@ng-cs1.aol.com> <93khhs$4cu$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A5EBAF2.4F2EC39F@Azonic.co.nz> <3a5e3bbf.161458334@news> <93lr2h$c32$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A5F0DFD.5370938B@brazee.net> <73233685D2982B05.53F6234B9F65F6C7.74D9EB0E2122077B@lp.airnews.net> <3A601A8A.8A59FF39@Azonic.co.nz> <3A5F7848.F1596793@neo.rr.com> <93oljd$vkl$1@news.hitter.net> <NM%76.1197$BQ6.63418@news4.atl> <3A61749F.5FFAA52B@Azonic.co.nz> <7o386.4113$ag.135479@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3A6203EE.D634606E@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
In an effort to clarify (for me and anyone else interested) the sponsorship
of CODASYL, the dictionary says:


1: one who assumes responsiblility for a person or thing.
etc.

DOD, DoD or what have you did NOT sponsor CODASYL. CODASYL inlisted the
participation of:

a: several companies some of which I have listed as memory fails me at times

b. several branches of the armed services, AFLC, Navy, AirForce as members
of the Executive Committee{

        to avoid this effort being a government or any sector of business
project...  to assist in the final placement of "products" in a public
domain vs. G.I. You may, but it is not possible to assignment of sponsorship
of CODASYL to any ONE organization.  IT was planned that way.

    It is possible as most always occures to gloss over the facts in a way
to simplify the "credit."

BTW, one of the members forgotten in my earlier response to this thread, was
Lockheed from whence Norm Reem or Rheem or ? went on to the Navy, convinced
Congress to re-instate Grace into the Navy, and keep her there at their
pleasure.  As Grace was a memberr of CODASYL, the sponsor was the people.

Or perhaps the Navy was the sponsor, but each member supported it's own
people and there were no group funds or governemental ties.

Warren Simmons,
Fromer Member of CODASYL, AND SECRETARY TO THE EXECUTIVE COMMITEE FOR SOME
YEARS.

p.s. it would seem appropriate to change this thread to COBOL before it's
time.
wgs
warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net



Cobol was developed by CODASYL (Conference on Data Systems Languages), a
group that consisted of computer manufacturers and users (in almost
equal proportions).  This group was _sponsered_ and _promoted_ by the
DoD.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infoworld: COBOL outdated and losing support

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** frlsfly@aol.com (FRLSFLY)
- **Date:** 2001-01-13T22:37:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010113173741.20125.00000004@ng-ft1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3A61749F.5FFAA52B@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
>The names of the original set of utility programs (rm, ls, cd, ed, ..)
>derived directly from that functionality within the interactive sessions
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Well, not that I was there...but:

First, it was spelt "MultiCS" no "x".   It stood for MULTiplexed Information
and Computer System.

Second, my understanding is that Thompson, Ritchie & Osanna wrote 'C' and UNIX
because their department blew the budget and couldn't afford to pay the
compiler and O/S software licenses on their DEC PDP-7.

As this meant they could no longer play  and extend this very absorbing
computer game called "Space Travel",  they worked up their own quick and dirty
compiler and O/S so they could get back to it. 
(Rumor has it they had been spending too much time playing Space Travel and not
enough working on Multics, which is why neither ATT nor  DARPA were interested
in extending them further funding- though the Multics project continued on.)
 
Third, fellow I know over at Cal, who has been in the trade since the mid-60's,
says he had some aquaintance with Ken Thompson, and that Thompson is fairly
horrified that Ritchie started pushing this slop as a practical solution to
real world problems.   He quotes KT as saying that if he had KNOWN that anybody
was going to try and use 'C' for anything besides stupid games he would have
put some THOUGHT into it!

Finally: get real.  This was the late 60s.  These were young guys doing cutting
edge stuff with somebody elses money.  If they weren't at LEAST half-in-the-bag
much of the time I'd be surprised.  I always figured that was why the best UNIX
Sys Admins were also the bigest stoners.


Nathan Meyer
Berkeley, CA
"Big Endian" is what I like.
```
