```
LOL!

A couple of points - _Space Travel_ led directly to the UNIX file system,
which is still
pretty much the same thing we use today.

They had to use a cross assembler under Multics to produce a PDP-7 program,
which was
then used to create a paper tape - which was then carried to a PDP-7
computer with a graphics
processor. Thompson and Ritchie had rewritten the game from scratch for that
PDP-7.

That re-write is really the significant point, since they didn't use any
existing software on the
PDP-7; they wrote a floating point package, pointwise specification of the
graphics characters
the display, and a debugging system that continuously displayed the contents
of typed in memory
locations in a corner of the screen.

This of course, because a game of Space Travel (which was essentially a
simulation of the movement
of the major bodies of the solar system, in fact a pretty darn accurate
simulation) cost about $75
in CPU time under GECOS. (At the time they ported the game, it ran in
FORTRAN under GECOS.
Oh yeah, and the display was jerky too. :)

-Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infoworld: COBOL outdated and losing support

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-01-14T20:15:30+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<3A6208E2.D7ED8E80@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3A61749F.5FFAA52B@Azonic.co.nz> <20010113173741.20125.00000004@ng-ft1.aol.com>`

```
FRLSFLY wrote:

> Well, not that I was there...but:
> 
> First, it was spelt "MultiCS" no "x".   It stood for MULTiplexed Information
> and Computer System.

Quite right.
 
> Second, my understanding is that Thompson, Ritchie & Osanna wrote 'C' and UNIX
> because their department blew the budget and couldn't afford to pay the
> compiler and O/S software licenses on their DEC PDP-7.
 
> As this meant they could no longer play  and extend this very absorbing
> computer game called "Space Travel",  

'Space Travel' was _not_ a 'game' that was 'played'.  There were no
aliens to shoot, no game play, no plot.  It was an astronomical
calculator that could give the location of the planets in the solar
system given date and time.

> they worked up their own quick and dirty
> compiler and O/S so they could get back to it.

The program was originally compiled on the GE machine and had to be
transferred to the PDP-9 that was otherwise unused in order to run it. 
The original Unix was written in 1969 so that they could compile and run
on the same machine avoiding the transfer.  This Unix and the compiler
were written in assembler, as was Space Travel.

They didn't write 'C and Unix'.  Unix started in 1969 in assembler.  B
was written by Thompson in 1970.  C was written in 1972.  Unix was
rewritten into C at edition 6 or 7 in 1973.

This wasn't 'because they couldn't pay the licence fees on the PDP-9,
nor was 4 years 'quick and dirty'. By the time the C version of Unix was
out it was being used extensively within AT&T on many different machines
of several types.

> Third, fellow I know over at Cal, who has been in the trade since the mid-60's,
> says he had some aquaintance with Ken Thompson, and that Thompson is fairly
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> put some THOUGHT into it!

I suspect that your 'fellow I know' or his 'aquaintence' were taken in
by the same spoof April 1 document.  If you want Thompson's _real_
opinions then read some of his actual articles such as those in 'The
Bell Technical Journal, July-Aug 1978' or in the 'Unix Programmers
Manual' (I have edition 7).

Your 'quote' is directly from the _spoof_ and bears no resemblence to
anything that he actually has said.

> Finally: get real.  This was the late 60s.  These were young guys doing cutting
> edge stuff with somebody elses money.  If they weren't at LEAST half-in-the-bag
> much of the time I'd be surprised.  I always figured that was why the best UNIX
> Sys Admins were also the bigest stoners.

Now you have degraded from mere misinformation to entire speculation.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ [OT]; Re: Infoworld: COBOL outdated and losing support

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-14T06:56:12-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<93s477$20ne$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<3A6208E2.D7ED8E80@Azonic.co.nz> <20010114050850.14929.00000937@ng-fu1.aol.com>`

```
H. M. Deitel, "An Introduction to Operating Systems,"
Addison Wesley, 1983, gives a history of UNIX.

Deitel says that, during 1965-1969, Bell Laboratories
participated with General Electric and Project MAC at
MIT in the development of Multics. In 1969, Bell Labs
withdrew from Multics.

"Some members of the research staff began to work on
a far less ambitious system called UNIX. The group led
by Bell Labs staff member Ken Thompson, sought to
create a computing environment for facilitating programming
research and development. The first version of UNIX was
produced for DEC's PDP-7 and was written in assembly
language.

"The UNIX effort attracted Dennis Ritchie who was
instrumental in rewriting UNIX in the C high-level language ..."

Deitel goes on to report that, "In 1973, Western Electric
agreed to license the system to nonprofit organizations. ...
By 1975, UNIX had become very popular in universities. ...
The first release of UNIX for public consumption was version
five. It looked more like the result of a research project
rather than a product development effort. Version six
appeared in 1975, and is still in wide use today [1983].
The Seventh Edition System, released in 1979, reflects a
great deal of polishing designed to make UNIX a valid
commercial product."

The image I get from Deitel is that researchers who
were, likely, involved with Multics were displaced by the
withdrawal of Bell Labs. These researchers wishing
to continue work on an operating system, started a new
project with Bell Labs "blessing." By chance, a language
researcher and an operating systems researcher
collaborated to accomplish something "interesting."

Finally, Bell Labs (through Western Electric) licensed
(not "gave away") non-commercial, unsupported source
code to nonprofit organizations (primarily universities).

Since this has moved too far from COBOL, this will
be my last comment.
------------------
Rick Smith

so
```

- **References:** `<20010110214928.29798.00001900@ng-cs1.aol.com> <93khhs$4cu$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A5EBAF2.4F2EC39F@Azonic.co.nz> <3a5e3bbf.161458334@news> <93lr2h$c32$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A5F0DFD.5370938B@brazee.net> <73233685D2982B05.53F6234B9F65F6C7.74D9EB0E2122077B@lp.airnews.net> <3A601A8A.8A59FF39@Azonic.co.nz> <3A5F7848.F1596793@neo.rr.com> <93oljd$vkl$1@news.hitter.net> <NM%76.1197$BQ6.63418@news4.atl>`

```
History Weenie Correction:
"C" was created by Dennis Ritchie in the early 1970's as part of the first
UNIX system.
M. D. McIlroy ported the very first language to UNIX, TMG - a "syntax
directed" language
developed in 1964/1965 to UNIX in 1970. Ken Thompson decided that they
couldn't sell
UNIX to AT&T management unless it had a  FORTRAN compiler, and started to
write one
in TMG soon thereafter. He wound up with a language definition and compiler
for "B" - which
owed more than a little to BCPL. (BCPL being essentially a early
(1968/1969?) tool for writing
compilers.It's descendants are LEXX and YACC and so forth.)

This all happened on the PDP-7 platform at Bell Labs.

Then the crew obtained a PDP-11 in 1971, and "B" was moved to it. Remember,
at this time
the entire O/S and most programs were still written in assembler language.
The desire to get
away from that led to the development of "C" and more, the re-write of the
UNIX kernel into
C starting in 1973.

Oh, and the point being it was written for fun exactly, it was written to
make selling UNIX to
AT&T management easier. As a side point, the way they did sell UNIX was a
word-processing /
typesetting system. <grin>

Now given that, does anyone know when COBOL was first implemented on a UNIX
platform?
I didn't encounter UNIX till 1982, and at time, COBOL compilers were
available for every commercial
UNIX platform I knew of. I'm sort of an amateur computer historian, and if
anyone has info on
the subject, I would sure love to document it into my notes. :)

Thanks
-Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infoworld: COBOL outdated and losing support

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-16T15:12:38-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<942a2u$1sc$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<20010110214928.29798.00001900@ng-cs1.aol.com> <93khhs$4cu$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A5EBAF2.4F2EC39F@Azonic.co.nz> <3a5e3bbf.161458334@news> <93lr2h$c32$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A5F0DFD.5370938B@brazee.net> <73233685D2982B05.53F6234B9F65F6C7.74D9EB0E2122077B@lp.airnews.net> <3A601A8A.8A59FF39@Azonic.co.nz> <3A5F7848.F1596793@neo.rr.com> <93oljd$vkl$1@news.hitter.net> <941q1j$pcp$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

luke wrote in message <941q1j$pcp$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>In article <93oljd$vkl$1@news.hitter.net>,
>  "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>some benefit.

It is possible to do in C some of what cannot be done or cannot
be done easily in C++ or COBOL or clearly in COBOL. This is
the benefit of C.

It is possible to do in C++ some of what cannot be done or
cannot be done easily or clearly in COBOL or C. This is the
benefit of C++

It is easy to do some things in COBOL that cannot be done
easily or clearly in C or C++. This is the benefit of COBOL.

For C, C++, and COBOL, those are the benefits -- everything
else, in a particular language, is "of benefit" (or an advantage)
depending upon the application.

COBOL has, by design, a distinct advantage in high-volume
processing and reporting of financial and other business related
data. The more the requirements of an application differ from
this distinct advantage -- the greater the benefit in using another
language.

The point (extent of difference) at which a COBOL programmer
will choose to implement all or part of an application in other
languages will depend upon how comfortable that programmer
is with those other languages. This means that a COBOL
programmer, who does not know or is uncomfortable with
other languages, will use COBOL even if it not the best
language for that application. This can result in well-constructed
gibberish to those who would have easily recognized that which
was being done were it programmed in a suitable language!

It is a corollary that a C or C++ programmer, who does not know
or is uncomfortable with COBOL, will use C or C++ even if it is
not the best language for the application.  This too can result in
well-constructed gibberish to those who would have easily
recognized that which was being done were it programmed in
COBOL!

The ability to avoid such problems and to present clear,
understandable solutions is the benefit of knowing multiple
languages.

[I eliminated C# from considration. It is a Microsoft product for
Windows that, as I understand it, is part IDE and part language.]
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infoworld: COBOL outdated and losing support

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A65AB01.2E826A6E@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<20010110214928.29798.00001900@ng-cs1.aol.com> <93khhs$4cu$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A5EBAF2.4F2EC39F@Azonic.co.nz> <3a5e3bbf.161458334@news> <93lr2h$c32$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A5F0DFD.5370938B@brazee.net> <73233685D2982B05.53F6234B9F65F6C7.74D9EB0E2122077B@lp.airnews.net> <3A601A8A.8A59FF39@Azonic.co.nz> <3A5F7848.F1596793@neo.rr.com> <93oljd$vkl$1@news.hitter.net> <941q1j$pcp$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <942a2u$1sc$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:

> The point (extent of difference) at which a COBOL programmer
> will choose to implement all or part of an application in other
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> COBOL!

These are very important considerations which really have nothing to do
with the language itself.  An adequate tool can be preferred to a superior
tool depending upon the skills of the tool users.   Or sometimes a superior
tool is only superior with a superior user (Tiger Wood's golf clubs are
very hard for most people to use - and assembly language can allow a novice
to really mess things up big time).

So what happens if you are starting a new MIS shop and you determine the
ideal tools for your environment are PL/I, RPG II, and IMS - then you look
around for experienced programmers to staff your shop?   To Bill Gates'
delight, you go with a lower common denominator.

So you decide to do some research to determine what types of programmers
will be available in the next 10 years.  You read Infoworld.  You check
with your local universities.  You find out what "experts" have to say...

The Pygmalion effect is real.
```

```
>Rick Smith wrote:

In the quoted paragraphs, I was not discussing the language
itself. I was discussing how the product of a programmer, skilled
in and using only one language, might be perceived by another
programmer, who is skilled in and uses multiple languages.

>  An adequate tool can be preferred to a superior
>tool depending upon the skills of the tool users.

I used "who does not know or is uncomfortable with" to state
that a programmer may lack certain skills for using other
languages. Certainly, their skill level will affect their
preference.

>tool is only superior with a superior user

I would drop the first "superior". A tool is just a tool until it is
used. I previously expressed a similar sentiment, except that
I used the word "technology."

>very hard for most people to use

not available for public use! ;-)

However, my game is not good enough to be affected, adversely;
therefore, it is possible that my game would improve due to the
better technology in those clubs versus my clubs. ;-)

> - and assembly language can allow a novice
>to really mess things up big time).

I refuse to answer on the grounds that any answer I may give
might tend to incriminate me. ;-)

>So what happens if you are starting a new MIS shop and you determine the
>ideal tools for your environment are PL/I, RPG II, and IMS - then you look
>around for experienced programmers to staff your shop?   To Bill Gates'
>delight, you go with a lower common denominator.

False premise! How would a new shop get a valid license
for RPG II? If a tool is not available, it cannot be ideal! ;-)

Change the premise to RS/6000 running AIX with COBOL,
C++, and DB2. (I think that's a vaild combination and according
to a blurb about C#, C++ is old! COBOL is old and DB2 has been
around for many years. That seems to fit a reasonable pattern --
older but still available technology.) How does that delight
Bill Gates?

>So you decide to do some research to determine what types of programmers
>will be available in the next 10 years.  You read Infoworld.  You check
>with your local universities.  You find out what "experts" have to say...

False premise! You assume that *I* care (or should care) what
those "experts" say! They know nothing of my business needs
(if I had a business)! ;-)

The near-by university teachs Java, C++, FORTRAN, and COBOL
on Sun workstations and IBM mainframes.

The local college teaches Microsoft Visual Basic, Microsoft
C/C++ with MFC, Microsoft and Novell networking, and provides
training for MCSE and other Microsoft and Novell certification
on Windows NT.

I hire from the university. I do not hire from the college until the
college provides suitable training for potential employees of my
business. Furthermore, if my business has several entry-level
openings every year and I find I am turning away alot of their
graduates, I inform the college *their* training is not meeting the
needs of my business.

I must confess that these responses are based upon a sense
of "non-reality." In responding, I know that *I* will not start a
"new MIS shop," I will not purchase equipment, and I will not
hire any staff.

The facts are the county and city still share a S/390 that is
also used for the college's admissions and financial systems.
These county, city, and college systems are mostly COBOL.
A few larger local companies use AS/400 with RPG/400.
This is still the same as it was seven years ago, when I moved
here. I find no evidence that these *real* shops are listening to
Infoworld or so-called "experts".

>The Pygmalion effect is real.

Huh? I am not sure what you mean. Perhaps you could
explain further.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infoworld: COBOL outdated and losing support

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-18T07:40:48-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A670070.CE9B0B6F@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<20010110214928.29798.00001900@ng-cs1.aol.com> <93khhs$4cu$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A5EBAF2.4F2EC39F@Azonic.co.nz> <3a5e3bbf.161458334@news> <93lr2h$c32$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A5F0DFD.5370938B@brazee.net> <73233685D2982B05.53F6234B9F65F6C7.74D9EB0E2122077B@lp.airnews.net> <3A601A8A.8A59FF39@Azonic.co.nz> <3A5F7848.F1596793@neo.rr.com> <93oljd$vkl$1@news.hitter.net> <941q1j$pcp$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <942a2u$1sc$1@news.hitter.net> <3A65AB01.2E826A6E@brazee.net> <9456ji$ad0$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:

> >   Or sometimes a superior
> >tool is only superior with a superior user
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> I used the word "technology."

That wasn't the point I was trying to make though.  Sometimes the best tool for
the average tool user is not the best tool for the expert.  See below:

> > (Tiger Wood's golf clubs are
> >very hard for most people to use
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> better technology in those clubs versus my clubs. ;-)

I have read that most expert golfers even on the PGA tour find his clubs hard
to use.  Those players can get clubs like his - but his clubs aren't very
forgiving and give him very good feedback when he hits the ball off center.  At
his level though, he would rather have the extra feedback than the extra
forgiveness.   People almost as good as he is usually prefer a slightly more
forgiving tool.

> >So you decide to do some research to determine what types of programmers
> >will be available in the next 10 years.  You read Infoworld.  You check
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> (if I had a business)! ;-)

You're not researching your business needs here, you're just researching the
supply of programmers who will be available.   If you don't care for those
sources, what are your sources?  When you write up your findings and present
them to your boss, what will be your reference citations?

> >The Pygmalion effect is real.
>
> Huh? I am not sure what you mean. Perhaps you could
> explain further.

I might have missed the target here - I should have said "self fulfilling
prophecy".  What I meant to say is that if enough people believe that we won't
have CoBOL programmers available, we will restructure our business needs so
that we won't need CoBOL programmers, resulting in fewer people training to
become CoBOL programmers.   Or visa versa.   Expectations can create results.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infoworld: COBOL outdated and losing support

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-18T15:58:20-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<947lgc$3vj$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<20010110214928.29798.00001900@ng-cs1.aol.com> <93khhs$4cu$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A5EBAF2.4F2EC39F@Azonic.co.nz> <3a5e3bbf.161458334@news> <93lr2h$c32$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A5F0DFD.5370938B@brazee.net> <73233685D2982B05.53F6234B9F65F6C7.74D9EB0E2122077B@lp.airnews.net> <3A601A8A.8A59FF39@Azonic.co.nz> <3A5F7848.F1596793@neo.rr.com> <93oljd$vkl$1@news.hitter.net> <941q1j$pcp$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <942a2u$1sc$1@news.hitter.net> <3A65AB01.2E826A6E@brazee.net> <9456ji$ad0$1@news.hitter.net> <3A670070.CE9B0B6F@brazee.net>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote in message <3A670070.CE9B0B6F@brazee.net>...
>Rick Smith wrote:
[...]
>> >So you decide to do some research to determine what types of programmers
>> >will be available in the next 10 years.  You read Infoworld.  You check
>> >with your local universities.  You find out what "experts" have to
say...
>>
>> False premise! You assume that *I* care (or should care) what
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>You're not researching your business needs here, you're just researching
the
>supply of programmers who will be available.   If you don't care for those
>sources, what are your sources?  When you write up your findings and
present
>them to your boss, what will be your reference citations?

The needs of the business are not separable from the research
(unless one is writing an article for Infoworld)! The purpose of
any such research would be to determine the supply of
programmers who will be available "to meet the needs of the
business."

Not all languages, nor programmers trained in those languages,
are of sufficient quality or sufficiently qualified to be considered
as "languages or programmers available to meet the needs of
the business." Compare this with "no one with only a private pilot's
license is sufficiently qualified to fly the aircraft used by an
international air freight company." Consider, also, "no one whose
only training is COBOL is sufficiently qualified to program an
embedded application, with hard real-time constraints, using C++."
The needs of the business are paramount!

At this point, I am not convinced that any individual company
or organization would benefit from such research; except, for
those companies and organizations that produce such
"research  reports."

Such reports often fail to recognize that demand drives the market
-- not supply. If previous reports had come true, we would be
starving for lack of food, freezing for lack of energy, in economic
chaos from Y2K failures, etc.

If businesses need COBOL and schools stop teaching COBOL,
a shortage of COBOL programmers will occur. The schools,
recognizing that COBOL programmers are needed, will begin
training COBOL programmers, again. I have no doubt that
schools will have made that decision long before any company
publishes a "research report" or Infoworld publishes an article
mentioning a shortage of COBOL programmers.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infoworld: COBOL outdated and losing support

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "Steve Dearth" <sdeart31@linuxworld.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-25T23:17:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Bu2c6.48029$YQ.9470434@typhoon.southeast.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<20010110214928.29798.00001900@ng-cs1.aol.com> <93khhs$4cu$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A5EBAF2.4F2EC39F@Azonic.co.nz> <3a5e3bbf.161458334@news> <93lr2h$c32$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A5F0DFD.5370938B@brazee.net> <73233685D2982B05.53F6234B9F65F6C7.74D9EB0E2122077B@lp.airnews.net> <3A601A8A.8A59FF39@Azonic.co.nz> <3A5F7848.F1596793@neo.rr.com> <93oljd$vkl$1@news.hitter.net> <941q1j$pcp$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <942a2u$1sc$1@news.hitter.net> <3A65AB01.2E826A6E@brazee.net> <9456ji$ad0$1@news.hitter.net> <3A670070.CE9B0B6F@brazee.net> <947lgc$3vj$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
This misses an important point.  I graduated from a mainframe oriented
school 3 years ago.  Most of my coursework was in COBOL and Assembler.
Fortunately, I was also exposed to Java, C, C++, etc.

I found that, in an economy where e-business, the web, etc, are considered
essential, I can get paid more and work on more interesting projects by
working in  a language other than COBOL.  The shop I work in writes tooling
that allows web access of legacy (read COBOL/mainframe) applications and
data.  Although we make most of our money on the host stuff, I'm valued for
my C++/Java, while my COBOL skills are seen as a weird throwback to more
primitive days.

My point is, even if schools start to teach COBOL again, students are going
to resist  as long as more "modern" skills are sought by employeers.
Feeling like an old timer when you're 25 isn't a position most college
graduates are happy to find themselves in.


"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote in message
news:947lgc$3vj$1@news.hitter.net...
>
> Howard Brazee wrote in message <3A670070.CE9B0B6F@brazee.net>...

programmers

check

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

those
> >sources, what are your sources?  When you write up your findings and
> present
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```


Steve Dearth wrote:

> This misses an important point.  I graduated from a mainframe oriented
> school 3 years ago.  Most of my coursework was in COBOL and Assembler.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> graduates are happy to find themselves in.

The difficulty is that they don't know that they will need both skills.

Part of this problem is caused by businesses which don't allow CoBOL
programmers to also do Web projects, and who pay more for outsiders to do Web
programs because they can't afford to let their CoBOL programmers have time off
from the important stuff.   It's taking time for corporations and programmers
to learn that we need broad based skills in today's computer environment.  We
need to work with the data wherever they are, and understand the business
needs.   Don't get tied to the tool, associate yourself with the business.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infoworld: COBOL outdated and losing support

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2001-01-26T16:07:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Qghc6.11281$Sl.580164@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<20010110214928.29798.00001900@ng-cs1.aol.com> <9456ji$ad0$1@news.hitter.net> <3A71880E.4222F800@brazee.net>`

```
In article <3A71880E.4222F800@brazee.net>,
Howard Brazee  <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>
>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>from the important stuff.

Mr Brazee, this is due to a variety of reasons/excuses (I leave it to
Wiser Heads to determine the difference between the two); in my experience
the two most commonly invoked are the Saw Conundrum (the details of which
I have mentioned before) and the Training Paradox of:

'The company needs people who have these skills in order to make More
Money but it cannot train existing personnel because it not only costs
money but also because once trained the employees will go to another
company which is willing to pay them more money for these skills.'

'Ummmmmm... why not pay them more money after they're trained?'

'Because *that* will cost More Money... and besides, the fact that they
are receiving training which will allow them to make the company More
Money should be good enough for *them*!'

>It's taking time for corporations and programmers
>to learn that we need broad based skills in today's computer environment.

It is taking time for conditions to be appropriate for a snowball fight
with the Prince of Darkness, too.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infoworld: COBOL outdated and losing support

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-26T11:20:58-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A71C00A.1C6AD3E2@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<20010110214928.29798.00001900@ng-cs1.aol.com> <9456ji$ad0$1@news.hitter.net> <3A71880E.4222F800@brazee.net> <Qghc6.11281$Sl.580164@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
NA wrote:

> 'The company needs people who have these skills in order to make More
> Money but it cannot train existing personnel because it not only costs
> money but also because once trained the employees will go to another
> company which is willing to pay them more money for these skills.'

An interesting case in point is the company which is a spin off of United Airlines
and does airline reservations (Covad?  I can't remember the name, but I work with
someone who contracted there - this post is hearsay only).  They are an IDMS shop
which used to be mostly assembler, but had to move to CoBOL to get projects out more
quickly.  They can't hire assembler programmers, so they train them instead.
Because nobody else uses assembler programmers anymore, they can keep the wages
(higher than what they used to make, but ) lower than what programmers generally
make.  When they contract out for CoBOL programmers, they have to pay the prevailing
wage.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infoworld: COBOL outdated and losing support

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2001-01-26T19:04:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mTjc6.11297$Sl.581343@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<20010110214928.29798.00001900@ng-cs1.aol.com> <3A71880E.4222F800@brazee.net> <Qghc6.11281$Sl.580164@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3A71C00A.1C6AD3E2@brazee.net>`

```
In article <3A71C00A.1C6AD3E2@brazee.net>,
Howard Brazee  <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>NA wrote:
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>wage.

Hmmmmm... sounds *very* hearsay but... things may have changed.  The last
time I saw any real movement in the ASM-jockey market was (when else?) for
Y2K work and then folks were *screaming* about the 'outrageous rates' a
good BAL-coder could command.

For the past year or so, though, things may have been different... I know
they have for COBOL-coders; calendar-year 2000 was a *miserable* time, the
equivalent of 1816's 'Year Without Summer' and I saw very little hiring
going on at *any* rates.  This might have been caused, however, by shops
having blown large budgets for remediation... whatever the reason was it
kept me in the legendary Pig-Of-A-Contract (batch COBOL using - for the
most part - flat files for a municipal authority) for *far* longer than I
liked.

Things seem to be looking up a bit more this year... but it is early, yet,
and offers don't (usually) start moving until after the 3rd week in
January (which just ended).  Time will tell.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infoworld: COBOL outdated and losing support

```
In article <3A71880E.4222F800@brazee.net>,
  Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
Why not just use COBOL to script the webpages.
>
>
> Steve Dearth wrote:
>
> > This misses an important point.  I graduated from a mainframe
oriented
> > school 3 years ago.  Most of my coursework was in COBOL and
Assembler.
> > Fortunately, I was also exposed to Java, C, C++, etc.
> >
> > I found that, in an economy where e-business, the web, etc, are
considered
> > essential, I can get paid more and work on more interesting
projects by
> > working in  a language other than COBOL.  The shop I work in writes
tooling
> > that allows web access of legacy (read COBOL/mainframe)
applications and
> > data.  Although we make most of our money on the host stuff, I'm
valued for
> > my C++/Java, while my COBOL skills are seen as a weird throwback to
more
> > primitive days.
> >
> > My point is, even if schools start to teach COBOL again, students
are going
> > to resist  as long as more "modern" skills are sought by employeers.
> > Feeling like an old timer when you're 25 isn't a position most
college
> > graduates are happy to find themselves in.
>
> The difficulty is that they don't know that they will need both
skills.
>
> Part of this problem is caused by businesses which don't allow CoBOL
> programmers to also do Web projects, and who pay more for outsiders
to do Web
> programs because they can't afford to let their CoBOL programmers
have time off
> from the important stuff.   It's taking time for corporations and
programmers
> to learn that we need broad based skills in today's computer
environment.  We
> need to work with the data wherever they are, and understand the
business
> needs.   Don't get tied to the tool, associate yourself with the
business.
>
>


Sent via Deja.com
http://www.deja.com/
```

```


lcccpiasabird@my-deja.com wrote:

> In article <3A71880E.4222F800@brazee.net>,
>   Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> Why not just use COBOL to script the webpages.

Probably because management says "Let's add some web pages", and then asks
what they need.  The existing tools won't do the job (most mainfame shops
don't have PC CoBOL).  If they are aware that PC CoBOL will work, they
compare its cost with Java.  It is expensive, and this is just a pilot.
They also don't have people who know what to do, so they hire a consultant
who knows Java and HTML.  And it grows from there.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infoworld: COBOL outdated and losing support

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** rjones1005@aol.com (RJones1005)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010118224939.08973.00000152@ng-fv1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3A670070.CE9B0B6F@brazee.net>`

```
By far, on any given software project, programmer's skills is much more
important than using "the right language."

And I've seen this maxum for myself.  Competent programmers utterly failed at
developing this new system using Ada.  It was scrapped and written in COBOL. 
This of course was, until then, virtually exculsively a COBOL shop.

The attempt was somewhat like taking a native out of the jungles of Borneo,
giving him some rudimentary flight training and expecting him to fly a jet.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Maintenance: COBOL v C (was: Re: Infoworld: COBOL outdated and losing support)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-13T12:47:11-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<93q49t$2nuh$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<20010110214928.29798.00001900@ng-cs1.aol.com> <93khhs$4cu$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A5EBAF2.4F2EC39F@Azonic.co.nz> <3a5e3bbf.161458334@news>`

```
I have practical experience in maintenance of poorly written
programs done in both COBOL and C. I can tell you, from
that experience, that ease of maintenance is determined
much more by the choice of developer than the choice of
language!

A good analyst will make fewer errors of omission when
writing the requirements. Sometimes omissions are
corrected during the maintenance phase of the life cycle.
There is nothing easier to maintain than a system that
requires no changes.

A good system designer will partition a system to prevent
interdependencies. Fewer interdependencies means
easier maintenance.

A good program designer/coder will reduce coupling,
increase cohesion, use meaningful names, test, test, and
test again to assure conformance with specifications and
to remove "bugs"; all of which eases maintenance.

Choosing a language that is expressive in the application
area eases understanding which eases maintenance.

In conclusion, if everything prior to the maintenance phase
is done well, the choice of language is almost irrelevant.
If everything prior to the maintenance phase is done poorly
then choice of language may be important. A business
application that was done correctly and well in C, is
easier to maintain than a business application that was
done incorrectly and poorly in COBOL.
------------------
Rick Smith

Kevin J. Hansen wrote in message <3a5e3bbf.161458334@news>...
[...]
>When you embed C functions deeply enough, it becomes practically
>impossible for anyone - even the original developer - to figure out
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>facilitates that task.
```

```
> Cobol programmers, face it: Your favorite code is outdated and losing
support
> ...
> Since its introduction 41 years ago, Cobol has undergone three major
revisions.
> Although the updates in 1974 and 1985 were essentially published for
the
> introduction of new features, it was not until the final revision,
Cobol 97,
> that object-oriented constructs were appended to the ANSI standard.
>
> So for the throngs of you who wrote to chide me for suggesting Cobol
code was
> not object-oriented, the 41 years of legacy Cobol code in the field
did not
> magically "become" object-oriented three years ago just because the
> specification was updated.
>
> Many of you took exception when I suggested Cobol is no longer taught
in the
> majority of our nation's institutes of higher learning. You have let
me know
> that you've heard of and taken courses, you have offered lists of
universities
> with courses, and have even let me know that you have read books
about Cobol.
>
> U.S. News and World Report recently published a list of the top
colleges and
> universities in the United States. Among them were the likes of MIT
and Cal
> Tech. We polled the top 10 institutions on the list and found that in
the
> classroom Cobol is mentioned only anecdotally in context of the
history of
> programming, but not a single school currently offers coursework in
Cobol. Not
> one.
> ...
> To the many who have cited the open-source movement as the catalyst
thrusting

will be

compiler.
```

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2001-01-11T11:14:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers,alt.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_979211647@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2000/07/02
Version: 3.01
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Sunday, July 2, 2000

The COBOL FAQ has moved.  To view the most recent version, please check out the
web version at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send private
email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

The following is Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

 o Recorded new location of the FAQ
 o Updated some old (bad) web links
 o Added some new COBOL books (particularly those written or contributed to - by
frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).
 o Added information about LegacyJ (PERCobol) and changed the company name from
"Synkronix" to "LegacyJ".
 o Updated information for changes in product and company names and addresses
where available
 o Updated the URLs for downloading the draft of the next COBOL Standard and
CobCy
 o Started the Samples/Example Section
 o Tried to get rid of most specific references to Windows/NT, Windows/95,
Windows/98, and Windows/2000.
 o Added links to Xinotech
 o Added information on Norcom and GUI ScreenIO
 o Added (back) information on Wang
 o Added (back) information on the Tiny-COBOL ('74 Standard) project
```

<a id="t-3436d9f0d7"></a>
## Thread: Finding the right COBOL compiler for me. — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Finding the right COBOL compiler for me.

- **From:** "Roger J. Olfert" <rjolfert@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-13T00:32:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A5FA274.36E745F0@home.com>`

```
I'd like to post a general question to all in this newsgroup to help me
in locating what would be the correct COBOL compiler/workbench
combination for my type of work.

Iï¿½d like to develop some software for myself.  Actually, Iï¿½d like to
rewrite some programs/applications that Iï¿½ve been working on for some
years under different languages and OSï¿½s.  These programs have become
rich in features and I donï¿½t want to loose them to the new OSï¿½s that are
coming out that might be incompatible.  I also want to add more features
and Iï¿½m frankly not as proficient in VBA as I am in COBOL.

Currently, Iï¿½m running under Win98 and will be upgrading to WinME very
soon.

Iï¿½ve developed some of my programs in Visual Basic for Applications
(under Excel), and some are stand alone programs running in CA-Realizer
Basic.  The CA-Realizer Basic is the final Basic of 5 different dialects
that have me tired of re-writing so many times.  Also, Iï¿½ve had trouble
attempting to determine if this Basic has been upgraded since the Win
3.1 days or if it will be in the future.

I have significant number of yearï¿½s (30) of experience writing and
developing programs (tools) and applications (systems) for the mainframe
world.  The primary languages were COBOL and ASSEMBLER with JCL to run
the Batch environment and CICS to run the online environment.

I have only my own programs as experience when it comes to the PC
environment.  While in the mainframe world, I used the ISPF tool under
MVS (and the latest versions of the MVS world) to do the major portion
of my editing and file handling for that work, on the PC I havenï¿½t many
good tools.  I have downloaded a demo version of the SPF tool to review
for my purposes in this project.  This SPF product suggests that there
is an interface with Micro Focus Workbench and CA-Realia II Workbench.
Iï¿½m hoping that I can find another alternative to these.  CA is
notorious (in my book) for dead-ending what they own!

My needs and desires for a COBOL platform are driven by my experiences
in the mainframe environment I worked in.  I suppose that some of my
needs are based on being spoiled by the power of the mainframe (donï¿½t
jump on that) but now that PCï¿½s are becoming so powerful, Iï¿½m expecting
the same power and flexibility from the PC.  Actually, if the vendors
have a mind to, the power and flexibility would be much better than the
mainframe platform.

Iï¿½d like to have:

   * A full-featured COBOL that allows me to have a GUI interface to the
     user, even though I was used to character based interfaces in the
     mainframe workplace until I left.  The programs that Iï¿½ve written
     at home use the GUI interface technique.
   * The version of COBOL should be as recent as practically available,
     but the most important slant on that is that the vendor or package
     is not a dead-end product/company.
   * A rich set of file handling verbs and file handling features for
     sequential and indexed (etc) formats.
   * A flexible and dynamic CALL statement that resolves itself at
     execution time so that the resulting modules are not FORCED to be
     linked together at compile time.  This is a desirable.  The option
     to LINK would be desirable too.
   * A robust and solid memory management background in the runtime
     modules so that the resulting programs and applications donï¿½t run
     into problems if they get large and complicated.  I write large and
     sophisticated applications.
   * Iï¿½m not interested in OO for my purposes.  Iï¿½ve found that writing
     in separate modules that do a specific function (a derivation of
     structured programming) serves the purpose very well.
   * The resulting compiled modules would be Callable from a directory
     structure that can be implied by external variables or self
     contained text strings in the program modules.  The specification
     of the file names can be handled in the same or similar way.

Price is an issue with me too.  Iï¿½ve noticed that in scanning the
Internet for various possible solutions that COBOL is usually a very
expensive choice for a programming language.  That beginning defined as
$500 to $3,000 dollars.  This is a big number for my purposes.  I want
full features and support, but donï¿½t want to pay more than $200 for
it!   Is that totally unreasonable?

I have looked at some of the answers on the newsgroup boards and other
places, but I donï¿½t have the direct answer that Iï¿½m looking for.  I
believe that those of you that have been using the various choices
available today might be in a position to give me the answer that I
want.  Please consider my desirables in the letter above and help me
make a choice.  I donï¿½t want to make a mistake with this choice since
there is a possibility that I will invest considerable time and money in
this venture.

Thanks so much for your consideration in this matter.

Roger Olfert

rjolfert@home.com
```

<a id="t-c0ad258996"></a>
## Thread: BMS Macro Problem — 3 this month / 8 total

_Thread started [2000-12](2000-12.md#t-c0ad258996)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: BMS Macro Problem

- **From:** "mangogrower" <mangogrower@telocity.co>
- **Date:** 2001-01-13T00:19:21-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4vR76.16813$jA.3047407@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`
- **References:** `<3a3a9808.18790389@news1.sympatico.ca> <20001216193624.08937.00004486@ng-ca1.aol.com>`

```
There are two sometimes useful items:
1) GRPNAME
    is the name used to generate symbolic storage definitions and to combine
specific fields under one group  name.  The same group name must be
specified for each field that is to belong to the group.
The length of the name is up to 30 characters though you should refer to
your compiler manual to make
sure that there are no other restrictions on the length.

    If this operand is specified, the OCCURS operand cannot be specified.
    The fields in a group must follow on; there can be gaps between them,
but not other fields from
    outside  the group.  A field name must be specified for every field that
belongs to the group, and the
    POS operand  must also be specified to ensure that the fields follow
each other.  All the DFHMDF
    macros defining the fields of a group must be placed together, and in
the correct order (ascending
    numeric order of the POS value).

    For example, the first 20 columns of the first six lines of a map can be
defined as a group of six fields,
as long as the remaining columns on the first five lines are not defined as
fields.

    The ATTRB operand specified on the first field of the group applies to
all of the fields within the group.

and 2)
OCCURS
    specifies that the indicated number of entries for the field are to be
generated in a map, and that the
   map definition is to be generated in such a way that the fields are
addressable as entries in a matrix or
   an array.  This permits several data fields to be addressed by the same
name (subscripted) without
   generating a unique name for each field.  OCCURS and GRPNAME are mutually
exclusive; that is,
   OCCURS cannot be used when fields have been defined under a group name.
If this operand is
   omitted, a value of  OCCURS=1 is assumed.


YukonMama <yukonmama@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20001216193624.08937.00004486@ng-ca1.aol.com...
> Like Liam I end up defining each field in the map and do a redefines of
the
> resulting copybook (sigh).
>
> I'm told there is a 'group' code for BMS code that is supposed to give you
an
> occurance but I've never gotten it to work.
>
> I suggest you either look up the documentation for BMS (available on IBM's
site
> www.ibm.s390.com - I think that's the URL - don't have it bookmarked here)
or
> lookup the archives of CICS-L and check for any discussion of this
situation
> there as I'm sure it's been brought up before.
>
> Eileen
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: BMS Macro Problem

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2001-01-13T15:35:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010113103554.13189.00001156@ng-ce1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<4vR76.16813$jA.3047407@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`

```
>From: "mangogrower" mangogrower@telocity.co 
>Date: 1/13/01 12:19 AM Eastern 

>There are two sometimes useful items:
>1) GRPNAME
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yes - I went into work afterward and looked these up.  I don't think either
will work for the original question (which I hope I'm remembering correctly
here)....

GRPNAME will be good with something like a date.  A GRPNAME of INVOICE-DATE
followed by the MM, DD, CCYY fields will allow editing of each individual piece
with the attribute assigned to INVOICE-DATE.

However I think the complaint was with the situation of having a line on a
screen consisting of several different fields repeated multiple times like:

part number, quantity, price
part number, quantity, price
etc

the OCCURS cannot be used in this instance because all the fields in the OCCURS
must be the same.  If you needed 25 part numbers in contiguous sequence then
OCCURS can be used. However you cannot do an OCCURS on the set of 3 different
fields.  

Since GRPNAME and OCCURS are mutually exclusive you are stuck coding each field
separately.
```

- **From:** "mark" <mark2741@NOSPAMhome.com>

- **Message-ID:** `<cA686.135384$w35.23129865@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com>`

```
I am a trainer by profession, and recently began a position with a new
company that, down the road, it would behoove me to have a basic (if not
advanced) understanding of COBOL and RPG so that I could teach the systems
classes. Our clients are on many different platforms, such as CICS, AS/400,
etc.

I have asked around at the office and many people say that the Sams book,
Learn Cobol in 24 Hours, is very good. Any opinions on this? I am a complete
novice when it comes to programming (minus the basic programming I fiddled
with on my commodore 64 back in 1984!).
```

- **From:** "Roger J. Olfert" <rjolfert@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-18T05:30:16+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<3A667F67.8509B1D0@home.com>`
- **References:** `<cA686.135384$w35.23129865@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com> <nh236tguunsuqj5ja4loi65g5fve9katg1@4ax.com> <8Dk86.136132$w35.23523353@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com> <3a63cfd7.54656518@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
I guess that I'm getting into this conversation in the middle (sort of) because
of you answered.

I was interested in getting your book and wanting to know which version of the
COBOL compiler and which version of the SP2 tools are coming with the current
book on the shelf at Amazon?

I've read some or most of the conversation above and below this point and believe
that Mark got more
perspectives on the original question then he intended.  Some of them really went
of on a tangent.

I personally wrote a question to the newsgroup labeled "Finding the right COBOL
compiler for me" on Fri at 04:32 which is above this point in the sequence.

If you happen to read that letter, you'll see that I'm experienced in the
mainframe environment, but I need to get a COBOL compiler that will meet my needs
in the PC world.

I have my own personal view on teaching and the background necessary to do so
adequately.  I've found that I'm only satisfied with my own teachings to tough
issues when I have personally attempted several answers to that issue.  I could
then speak with confidence as to WHY something works better this way and the best
that way.

I had the luck to start my learning at a time and a place where help was
available and self help was encouraged.  This was beneficial because I learned
the reason why something works.  Over the years I ran into several good people,
but I was disappointed at the number of people that didn't have any concern for
reasons why something works for the short term, but a better way will work
longer.  That means with more stability and ease of maintenance and upgrading.

It had gotten to the point that politics and the superstructure of management
outweighed the concern for doing a good job and getting work done so I had to get
out of there.  This was sad because I loved the work and liked to work with other
people that wanted to accomplish meaningful projects for the outfit.


Back to the question,  do you know which versions of the products come with the
book now?

Thanks:

Roger
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: learning cobol

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-01-21T11:28:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3a6ac6cf.48876622@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<cA686.135384$w35.23129865@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com> <nh236tguunsuqj5ja4loi65g5fve9katg1@4ax.com> <8Dk86.136132$w35.23523353@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com> <3a63cfd7.54656518@news1.attglobal.net> <3A667F67.8509B1D0@home.com>`

```
Hello Roger,

We are shipping with v3 of Fujitsu COBOL - includng manuals etc, and
3.5 of Sp2.

Presently Fujitsu is shipping with v5 and Sp2 is at 4.0.  The new sp2
can be dowloaded (eval version) from the Flexus Web Site:
http://www.flexus.com.

My aim in including a free compiler with the book was to give the
students immediate feedback, in that they can code and test their
programs.  I do not market the book as a way to get a free compiler
for production use.  While v3 is a good product, v5 is so much better
that you are not giving Fujitsu a fair evaluation is you base that
evaluation on the v3 product.

You can get a 30 day eval of their latest just by asking at
http://www.adtools.com.  The sp2 Evals (and v4 is again much improved
over v3.5 there) are for 60 days.
```

#### ↳ Re: learning cobol

- **From:** "TJ Dombrowski" <keldin@earthlink.not>
- **Date:** 2001-01-15T02:22:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
The best books on COBOL and JCL are
by Gary Deward Brown and are both available from amazon.com

http://www.amazon.com/

Advanced COBOL for Structured and Object-Oriented Programming, 3rdEdition
by Gary Deward Brown
ISBN: 0471314811

and

System 390 Job Control Language, 4th Edition
by Gary Deward Brown
ISBN: 0471283096

Using the mainframes everyday at work these two books are essential.
Both books are $54 each but besides the IBM reference manuals are the most
used books in my
mainframe library.   They are both worth their wieght in gold, and will be
used many times
by the owner of them.

BTW the IBM link for www.s390.ibm.com fowards to the Zserver page since IBM
has renamed
os390 computers Zservers the link probably won't work for ever.
Most IBM manuals for the mainframe are in pdf format under the Library link
on the left,
but they keep moving stuff around on the website.  It gets harder to find by
the year.

Have fun
TJ
```

#### ↳ Re: learning cobol

- **From:** atharmon@swbell.net (Tony Harmon)
- **Date:** 2001-01-15T16:48:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3a63287a.236278009@news.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<cA686.135384$w35.23129865@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com>`

```

Personally, given the environment you descripe I would recommend
(though they may not still be in print. aka "Old School" Stuff) the
following:

Structured COBOL 74/85
Fundamentals and Style
By Welburn and Price
0-07-836489-2

Advanced Structured COBOL
Batch, On-Line, and Database Concepts
by Welburn
0-87484-558-0

Comprehensive Structured COBOL
by Bradley
0-07-836549-x

Modern COBOL Programming
1985 Standard with Microcomputers.

I personally think these books have better fundamentals and style then
the newer books, but that's just me. I think the newer books bypass
things such as PACKED-DECIMAL, aka COMP-3, and things like that and
are more into DISPLAY, and the gui extenstions...

…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: learning cobol

- **From:** rjones1005@aol.com (RJones1005)
- **Date:** 2001-01-19T04:14:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010118231404.08973.00000165@ng-fv1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3a63287a.236278009@news.swbell.net>`

```
>>I have asked around at the office and many people say that the Sams book,
>>Learn Cobol in 24 Hours, is very good. Any opinions on this? I am a complete
>>novice when it comes to programming (minus the basic programming I fiddled
>>with on my commodore 64 back in 1984!).

I think "Structured COBOL Programming" by Stein & Stein is quite good.  It's
designed as a text book - careful and complete explanations and lots of
exercises.  It does not cover the complete COBOL language but there's enought
in there anyway.

One thing I really liked about the book is how the authors contrasted COBOL 74
and COBOL 85 style of programming.  COBOL 85 gave us lots of changes that
fundamentally (allows you to) change(s) the style of programming.

"COBOL Unleashed" is a complete presentation of the COBOL language.

When I was re-learning COBOL after not having written a line of it in 20 years,
I found the Stein & Stein book easy to read and quick to learn from.  The
Unleashed book then became an invaluable reference once on the job.
```

#### ↳ Re: learning cobol

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-16T12:36:38+00:00

```
In article <cA686.135384$w35.23129865@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com>,
  "mark" <mark2741@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote:
> I am a trainer by profession, and recently began a position with a new
> company that, down the road, it would behoove me to have a basic (if
not advanced) understanding of COBOL and RPG so that I could teach the
systems classes. Our clients are on many different platforms, such as
CICS, AS/400, etc.

I am a complete novice when it comes to programming (minus the basic
programming I fiddled with on my commodore 64 back in 1984!).

Hi:

Personally, If I found out that my trainer's sole qualification
to teach me COBOL was based on reading a book which I could buy
myself (and get the compiler and SP2), I would be pretty annoyed.

I would also think that any organization who hired a 'trainer'
to teach COBOL programming who's sole qualification was reading
a book would be in need of severe criticism.

No wonder we get posts from such as Deek who are apparently being
taught by 'trainers' who seem to know nothing.

Surely, COBOL programming should be taught only by people who
know it VERY WELL, but also have experience in the harsh, real
world and on the platform(s) for which they teach.

I am also surprised that I am the only person (apparently) who
finds a problem here. . .

(This is not intended to be critical of author Mark, but of the
system in general, which, as we all know so well, is just awful.)

Thanks

Tony Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com
http://www.deja.com/
```

```
Asimov may have been "certifiable" but that's not the same as being
certified to teach in the public school (k-12) system.  Actually, very
few instructors/lecturers/professors at the college/university level
have the licensure necessary to teach in the public schools.

- Ed Stevens
```

- **From:** "mark" <mark2741@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-18T02:50:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8Ss96.146546$w35.25716160@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com>`
- **References:** `<cA686.135384$w35.23129865@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com> <941f8l$fuq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3a644b4c$0$227@hades.is.co.za> <942njl$2pf4$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com> <Io796.4121$KE.216129@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <CQ796.4214$KE.226581@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <vV796.144118$w35.25207832@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com> <944ao3$skk$1@news.igs.net>`

```
I've trained many "doers" in my day I can assure you. Unfortunately,
teaching/training is one of the few professions out there that absolutely
everyone in the world thinks they are qualified to do, mostly because they
have seen so much of it during their lives as students starting at a young
age. But to compare the garbage that is construed as "teaching" in 90% of
public school classrooms, and 99.999% of college classrooms to the
technologies and techniques that a professional trainer of adult workers is
mandated to have (or else he/she won't last long) is absurd.

I can see many in this NG haven't had much quality training (hence the
negative comments). : )
```

```
"TJ Dombrowski" <keldin@earthlink.not

> > Shame on you. You got me started.
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > that is competent, by law, to teach in the public schools of my
state.
> >
> > (or at least that's what my research showed a few years ago)
>
> So you believe that Isaac Asimov and Arthur C Clark are not
qualified to
> teach...
> Very strange that anyone would believe that crap you just posted
above

I do say that Asimov (were he alive) and Clark are not qualified to
teach in the public schools of my state (and probably most others).

I agree it's crap. You better believe it, though, because it is also
the law. Violators are subject to severe monetary fines and
incarceration.

It's worse than you can imagine. In my field, mathematics, it is
possible to be 'certified' to teach mathematics in the secondary
schools without ever having had a college course in calculus. You see,
you have to have only 18 semester hours of advanced hours to obtain
certification and you can get those six courses from obliging colleges
by taking 'History of Mathematics,' 'Elementary Number Theory,'
'Advanced Geometry,' etc.

A recent study by our State Board of Education found that 35% of
high-school biology teachers believed humans and dinosaurs were
contemporaneous.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: learning cobol

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-01-19T00:52:39+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<946vhl$iv7$2@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<cA686.135384$w35.23129865@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com> <941f8l$fuq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3a644b4c$0$227@hades.is.co.za> <942njl$2pf4$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com> <Io796.4121$KE.216129@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <CQ796.4214$KE.226581@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <46r96.427$Tw1.36740@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```

Jerry P wrote in message
<46r96.427$Tw1.36740@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>...

>
>A recent study by our State Board of Education found that 35% of
>high-school biology teachers believed humans and dinosaurs were
>contemporaneous.
>
Hahaha!

What do you expect from a culture which doesn't read books and is educated
by Hollywood... (I'm not bashing the USA here;  sadly, our's is just the
same...<G>)

I blame Raquel Welch for running round in a fur bikini and being rescued
from dinosaurs in "50 Million Years B.C.". (Yes...I know...still a lovely
image after all these years...<G>)
(They even got the dates wrong; by 50 million years B.C. dinosaurs had been
extinct for around 15,000,000 years...)
But the lovely Miss Welch looked so good and the film made such an
impression, that it spawned a whole genre of "cave man"  B movies, where the
tension could only be heightened by a couple of dinosaurs tearing each other
up as a cave man tried to escape...

The latest (and infinitely slicker) members of this genre, Jurassic Park (1
and 2), DID  try to explain how it was possible for dinosaurs and people to
exist at the same time, but Michael Crichton's ingenious (if unlikely)
explanation would've been lost on many of the audience who just wanted to
see the Raptors hunting people and T. Rex ripping the place up... (Actually,
it is unfair to include movies like the latest attempt at Godzilla, and
Jurassic Park(s), in the category of B movies; patently they are not.)

So, we all enjoy a good time at the movies and gasp at the (computer
generated) realism of SFX, but the result is that 35% of Biology Teachers
are affected by it to the point where they can't tell reality from fantasy.

Mind you, there's probably 35% who believe the World was made in 6 Days...

Wonder if it's the same 35%?

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: learning cobol

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2001-01-16T14:26:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010116092632.20125.00000279@ng-ft1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<941f8l$fuq$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Tony Dilworth writes ...

[snip]

>Personally, If I found out that my trainer's sole qualification
>to teach me COBOL was based on reading a book which I could buy
>myself (and get the compiler and SP2), I would be pretty annoyed.
>

Right on.


>I would also think that any organization who hired a 'trainer'
>to teach COBOL programming who's sole qualification was reading
>a book would be in need of severe criticism.


Agreed. But he didn't say he was to be a COBOL trainer, just a trainer who had
to have some knowledge of "COBOL and RPG so that I could teach the systems
classes." It's hard to read exactly what would be in such a "systems class". In
my mind, "systems classes" are about operating systems and they would likely
not even mention COBOL nor RPG. So to have even some knowledge of how a system
is used, to give the systems staff some idea of the applications world, is not
a bad thing.


>
>No wonder we get posts from such as Deek who are apparently being
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>world and on the platform(s) for which they teach.

Absolutely. If teaching COBOL is the objective.

>
>I am also surprised that I am the only person (apparently) who
>finds a problem here. . .
>

Yes, well, I was concerned when a prospective teacher of systems classes says,
"I am a complete novice when it comes to programming". I mean we all have to
start somewhere, but getting your start in the field as a trainer of systems
classes seems to be pushing it a bit. But, since he obviously isn't going to
sign up for one of our courses in any event I had decided to remain silent on
the issue.

Regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: learning cobol

- **From:** deek40@aol.com (Deek40)
- **Date:** 2001-01-16T16:19:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010116111901.07823.00000677@ng-bd1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<941f8l$fuq$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
:)  Sorry I have to be set an example of, my professor was good at programming,
but terrible at explaining what terms meant and how to fix errors.  I have been
teaching myself out of Sam 21 days and Sams24 hrs, I ended up with a B for the
semester, I am taking the second half of the class this semester.

BTW, my old prof.  Was a part-time prof, my new one is full time, i hope this
helps me out.  

But I agree someone self taught out of a few books will not be able to teach
others.  After reading throuhg Thane's book I have a better understanding of
COBOL, but I wouldnt dare try to teach someone else.  I would say reading the
books has helped me out in general, but what has helped me the most other than
the people in this newsgroup would be practice practice practice,  OVer winter
break here I have done COBOL COBOL COBOL, I have worked for hours trying to fix
something that I couldnt figure out.  I think the best learning tool is
determination.  MAny of you remember how discouraged I was well I was
determined to figure this stuff out and I am doing better now and plan on to
continue my study of COBOL outside of college.
```

```
> others.  After reading throuhg Thane's book I have a better understanding
of
> COBOL, but I wouldnt dare try to teach someone else.  I would say reading
the
> books has helped me out in general, but what has helped me the most other
than
> the people in this newsgroup would be practice practice practice,  OVer
winter
> break here I have done COBOL COBOL COBOL, I have worked for hours trying
to fix
> something that I couldnt figure out.  I think the best learning tool is
> determination.  MAny of you remember how discouraged I was well I was
> determined to figure this stuff out and I am doing better now and plan on
to
> continue my study of COBOL outside of college.
```

```
Hey dope,

I have not, nor have I ever stated, that I am a COBOL trainer. However,
since the software that I have been hired to train people on is written in
COBOL and we do discuss (at a very high level) the LINK area that is
required in order for the host system to interface properly with our system.
I don't have to understand a thing about COBOL to teach a productive, useful
class. But I would like to understand it for myself, at a more detailed
level so that I can improve in my job.

Now that you've been further informed, please take the foot out of your
mouth....or would you like some salt with that leather?

"Foodman" <foodman123@aol.com> wrote in message
news:941f8l$fuq$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
```

```
BTW,  you can't just make a statement and claim it is an "adage". (even if

called a dope.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: learning cobol

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2001-01-18T11:29:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PsA96.9876$Sl.514625@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<cA686.135384$w35.23129865@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com> <943v61$dlm$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <Lph96.9755$Sl.508623@iad-read.news.verio.net> <945fu6$g96$2@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`

```
In article <945fu6$g96$2@hermes.enternet.co.nz>,
Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
>NA wrote in message ...
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>Maybe you could elucidate?

Quite simple... when one attempted to justify one's actions by the actions
of another (X) or others (especially, for the Young At Mind, the popular
'But *everyone else* is (y)!') to my Sainted Paternal Grandmother - may
she sleep with the angels! - or other women of similar background an
oft-heard rejoinder was 'So?  (X)/'everyone else' is jumping off the
Brooklyn Bridge, *you* will, too?'

>
>BTW,  you can't just make a statement and claim it is an "adage". (even if
>it is pithy and sounds wise...).

As my memory - admittedly porous - has it the statement (or one similar
thereunto) was made by Robert A. Heinlein in one of his many books... but
I cannot recall which one.  According to http://www.m-w.com an adage is 'a
saying often in metaphorical form that embodies a common observation'...
what discounts my statement from this?

>
>And I wasn't "inciting to riot". Foodman was out of line and deserved to be
>called a dope.
>Nothing inflammatory here. Simple statement of fact.

See above about 'inciting to riot' and (keeping in mind metaphor) try
substituting something appropriately... dopey.

>
>Anyway, what do you suppose gives you the right to decide who gets taken
>seriously here? <no, that's a joke...it's irony...put that cudgel
>down...I'll tell Snow White....Gerroff!!!)

This is strudel, not a kugel... care for a bite?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: learning cobol

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-01-19T01:22:44+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<946vhm$iv7$3@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<cA686.135384$w35.23129865@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com> <943v61$dlm$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <Lph96.9755$Sl.508623@iad-read.news.verio.net> <945fu6$g96$2@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <PsA96.9876$Sl.514625@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```

NA wrote in message ...
(regarding "Brooklyn Bridge defense"...)
>
>Quite simple... when one attempted to justify one's actions by the actions
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Ah, the memories that conjured up...!

I could actually hear my Mother (now, sadly deceased) saying in her gentle
Kiwi drawl:

"So, if X put his head in an oven, you would too?"

(The Kiwi variation on the Brooklyn Bridge is the "head in an oven"
defence).

The best defense I could muster at the time was:

"Well, I'd certainly want to look in the oven and see what made him put his
head in it..."

At which point my Mother would give me one of those "I am your Mother and
enjoy the protection of your Father, who is not to be trifled with; do not
proceed with this..." looks, and I would move to another room feeling
suitably chastened...

I tried it on my Dad once. (I vaguely remember my Mother shouting "Not the
head! Hit him on the legs!") but it was too late...

(I grew up in an era when parents were still allowed to correct their
children. I was never beaten as a child and adored both my parents, but I
still remember the odd clip around the ear from my Dad. Come to think of it,
I guess that was the whole object of the exercise...<G>)

Thanks for that, Doc. I learned something. And my ear doesn't hurt...

>As my memory - admittedly porous - has it the statement (or one similar
>thereunto) was made by Robert A. Heinlein in one of his many books... but
>I cannot recall which one.  According to http://www.m-w.com an adage is 'a
>saying often in metaphorical form that embodies a common observation'...
>what discounts my statement from this?
<<<
Tsk! Tsk! The "Argument from Authority" (Heinlein, no less...) and you can't
provide the source?!

OK, I'll withdraw my objection to "adage", given the definition you quoted,
and the fact that you mention the Holy Heinlein. Agree, your statement
conforms <G>.

>>>
>
>This is strudel, not a kugel... care for a bite?
<<<
It's a wise man who knows his strudel from his kugel...<G>

Thanks. I'll certainly take up your generous offer.

Pete.


>DD
>
```

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>

…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>defence).

A regional variation, it seems... but in similar spirit.

>
>The best defense I could muster at the time was:
>
>"Well, I'd certainly want to look in the oven and see what made him put his
>head in it..."

Gas-range or woodstove?

>
>At which point my Mother would give me one of those "I am your Mother and
>enjoy the protection of your Father, who is not to be trifled with; do not
>proceed with this..." looks, and I would move to another room feeling
>suitably chastened...

Curious names for household staff you seem to have down there... was Ms
Chastened an upstairs or downstairs maid?

[snippage about disciplinary styles]

>Thanks for that, Doc. I learned something. And my ear doesn't hurt...

Well, then, let me tell you a *fine* old story, about when were were
sailin' the ol' South China Seas in a great blue nor'east sou'westerly
heartily hale mid-winter gale... *that* oughta bend your ear somethin'
fierce!

>
>>As my memory - admittedly porous - has it the statement (or one similar
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>provide the source?!

Not an Argument from Authority (which is, of course, one of the weakest
sort... and this *must* be true because Augustine said that Boetius said
so!); just a belated - and incomplete - attempt to cite a source.

>
>OK, I'll withdraw my objection to "adage", given the definition you quoted,
>and the fact that you mention the Holy Heinlein. Agree, your statement
>conforms <G>.

Most gracious of you... now, about that midwinter gale... th' Captain - a
*fine* figger of a fellow, the likes o' which ain't been seen 'round these
parts since... well, since I was sailin' the ol' South China Seas in a
great blue nor'east sou'westerly heartily hale mid-winter gale... well,
maybe a little bit afterwards, I remember one time when I was a-wand'rin'
'long a queerly quaint quay in Queensland... heh, *that'll* show ya how
long ago it was, wasn't even Queensland then, it was still Princessland...
anyhow, the sun was bright, the sky was blue... y'know, ya don't see many
days like that anymore but back then they was as common as shells on the
beach... an' speakin' of shells, I recall one particular day - this was
just after that day I was tellin' ya about, when I was a-wand'rin' 'long a
queerly quaint quay in Queensland... you know, away back when, when it was
still Princessland...

... how's the ear *now*, van Gogh?

>
>>>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks. I'll certainly take up your generous offer.

All right... on which leg?

DD
```

- **References:** `<cA686.135384$w35.23129865@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com> <941f8l$fuq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <_1696.143797$w35.25133646@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com> <E7796.9726$Sl.504932@iad-read.news.verio.net> <943v61$dlm$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`

```
Fortunately, or unfortunately depending on how you look at it, a programming
background is not necessary to teach the implementation course in question.
At least, after observing a live class that went fairly well, I know that
the instructor of that class does not have a programming background. The
students attending the class that *are* programmers (most are usually
project managers) seem to already know what to do, but just need to be
pointed towards the right information and to understand the system from a
user's standpoint.

All of the questions that are asked by the programmers are not about COBOL,
per se, but about the type of information that needs to be passed. Exactly
WHAT they must do (meaning code) to pass the information, it is assumed,
they are supposed to know prior to coming to the class. I just want to know
enough so that I am more familiar with the terminology that is usually
discussed during the class. Right now I just teach (actually, will be
teaching beginning next week) the user classes.

"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:943v61$dlm$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz...
>
> > Aside to Mark:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
>>>
>Fortunately, or unfortunately depending on how you look at it, a
programming
>background is not necessary to teach the implementation course in question.
<<<
It certainly has to help. When they start talking about data formats, are
you facile with what a double byte integer is, the meaning of a COBOL
PICTURE and any USAGE clauses attached to it?

If the answer is "No, and I don't need to be..." then should we conclude
that  your interest in COBOL is simply a passing one for your own
edification? I got the impression from your previous post that it was more
than that. (Not that there's anything "wrong" with a passing interest...)

>At least, after observing a live class that went fairly well, I know that
>the instructor of that class does not have a programming background. The
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Agreed that the Implementation can be handled from a User Standpoint with a
minimum of Technical content, but I doubt that the same can be said for the
data flow analysis.

>>>
>All of the questions that are asked by the programmers are not about COBOL,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>discussed during the class.
<<<

Understood. Your primary interest is probably in looking at the way in which
Programmers describe data so you can understand them, and, in turn, couch
things in the terminology they are most comfortable with. Studying the COBOL
Data Division will definitely help here.

>>>
Right now I just teach (actually, will be teaching beginning next week) the
user classes.
<<<
Ah, so you have no immediate or essential requirement for technical
background? OK, I think it's clear now...<G>

Pete.
```

- **Date:** 2001-01-19T02:38:06+00:00

- **References:** `<cA686.135384$w35.23129865@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com> <941f8l$fuq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <_1696.143797$w35.25133646@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com> <E7796.9726$Sl.504932@iad-read.news.verio.net> <943v61$dlm$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <41t96.146551$w35.25724486@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com> <946vhj$iv7$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`

```
Correct, It is not essential to understand COBOL to teach the implementation
classes (other than knowing what a few terms mean, such as 'copybook', and a
few others (we supply copybooks for the programmers to use)).

However, I quickly noticed after starting at my new job, that everyone
sitting around me (support reps, implementation people, not developers) has
either the Sams' book or some other texts on COBOL, so I figured I'd better
as well.

Believe it or not, after only working through the first couple of "hours" of
Thane's book, it has proven useful to my work. I was just bestowed ownership
of a couple of training manuals (responsible for working with the tech
writer to make changes/updates to the info). Soon afterward I was handed a
list of changes that were had been incorporated into the latest revision of
the software. The first change was a bug fix in the COBOL version of the
program, and wouldn't you know it....they listed the line of code right on
the bug report and it was just a simple PIC definition line that was screwed
up. I didn't understand the entire line of code, but I got the basic jist,
which was pretty cool (and it impressed my non-programmer co-workers as well
: ) )

Now, I didn't need to know how to decipher the code, as accompanying it was
an explanation. But sometimes the exaplanations are not very clear, so I
think it would benefit me to learn COBOL.

Thanks for the response!

mark
```
