###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: learning cobol

- **From:** "mark" <mark2741@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-17T02:35:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Tx796.144106$w35.25193431@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com>`
- **References:** `<cA686.135384$w35.23129865@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com> <941m3e$lr0$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <S5696.143824$w35.25136339@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com>`

```
To clarify what I meant by "systems" class in my original post:

We have a class that is targeted towards System Implementors. To implement
our software, it's not just a matter of installing it and let it run. There
is more to it. For example, for many of our customers, a link area must be
built between their homegrown host system and our software in order for the
proper information to be passed between the two systems.

"System Implementors" can and do include the following: Project Managers,
Team Leaders, and Programmers. We supply copybooks and other aids to the
system implementors/programmers to aid them in building the link interface,
so the System Implementors class primarily deals with going over the types
of information that must be passed, in addition to optional information that
can be passed between the systems. It is not a programming class, nor is it
about programming. If I had to define it in its simplest terms, it would be
a class in which all of the different fields of data that should be passed
from one system to the other and vice-versa are defined and discussed (by
defined I mean, we go over the listings with them from the programmer's
reference manual). This is all after the usage of the program itself is
covered.

I have not taught this class, and am not expected to for quite some time
(about 6 months to a year from now minimum). My feeling after sitting in on
the class was that a basic grasp of the terminology used (hence COBOL, as
the program is written for both COBOL and RPG III platforms but we mainly
discuss the COBOL version) would greatly enhance my understanding of the
material.

I assure you that the people that currently teach the class have no
experience in COBOL programming, nor do they imply that they are
programmers. Implementation and programming are two different things, in
this case the best comparison would be the overall project management in
comparison to the coding for the project. They do a good job, with the proof
being the results of hundreds of properly implemented systems upon
completing this 2-day course.

I apologize if my remarks misled (or in the case of "the dope" remark,
offended) anyone, however when one's professional qualifications are
questioned one tends to get a little edgy : )

For those of you that gave me book recommendations here's an update: over
this past weekend I purchased the Sams' Cobol in 24 hours book, by Thane
Hubbel. Hours 1 & 2 were simple enough, but Hour 3 is gonna require a reread
on my part as I am a tad lost...but I'm getting there.

Thanks for your help, and I hope to be able to use this newsgroup as a
springboard for questions & answers as I continue to struggle learning
COBOL.

#### ↳ Re: learning cobol

- **From:** bgwillia@vcu.edu (Boyce Williams)
- **Date:** 2001-01-17T14:23:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3a65a0ce.2699371@mercury.vcu.edu>`
- **References:** `<cA686.135384$w35.23129865@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com>`

```
One thing you can do is to sit in on your fellow instructor's classes
on the subject.  That way you can not only get the knowledge yourself,
but also your school's teaching methodology.

Failing that, attend night school on COBOL at your local University or
Community College.  The one thing formal courses provide that a "teach
yourself book" can't is the human interface.  Your angle will be
different then most students, as you're focusing on the teaching
method as well as the subject.  CICS and RPG classes are a little
harder to obtain at the higher institutions of learning, so you may
have to get that knowledge from the "road show" seminar courses like
Learning Tree, Data Directions, IBM (good, but very expensive), etc.

In any case experience is needed so your students can have confindence
in your experience and you.  So practice writing, compiling,
debugging, and the testing many programs so you can experience first
hand: SOC4's, SOC7's, ASRA's and lost TS queues (if planning to teach
CICS sometime in the future), run-away "Wild Weasel" code, spaghetti
programs and other sort of malfeasance and malfunctions one tends to
get oneself into and need to work back out in the course of getting a
program operational.  One of the critical knowledge factors your
students will depend on you for is your ablliity and knowledge to
teach them problem-solving skills in the language.

Well, that's my two cents - adjusted for inflation.  Good luck.

---Boyce--

On Sun, 14 Jan 2001 00:40:40 GMT, "mark" <mark2741@NOSPAMhome.com>
wrote:

>I am a trainer by profession, and recently began a position with a new
>company that, down the road, it would behoove me to have a basic (if not
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>with on my commodore 64 back in 1984!).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: learning cobol

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-17T08:58:08-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A65C110.1881986C@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<cA686.135384$w35.23129865@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com> <3a65a0ce.2699371@mercury.vcu.edu>`

```
Boyce Williams wrote:

> In any case experience is needed so your students can have confindence
> in your experience and you.  So practice writing, compiling,
> debugging, and the testing many programs so you can experience first
> hand: SOC4's, SOC7's...

I remember one time when someone was giving a class in dump reading.  He set up a
program with a runaway table (referencing data outside of the table range).  To
his delight, he got a SOC7 (data exception), instead of the error he expected.
Figure out common beginner errors and try them.   See what happens when you
finish an internal sort and then drop through to the input procedure a second
time.   Go outside of table bounds.   Try different formats and moves for dates
(numeric, group, and alphanumeric pictures).   Vary your record size from what
the Cobol expects.  Vary the record format (FBA).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: learning cobol

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-18T14:30:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<M5D96.418$Nj6.47664@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<cA686.135384$w35.23129865@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com> <3a65a0ce.2699371@mercury.vcu.edu> <3A65C110.1881986C@brazee.net>`

```
Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:3A65C110.1881986C@brazee.net...
> Boyce Williams wrote:
>
> I remember one time when someone was giving a class in dump reading.  He
set up a
> program with a runaway table (referencing data outside of the table
range).  To
> his delight, he got a SOC7 (data exception), instead of the error he
expected.

So what did he expect?

A bounds violation on a table can give you at least four different types of
errors/abends:

If you overrun the table into your own code segment, good chance for a
(S0C1?) "Invalid opcode" abend.

If you overrun your table into memory your application does not own, count
on a (S0C4?) "protection exception" ("unavailable storage") abend.

If you overrun a table but stay in memory your application does own, you can
corrupt other program data and get the aforementioned S0C7 "data exception"
abend.

And, you can overrun your table into memory owned by your application,
corrupt your data, not suffer a S0C7 data exception, but gee whiz can you
get some weird output!

... Which is why every mainframe COBOL programmer should learn how to
compile a program which uses tables using the SSRANGE compiler option when
the program needs debugging. (And how to compile it back for production with
NOSSRANGE, and why you don't use SSRANGE on production code).
```

### runtime error

- **From:** noah eltzroth <calble@sigecom.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-15T08:17:32-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A63067C.1D1E8F33@sigecom.net>`

```
I use fujitsu cobol 3.0 as my compiler and when I go to run a linked and
compiled program it says that f3biprct.dll is missing and the program
can not run what should I do?
        Noah
```

#### ↳ Re: runtime error

- **From:** "Brad Prothero" <brad.prothero@clarica.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-15T11:26:08-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VpG86.213$t6.15035@news.corpcomm.net>`
- **References:** `<3A63067C.1D1E8F33@sigecom.net>`

```
I believe that the application can not find the runtime. Make sure that the
directory of the runtime is in the path for the application.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: runtime error

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2001-01-16T00:45:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010115194540.17535.00000641@nso-fy.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<VpG86.213$t6.15035@news.corpcomm.net>`

```
In article <VpG86.213$t6.15035@news.corpcomm.net>, "Brad Prothero"
<brad.prothero@clarica.com> writes:

>I believe that the application can not find the runtime. Make sure that the
>directory of the runtime is in the path for the application.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Also check to be sure that the PATH does not include any 
LongFileNames with embedded spaces.  IIRC, vs 3.0 and 4.0 
would not play nice when dealing with directories/files with
embedded spaces.
```

#### ↳ Re: runtime error

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-01-16T04:39:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3a63d040.54761915@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<3A63067C.1D1E8F33@sigecom.net>`

```
I have conversed with Noah and he has the problem solved.  BTW, this
was under Windows ME which does "strange" things with the Autoexec.bat
and config.sys - and the 3.0 version requires a path change in
autoexec.bat.

## Thread: InkJet Printer Support anyone? — 9 msgs

### InkJet Printer Support anyone?

- **From:** "Sam" <Music98@Mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-15T19:47:34-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<940997$shg$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Hi All;

I'm working with Micro Focus 3.1 and would like to use my Lexmark InkJet to
produce reports.  So far I've been unsuccessful in getting the printer's
control program to process the output from the PC.

Anyone know the control-codes/escape-sequences necessary to overcome this?

--Sam

__________________________________________________________________________
Stop USFSPA Abuse!  Sign a petition at:

http://www.e-thepeople.com/affiliates/messengerinquirer/index.cfm?PC=PETFV1&
PETID=482614

'Till Death Do Us Part should not apply "ONLY" to Military Retirement Pay!
```

#### ↳ Re: InkJet Printer Support anyone?

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2001-01-16T02:07:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-Kp3KvSs2i9qI@tcpserver>`
- **References:** `<940997$shg$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On Tue, 16 Jan 2001 01:47:34, "Sam" <Music98@Mindspring.com> wrote:

> Hi All;
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> --Sam

You probably have to use the PC_PRINTER_xxxx routines to use the 
Inkjet printers. A lot of the inkjet printers are "mindless 
win-printers" and must be accessed through the printer driver supplied
by the manufacturer. There is no "printer command language" like PCL 
or Postscript for these things, they depend on the Windows print 
driver to rasterize the output and direct the bits to the printer.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: InkJet Printer Support anyone?

- **From:** Calvin Crumrine <Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us>
- **Date:** 2001-01-16T07:51:38-09:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A647C19.AB2474B1@dced.state.ak.us>`
- **References:** `<940997$shg$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-Kp3KvSs2i9qI@tcpserver>`

```
lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca wrote:

> On Tue, 16 Jan 2001 01:47:34, "Sam" <Music98@Mindspring.com> wrote:
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Lorne Sunley

Lexmarks might be "mindless win-printers" but I've not found this true of other
inkjets. Are you sure you aren't letting one brand prejudice you against an
entire technology?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: InkJet Printer Support anyone?

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2001-01-16T19:53:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-dddZdMKj93H4@tcpserver>`
- **References:** `<940997$shg$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-Kp3KvSs2i9qI@tcpserver> <3A647C19.AB2474B1@dced.state.ak.us>`

```
On Tue, 16 Jan 2001 16:51:38, Calvin Crumrine 
<Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us> wrote:

> lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca wrote:
> 
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Not really, you will notice that I did not name Lexmarks as a 
particular "mindless win-printer" source. Almost all manufacturers 
seem to have several models of inkjet printers that are "host based" 
or "mindless win-printers". You can usually pick them out because they
always require "Windows XX" as an operating system and do not mention 
support of a particular command language (not to mention they are 
usually the "most inexpensive"). HP, Lexmark, Canon all have one or 
models.

One other problem that I forgot to mention - even if the printer is 
not a "mindless win-printer" the driver may require you to select 
"support DOS printing" during installation. Without that support 
Windows will not provide you with the ability to open a line 
sequential file on "LPTx" and write to it.

The PC_PRINTER_xxx library calls allow you to access to basic printer 
functions and font control. If you want to do more complex page 
handling the PC_PRINT_OPEN? call returns the handle to the Windows 
device context and you can use that to call any of the Windows 
graphics drawing APIs to do whatever you want on the printer.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: InkJet Printer Support anyone?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Calvin Crumrine <Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us>
- **Date:** 2001-01-16T12:25:26-09:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A64BC46.DE48D9E2@dced.state.ak.us>`
- **References:** `<940997$shg$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-Kp3KvSs2i9qI@tcpserver> <3A647C19.AB2474B1@dced.state.ak.us> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-dddZdMKj93H4@tcpserver>`

```


lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca wrote:

> Not really, you will notice that I did not name Lexmarks as a
> particular "mindless win-printer" source. Almost all manufacturers
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> Lorne Sunley

Apparently I misunderstood your original posting. I took it as you saying that a lot
of the inkjet printers were 'mindless win-printers' which is incorrect. I won't argue
that all inkjet printer manufacturers have produced at least one model which is a
mindless win-printer. It's true of all *manufacturers* that I know of but it isn't
true of all inkjet *printers* I know of. Several come to mind that *will* print a
text file simply by copying it to LPTx. *Some* of those 'require' Windows because
they only ship with Windows drivers. They also support a 'printer control language'
although it's often expressed as an 'emulation mode' and is for a dot-matrix printer,
usually an Epson. (By the way, I just realized that these are several years old so
maybe they aren't manufactured any more. Maybe you're right about all currently
manufactured inkjet printers.)

Anyway, my comment re: Lexmark was to accept what I understood to be your comment
about *all* inkjet printers as possibly applying to all *Lexmark* inkjet printers.
(This is because I'm not familiar with Lexmark inkjet printers.) This was not meant
to imply that some non-Lexmark inkjet printers don't share the same failings, only
that not all of them do.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: InkJet Printer Support anyone?

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com>

#### ↳ Re: InkJet Printer Support anyone?

- **From:** Warren Porter <wbport4@bellsouth3.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-16T18:03:13-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A64E141.99117F02@bellsouth3.net>`
- **References:** `<940997$shg$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
You might want to contact the maker of your printer for this information.  I was
able to "print" an escape sequence for an HP printer a few years ago, but
nothing is available for my Epson at home.  The printer would have to have its
own memory to handle escape sequences.  If the print driver converts text into
Run Length Encoding (fire the ink jet when you get here and for this long), you
might be out of luck trying to go around that.

Sam wrote:

> Hi All;
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Anyone know the control-codes/escape-sequences necessary to overcome this?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: InkJet Printer Support anyone?

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-17T02:18:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Oh796.4093$KE.213456@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<940997$shg$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <3A64E141.99117F02@bellsouth3.net>`

```

"Warren Porter" <wbport4@bellsouth3.net> wrote in message
news:3A64E141.99117F02@bellsouth3.net...
> You might want to contact the maker of your printer for this
information.  I was
> able to "print" an escape sequence for an HP printer a few years
ago, but
> nothing is available for my Epson at home.  The printer would have
to have its
> own memory to handle escape sequences.  If the print driver converts
text into
> Run Length Encoding (fire the ink jet when you get here and for this
long), you
> might be out of luck trying to go around that.
>

Exactly. Printer manufacturers can save $2.35 per printer by putting
the escape decode logic in Windows software rather than a chip inside
the printer. $2.35 here, $2.35 there. It adds up.
```

#### ↳ Re: InkJet Printer Support anyone?

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-01-18T01:17:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3a6643ac.129779204@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<940997$shg$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
If you want to create some really nice printouts with pictures,
different fonts, or even if you just want to create simple reports
from your COBOL programs, you should check out COBOL Form Print from
Flexus (http://www.flexus.com).  It's a breeze and makes printing to
ANY windows based printer from your COBOL program easy and fast.

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dylan,comp.lang.eiffel,comp.lang.forth,comp.lang.forth.mac,comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.functional,comp.lang.hermes,comp.lang.icon,comp.lang.idl
- **Message-ID:** `<Ba196.739$FL1.33211@twister2.tin.it>`

```
http://members.xoom.it/antsant/
Please, excuse me if this message is not connected to the manifesto of your
newsgroup but I need your help. I beg you to nopt consider this as spam
My name is Antonio Santoro, e-mail antonio.ing@tin.it. I am a town
councellor in my town, Casagiove in suthern Italy. Its territory adjoins one
of the most beautiful monuments ever: The Royal Palace of Caserta, built by
the Borbons dinasty at the beginnings of 1752 according to the project of
Luigi Vanvitelli. The inside is a treasure of marvellous works; in the dense
Park you can admire wonderful fountins with statues, waterfalls and the
english Garden. Well, all a surrounding area of the complex of the Royal
Palace and park is subject to a binding obligation of the landscape with a
ministerial decree for the National Trust 9 July 1996.
In this decree it is stated that "the external boundary has a notabile
public interest because, due to its outstanding panoramic beauty which forms
a natural painting of incomparable suggestivity, a complex of real property
having a traditional and beautiful value. considering that the entire
complex of incomparable panoramic beauty, once surrounded by fields and
vegetables patches is now in the centre of the built-up areas of caserta and
casagiiove wich continue to expand without any regulation whatsoever for the
building activities in the areas bordering the vanvitellian complex .
considering that the binding obligation does not mean absolute prohibition
of building or however, modifications to the state of protected places by
the law but it must be considered an indispensable instrument in maintaining
both the remaining green hilly areas and the built-up plains, bordering the
park, by controls that prevent the degrading and the transformation of the
area it is stated that it is of noteable public interest according to the
law no. 1497 the 29 june 1939 and in application of art. 82 of the Decree of
the President of the Republic no. 416 24 july 1977.
In that area of territory subject to the binding obligation of the landscape
wich applies to my town, a building pernmit has been grated. Asd I explain
in the included report which I sent to the Department for National Trust and
the Republic Power this permit is based on an incorrect procedure therefore,
it is invalidated by lack of inquiry and breaching of the law. Moreover, the
authorities for the National trust of Caserta did not intervene promptly to
annul this permit. The matter is at present being closely examined by the
legal authorities. In the statement to the Department, wich is included, it
is evident the great environmental  impact of the building considering also
the great contrast of this structure with the building and decorative
aspects of the buildings situated in the vicinity that characterize the
places from a landscape point of view so a to be considered the most
significative appurtances of the nearby Royal Park of Caserta. I ask for
your help in intervening with the competent authorities in order to reach
the restoration of the places and that the competent authorities control
more efficiently the places subject to a binding obligation. I have prepared
for you two e-mail adressed to the members of  Italian Parliament  and  the
chief of our government, the National Trust and some newsmen, with my
manifesto. With a click you would help me and a monument that is a property
of all Humanity.
Thanks
You'll find the mail on my site at this address
http://members.xoom.it/antsant/Versione%20Inglese.htm
Clicking there you will find also a zip file containing a photographic
documentation


Clicking there you will find some mails ready to be send to the Italian
Government, the Memebers of Italian Parliament and the main newsmen
```

### JOB-US-VA-Richmond- COBOL Mainframe Programmer/ Analyst

- **From:** Joe Bedits- Object Systems Group <joe.bedits@osgcorp.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-16T22:14:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mJ396.37436$68.5286558@typhoon.tampabay.rr.com>`

```


Immediate opening available for a COBOL Mainframe Programmer/ Analyst  
in the Richmond, VA area. 

The project will involve analyzing existing mainframe applications and  
preparing it for porting to a Unix platform.

*  Must have experience with COBOL 1 & 2 (77 & 85)
*  Experience with porting is a plus.
*  Experience with Unisys is a plus.

Contract should be 6 mos.-1 year+. Rates are commensurate with  
experience.

Please feel free to send resumes to:

Joe Bedits
Object Systems Group, Inc. (OSG)
joe.bedits@osgcorp.com
(727) 786-3131
F: (727) 773-9683
www.osgcorp.com

Object Systems Group, Incorporated is a technology-based consultancy that
assists Fortune 500 class corporations with the migration and re-engineering
of traditional corporate systems environments to object-oriented based  
systems.
```

### Quantum extensions in HL-languages

- **From:** Herwig Huener & Josella Simone Playton <webmaster@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>
- **Date:** 2001-01-17T23:20:33+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A661AB1.1D38CDA9@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>`

```
2001-01-17 23:23:23 MET

A few weeks ago, I was speculating about quantum computing
concepts in high level languages and high level language
compilers right here. I did some reading in the meantime, but I
did not get much wiser.

As a new standard for COBOL is being defined at this time,
one could include a few quantum concepts, without affecting
other parts of the language. But the practical value is
nothing more than a sort of marketing gag. It would sound
swell indeed: "COBOL first quantum computing language!"

But that's all. No immediate practical value.

However, it is legitimate to ponder which concepts could be
included into a programming language right now safely. To
my mind, there are three:

1) The ability to define qbit data. In COBOL, this would be an
   extension to the PIC clause.

2) The Hadamard operator. This is needed for doing a quantum
   rotation on single qbits in order to effect the superposition
   of 0 and 1. In COBOL, it could have the form of an intrinsic
   function.

3) Decoherence - either implied, that is, decoherence takes
   place anytime qbit data are moved back into ordinary data -
   or explicit, also in the form of an intrinsic function.

That's all for now. Computing with qbits needs reversible
operations - and most of the ordinary things one can do in a
programming language are not reversible by themselves. They
*can* be made reversible, but the point is, there are operations
which are more easily expressed as reversible. Fast Fourier
Transforms, for instance, is easily done in a reversible manner,
but a simple addition is not (It could be made, but the logic
has to be constructed specially for qbits, out of Toffoli
gates and such. Thats an issue of hardware-architecture.).

So one had to decide (somewhen in the future) which operations
are allowed for qbit-data and which ones are not, and which
kind of new operations should be included into the language.

Then, a few sequentional computations must be possible while
taking care not to effect decoherence too early (which is
quite a challenge for hardware engineers!). The language
must allow that. But it is not possible, in any language,
to examine qbit data by inspection. No IF and no EVALUATE,
at least on qbit data! - I expect, a working compiler for
quantum code should have a look on this (for that reason,
I would prefer an explicit decoherence operator above
so that a compiler can do some checking - that's a cleaner
solution.).
```

#### ↳ Re: Quantum extensions in HL-languages

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-01-18T13:14:38+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<945fu4$g96$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3A661AB1.1D38CDA9@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>`

```

Herwig Huener & Josella Simone Playton wrote in message
<3A661AB1.1D38CDA9@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>...
>2001-01-17 23:23:23 MET


>As a new standard for COBOL is being defined at this time,
>one could include a few quantum concepts, without affecting
>other parts of the language.

Have you any idea of how difficult it is to get ANYTHING into this standard?
The process which is followed involves committees and reviews and drafts and
public reviews and then a final draft (a year later...). We are having a
very hard time just getting Object Oriented Extensions which everyone can
agree on, into the 2001 Standard. And this is REAL!

A submission to J4 with Quantum extensions as you describe would necessarily
push the whole process even further back, and the chance of anyone
understanding and supporting the proposed extensions is "small but finite"
(about the same chance as the molecules of your body aligning with the
spaces in the wall, thus allowing you to pass through it...it COULD happen,
but you might have to wait longer than the age of the Universe for it to
occur...<G>)

>>>
>But the practical value is
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
<<<
Agree it SOUNDS good... but it would also make COBOL look very stupid when
we explained that it is impossible to implement...


>>>
>However, it is legitimate to ponder which concepts could be
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
<<<
Now it's just getting silly...

I'll suspend belief (for  the sake of your argument) and buy number 1
above...the ability to define a qbit.

Now we come to number 2... The Hadamard operator. As soon as you try and do
ANY kind of operation on a qbit you collapse the wave function and drop it
into Reality...Even just LOOKING at it will do this.

Now consider that I  am a COBOL compiler writer. (As I once was, "long ago,
in a distant Galaxy"...)

You are asking me to define storage that I must NEVER reference...!

Option 3 hardly needs to be an intrinsic function; you can  (and will) get
decoherence just by referencing the qbit.

The current state of computing (based on the sequential Von Neumann model)
is not ready for Quantum Computing. This includes the compiler writers.

Neither is the Engineering community. The fact that some extremely expensive
projects in some Academic ivory towers have managed to produce a Quantum
device with 4 Qbits, does NOT mean that such devices are "just around the
corner".

There are still enough questions over the whole field to confine it to the
marble halls of Acadaemia for the foreseeable future.

Assuming such a device COULD be built, and software to operate it COULD be
written, it is VERY unlikely that COBOL will be the language of choice. By
the time such a device is operating (if EVER) COBOL programming will have
been relegated to the history books and will be about as relevant as Latin
is today. (Useful for research and history, not practical current
applications...)


>
>So one had to decide (somewhen in the future) which operations
>are allowed for qbit-data and which ones are not, and which
>kind of new operations should be included into the language.
>
Well that's pretty easy...EXISTS or NOT EXISTS just about covers it for a
Qbit...

The whole concept of "operations" on Qbits is a contradiction in terms. As
soon as you do ANY kind of operation on it, it is no longer a Qbit.

>>>
>Then, a few sequentional computations must be possible while
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>at least on qbit data! -
<<<
You got that right...!!! And it is a "cop out" to suggest that delaying
decoherence is an engineering problem.

The whole concept is unworkable within the framework of Von Neumann
computing.

And that is the framework within which current programmers (and the COBOL
Language) exist.


>>>
>This was the limited supply of ideas I have now. I have found
>not much information in the net on how a sequential quantum
>algorithm should look like - and a programmer in a quantum
>language should be fluent in such things.
<<<
I have tried to show that a "Sequential Quantum Algorithm" is a
contradiction in terms (like "Military Intelligence" or "Customer Service"
from your Bank...<G>).

It doesn't suprise me you have found nothing. Quantum Algorithms (IF and
when we EVER achieve a practical implementation of them) MUST be parallell
by their very nature. A Quantum Algorithm would operate across the
Multiverse until decoherence. It cannot be sequential.

OK, Herwig, a refreshing and stimulating idea (not to mention good fun) but
please don't
get serious about connecting it to COBOL.

COBOL is having enough image problems at the moment without being labelled
as a "crank" vehicle.

Regards,

Pete.
```

```

Herwig Huener & Josella Simone Playton wrote in message
<3A669C3C.50711B50@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>...
>2001-01-17 08:36:00 MEZ
>
>"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

>>>
>> Agree it SOUNDS good... but it would also make COBOL look very stupid
when
>> we explained that it is impossible to implement...
>
>Not really. We can blame the hardware-engineers.
<<<
Hahaha! I LOVED that, Herwig! Excellent!

>>>
>
>> Now we come to number 2... The Hadamard operator. As soon as you try and
do
>> ANY kind of operation on a qbit you collapse the wave function and drop
it
>> into Reality...Even just LOOKING at it will do this.
>
>The Hadamard operator is a quantum operator. It will not
>collapse the wave-function.
<<<
er... in that case, it can NEVER be implemented in a Quantum computer.

Herwig, we MUST draw a distinction between theory and practice if you are
for one minute serious about officially extending the COBOL standard.
Irrespective of theoretical operations, in PRACTICE (certainly in THIS
Reality) ANY operation on QBits (other than superposition of states; which
is an inherent property and NOT an Operation) WILL collapse the wave
function with immediate consequent decoherence. Now, this might be OK if you
define your operation to occur AFTER decoherence...


>>>
>> Now consider that I  am a COBOL compiler writer. (As I once was, "long
ago,
>> in a distant Galaxy"...)
>>
>> You are asking me to define storage that I must NEVER reference...!
>
>Unless you want to do decoherence. Then you may.
<<<
OK. So EVERYTHING you are discussing is only achievable AFTER decoherence,
by analagous bits in this Universe.

Why have a Quantum Computer at all? Just build something with REAL bits in
it that emulates EVERY possible superposition and "train" it to examine
EVERY possible result, selecting the ones which might be a solution...(Bit
like a Super Chess program really...)

I would agree that that COULD be programmed in COBOL. In fact, it would
require no additions to the language spec...<G>

>>>
>> Option 3 hardly needs to be an intrinsic function; you can  (and will)
get
>> decoherence just by referencing the qbit.
>
>Not as long as the reference is done by reversible logic.
<<<
Well, I should've guessed that anyone who's prepared to take on J4 in order
to get non-implementable extensions to the COBOL Language, certainly
wouldn't hesitate to defy the Laws of Physics...<G>

Decoherence will occur whether the Qbit is referenced with reversible logic
or not.  But you know that...is this a wind up? <G>


>>>
>> Assuming such a device COULD be built, and software to operate it COULD
be
>> written, it is VERY unlikely that COBOL will be the language of choice.
By
>> the time such a device is operating (if EVER) COBOL programming will have
>> been relegated to the history books and will be about as relevant as
Latin
>> is today. (Useful for research and history, not practical current
>> applications...)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>so will COBOL.
<<<
Well, my COBOL credentials are strong enough that I can be realistic about
the fate of the language.

(Indeed, "computer programming" in general. There is a limited life for the
current model.)

My comment is not a criticism of COBOL; it's a reflection of when Quantum
Computing is likely to become a reality...<G>

>>>
>Which must be the case, because, as I said, Quantum Computing
>is a step beyond classical computing.
<<<
As Science Fiction is a step beyond Science...

>>>
>> It doesn't suprise me you have found nothing. Quantum Algorithms (IF and
>> when we EVER achieve a practical implementation of them) MUST be
parallell
>> by their very nature. A Quantum Algorithm would operate across the
>> Multiverse until decoherence. It cannot be sequential.
>
>Misunderstanding there. A few quantum computation steps
>might be carried out in succession.
<<<
Yes, they MIGHT. BUT ONLY AFTER decoherence!

There is ONE possibility in all of this which MIGHT be a Show-Stopper or an
Enabler. The EPR effect (Einstein-Podolsky-Rosen). If the Qbit could be
constructed in such a way that it was composed of a wavicle AND an
anti-wavicle (notice I did NOT specify particle or wave), AND these two
wavicles could be subject to EPR separation, it MIGHT be possible to deduce
the state of a Qbit WITHOUT actually looking at it and thereby causing
decoherence. It's a pretty big MIGHT...and I have no idea how a device which
used such wavicles could be built in the Real world, but an EPR experiment
was successfully carried out in Paris in 1983, confirming the theoretical
predictions of Nils Bohr against those of Einstein, Podolsky, and Rosen. In
this regard, Quantum Mechanics violates Relativity and information "travels"
faster than light, being available at a distance instantaneously. Of course,
a Qbit which utilized the EPR effect could only be used ONCE... (unless
someone comes up with a bright idea to get round this...I believe the
University of Sussex are working on it (at least, they were in the
mid-Eighties...heard nothing since, which does NOT imply success...)

Come to think of it, this is more likely to be a Show-stopper...the probable
result would be an Infinity as the Qbit adopted the superposition and could
never return a bit state. So no useful information could be obtained, the
Uncertainty Principle is not violated, and God and Heisenberg chalk up
another one over Einstein...

All we are getting out of this is that there is no point in applying
conventional logic to the weird world of Quantum Mechanics.

I for one don't have enough in-depth knowledge in this field to predict the
outcomes, once you get away from the basic fundamental principles.

I DO understand enough to know what is feasible in the Real World at this
time. (At least I think I do...I'm really Uncertain  <G>)




>>>
>Well, thinking aloud is legitimate, isn't it?
<<<
Not only legitimate, but to be encouraged.

However, the bottom line is that Quantum extensions to COBOL are unrealistic
for the following reasons:

1. They cannot be implemented.

2. They cannot be implemented.

3. They cannot be implemented.

Pete.



>Herwig
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>GruberStrasse 10 A / D-85655 GrossHelfenDorf / Bayern / EU
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Quantum extensions in HL-languages

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Herwig Huener <Herwig.Huener!@!fujitsu-siemens.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-18T17:20:53+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9475bm$j6b$1@news.mch.sbs.de>`
- **References:** `<3A661AB1.1D38CDA9@Josella-Simone-Playton.de> <945fu4$g96$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A669C3C.50711B50@Josella-Simone-Playton.de> <946vho$iv7$4@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`

```
2001-01-18 17:19:21 MEZ

"Peter E. C. Dashwood" schrieb:
> 
> ...

> >The Hadamard operator is a quantum operator. It will not
> >collapse the wave-function.
> <<<
> er... in that case, it can NEVER be implemented in a Quantum computer.

What? It is most essential for a quantum computer. It is the device
which makes the superposition. You move a logical '0' to a qbit
and the hadamard operator creates the superposition

|0|   -->   x |0> + y |1>

(x and y same size)

If you wish, you can read that qbit right now, an decoherence will
happen, and you get either 0 or 1. But most probably you will do
something with those thusly prepared qbits.

> ...

> Herwig, we MUST draw a distinction between theory and practice if you are
> for one minute serious about officially extending the COBOL standard.

I have not yet thought about my degree of seriousness.

> Irrespective of theoretical operations, in PRACTICE (certainly in THIS
> Reality)

:-)

> ANY operation on QBits (other than superposition of states; which
> is an inherent property and NOT an Operation) WILL collapse the wave
> function with immediate consequent decoherence. Now, this might be OK if you
> define your operation to occur AFTER decoherence...

which would be ordinary logical operations. Those collapse indeed,
but for those, wee need no quantum computer.

Operations which do not destroy information do not necessarily
make decoherence - and the class of hadamard operators belong to
those (might be that those operations are called "unitary operators",
but my knowledge of QM is feeble).

I would categorize hardware gates as follows:

---------------------+-----------------------------
ordinary logic       |     causes decoherence
gates (AND, OR,      |     & generates entropy
NAND, NOR, XOR)      |     classical logic
---------------------+-----------------------------
reversible logic     |     no decoherence
gates (Fredkin,      |
Toffoli, NOT, ...)   |
---------------------+-----------------------------
reversible quantum   |     no decoherence,
rotations            |     causing interference
(Hadamard, ...)      |     of superpositions
---------------------+-----------------------------

Today, our computers are build from the first category of gates,
the other two would be needed for a quantum computer. As for
me, I regard the second row of gates as a way to compute
reversibly with different data, and the third row as a way
to compute with the same data in different superpositions.
The third row extends quantum algorithms into the direction
of multiverses. Therefrom comes the power of quantum algorithms.

As far as I have grasped it.

> ...

> Why have a Quantum Computer at all? Just build something with REAL bits in
> it that emulates EVERY possible superposition and "train" it to examine
> EVERY possible result, selecting the ones which might be a solution...(Bit
> like a Super Chess program really...)

Of course you can simulate a quantum program with classical logic.
In some cases, a few 10^100 computation steps are necessary -
depending on your data.

> ...

> Well, I should've guessed that anyone who's prepared to take on J4 in order
> to get non-implementable extensions to the COBOL Language, certainly
> wouldn't hesitate to defy the Laws of Physics...<G>

Well, let us see the results of the public review.
Maybe J4 will be drowned in non-implementable
extensions!

> ...

> >What are you saying about  COBOL in a COBOL-newsgroup?
> >If there are classical programming languages around,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> current model.)

Yes! But (OT now): COBOL is oftenly the language in large
institutions. Large institutions suffer from Bureaucracy.
Bureaucracy is for ever. So COBOL is forever. - OT end.

> ...

> >> It doesn't suprise me you have found nothing. Quantum Algorithms (IF and
> >> when we EVER achieve a practical implementation of them) MUST be
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Yes, they MIGHT. BUT ONLY AFTER decoherence!

No. What I have in mind is a compiler analyzing several
successive quantum operations, compute a reversible
net of gates which do exactly that computations and
generate instructions which command the hardware to
assemble those gates into that logical network so
that they might do this sequence of operations in
a reversible manner in one step.

You are right: We are in the Science Fiction business
now.

BTW (OT again): Suppose the commity draft would get
the famous HUGO-Award (an award for Science Fuction).
Would they be offended or flattered? - OT off.

> ...

> There is ONE possibility in all of this which MIGHT be a Show-Stopper or an
> Enabler. The EPR effect (Einstein-Podolsky-Rosen). If the Qbit could be
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> decoherence.

It cannot be done. Suppose I prepare two entagled qbits:

x |00> + y |11> + 0 |01> + 0 |10>

(x and y same size)

If you look on one of the qbits in order to find out
what the state of the other one is, you cause
decoherence for both. In the multiverse-view, you find
yorself either in the universe with both bits 1 or in
the universe with both bits 0. The universes with the
qbit combinations 01 and 10 do not exist.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Quantum extensions in HL-languages

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-01-19T13:03:56+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9482d4$kfu$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3A661AB1.1D38CDA9@Josella-Simone-Playton.de> <945fu4$g96$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A669C3C.50711B50@Josella-Simone-Playton.de> <946vho$iv7$4@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <9475bm$j6b$1@news.mch.sbs.de>`

```
Some interesting ideas, Herwig.

I agree you are correct about entanglement. Decoherence in both universes
would occur; that's why I suggested that such a Qbit device could be used
only once...It is totally impractical and arose from my effort to try and
see SOME way we could view or deduce the actual state of a qbit. This is
completely fallacious as the actual state of the Qbit is the superposition
and anything else is irrelevant...I guess a lifetime of computer programming
causes us to think in certain ways, which are completely inappropriate to
QM...

It seems to me that your grasp of Theory is excellent but you may have
missed the practical problems of translating this into the current Reality
(whatever the Hell THAT is...!)

I also believe that the Universe shrouds her secrets behind things like the
Uncertainty Principle and there may be some nasty surprises in store when we
actually start to build Quantum Computers.

I liked your analogy of the eye, the telescope and the photons, but I am
still not totally persuaded...

Anyway, I can't spend any more time on this. Thanks for the stimulation and
keep taking the tablets...!

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Quantum extensions in HL-languages

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Herwig Huener <Herwig.Huener!@!fujitsu-siemens.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-19T10:29:47+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9491kt$2mh$1@news.mch.sbs.de>`
- **References:** `<3A661AB1.1D38CDA9@Josella-Simone-Playton.de> <945fu4$g96$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <3A669C3C.50711B50@Josella-Simone-Playton.de> <946vho$iv7$4@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <9475bm$j6b$1@news.mch.sbs.de> <9482d4$kfu$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`

```
2001-01-19 10:33:01 MET

"Peter E. C. Dashwood" schrieb:
> 
> Some interesting ideas, Herwig.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> QM...

A nice way to say that we are too old to learn new concepts!
And that is certainly right: I have my difficulties with
QM. Back in the old days when I made my diploma, I tried to
avoid QM. Successful.

> It seems to me that your grasp of Theory is excellent but you may have
> missed the practical problems of translating this into the current Reality
> (whatever the Hell THAT is...!)

One could be depressed when one sees the first prototypes
of qbits which are built this time. But then, technical
progress comes in surprises. And, as I told, we have
already quantum computation devices, such as optical
instruments. So we know it's not impossible, and we
should somehow be prepared.

Just the small detail of rewriting the foundations of
computer science is yet unfinished, but I trust David
Deutsch will do a good job there.

With the proof that the classical Turing machine does not
exist, it follows that no classical programming language
exists. Including COBOL.

But we wont tell our customers and continue to
sell a COBOL-compiler!

> I also believe that the Universe shrouds her secrets behind things like the
> Uncertainty Principle and there may be some nasty surprises in store when we
> actually start to build Quantum Computers.

One of the nasty surprises is (might be) that the universe
is isomorph to a mathematical structure. Then it is not
necessary that the universe exists - we would not see the
difference!

> I liked your analogy of the eye, the telescope and the photons, but I am
> still not totally persuaded...
> 
> Anyway, I can't spend any more time on this. Thanks for the stimulation and
> keep taking the tablets...!

Now and then I shall continue to drop some ideas here.
But I also cannot spend more time on this right
now because my employer expects me contributing to our
COBOL compiler, and not proving that no COBOL and no
computers exist.

Herwig
```

#### ↳ Re: Quantum extensions in HL-languages

- **From:** "TJ Dombrowski" <keldin@earthlink.not>

```
I would be satisfied if Cobol would have unsigned comp-3 as a datatype for
reading data from
unix programmers that don't know how to pack data properly.
Why do unix "programmers" (used lightly)  insist on packing data as  4321
instead of   S999 or S99999.  s9(3) comp-3 or s9(5) comp-3. I keep getting
data from people packing it as 9999 (packed with no sign)
signed comp-3 should always be odd so the sign fits in the field. It's
getting anoying to fix these fields.
Currently using MVS COBOL II, does the lastest OS/390 COBOL II have a new
type for these?

Have fun
TJ

"Herwig Huener & Josella Simone Playton"
<webmaster@Josella-Simone-Playton.de> wrote in message
news:3A661AB1.1D38CDA9@Josella-Simone-Playton.de...
> 2001-01-17 23:23:23 MET
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> did not get much wiser.

> 1) The ability to define qbit data. In COBOL, this would be an
>    extension to the PIC clause.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Quantum extensions in HL-languages

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-18T00:44:43-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9463d6$cgd$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3A661AB1.1D38CDA9@Josella-Simone-Playton.de> <muv96.326$tD2.32052@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
No, the latest IBM mainframe (or workstation) COBOL compiler does NOT
provide a data-type for unsigned packed-decimal items.  (You can "specify" a
PIC 999 COMP-3 or Packed-Decimal data description - but it still assigns
storage for the sign-nibble).

The ANSI/ISO Standard (past, current, and future) allow the implementor to
decide what to do with such fields.  Some vendors have a "COMP-6" usage
specifically for this - but not IBM.

P.S.  Of course you CAN always do a SHARE requirement to ask for this - but
I don't think you should hold your breath until IBM provides it.
```

I have been using a product that uses assembly to strip off the sign
bit by starting from a given half-byte, either left or right. I don't
know what good that would do if at all compared to how COBOL reads the
data.  When I was taking Assembly, when we packed a number, it had an
initially non-signed state, but it still had a sign bit.  Then when you
moved it or used it in an operation it had a specific sign applied to
it.  In assembly it also took 5 steps to add the dollar sign without a
macro; Of course we used an assembler that fit on one floppy disk.


In article <20010118073125.06843.00000880@nso-fc.aol.com>,

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ unsigned comp-3

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "TJ Dombrowski" <keldin@earthlink.not>
- **Date:** 2001-01-19T03:15:55+00:00

```
COBOL in MVS needs the sign nibble for COMP-3 or you cant use the field.
so if you send a file from some other system to an IBM mainframe.  You
should
save room for the sign and use an odd number of digits.

These people think they are saving space in transmission of half a byte.
They have no idea that they are making all of these fields very difficult to
use.

Their standard response is well SAS can read it.  Well thats fine and dandy
but SAS isnt production COBOL is.     See the anaylsts who do the
requirements
don't care if the programmers that have to load the crap have trouble.
since they use SAS 98% of the time what do they care.

Its seems to be happing more and more.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Quantum extensions in HL-languages

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-19T05:22:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A67CEFA.FBE4DA2@optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3A661AB1.1D38CDA9@Josella-Simone-Playton.de> <muv96.326$tD2.32052@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
TJ Dombrowski wrote:

> I would be satisfied if Cobol would have unsigned comp-3 as a datatype for
> reading data from
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> type for these?

This is a hardware specification, not a COBOL one. It's the way packed-decimal
numbers have been implemented on IBM boxes since the S/360's (almost 40 years
ago, they were announced in April, 1964).

LiamD
```

<a id="t-d516fed7ed"></a>
## Thread: COBOL/VSE and ICCF batch partitions — 1 msgs

### COBOL/VSE and ICCF batch partitions

- **From:** "Leon" <Leon.in.Newport.News@att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-18T01:39:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uPr96.439$Mu1.17281@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
I have a few programs written in DOS/COBOL that were intended to be run in
an ICCF batch partition.

When I converted one of the programs to COBOL/VSE I discovered that it would
not run because the ICCF batch partition was loading run-time phases from
the SCEECICS library instead of the SCEEBASE library.

Is there a way to do the equivalent of a LIBDEF in an ICCF batch partition
to force the loading of run-time phases from the SCEEBASE library?

Leon
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu compiler 3 problem for beginner

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2001-01-18T06:42:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010118014220.25954.00001083@nso-fm.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<20010117213738.16178.00000732@ng-ca1.aol.com>`

```
In article <20010117213738.16178.00000732@ng-ca1.aol.com>, merfair@aol.com
(MERfair) writes:

>
>Can anyone tell me where I can put my input files so that they will be read
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You should be able to place them in any directory and 
then point to them via the RunTimeEnvironment setup.
It really depends on how you have defined the files in
your program.  Have you coded the filenames on the select
or in WS?  Or did you use an implementor name that 
can be 'fed' the real file name via environment variables
created/provided thru the Run-Time Environment setup.
```

## Thread: Cobol student question — 14 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Cobol student question

- **From:** Eoyore <mac3@slic.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-17T23:27:17-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A669AD5.4D948C92@slic.com>`

```
I just started class.  There is no compiler or book yet.
The following was taken from a handout.

There are two Procedure Divisions below.  The first is verbatim
from the handout in class.  I altered the second.  Will both 
produce the same results?  I changed the Read sentences around.

Thanks
Eoyore

Procedure Division.
Prepare-Senior-Report.
	Open Input Student-File
	     Output Print-File.
	Read Student-File
	     At End Move 'No' to Data-Remains-Switch
	End-Read.
	Perform Write-Heading-Line.
	Perform Process-Records
	     Until Data-Remains-Switch='No'.
	Close Student-File
	      Print-File
	Stop Run.

Write-Heading-Line.
	Move Heading-Line to Print-Line.
	Write Print-Line.

Process-Records.
	If Stu-Credits > 110 And Stu-Major= 'Engineering'
	   Move Stu-Name To Print-Name
	   Move Detail-Line to Print-Line
	   Write Print-Line
	End-If.
	Read Student-File
	   At End Move 'NO' to Data-Remains-Switch
	End-Read.

Procedure Division.
Prepare-Senior-Report.
	Open Input Student-File
	     Output Print-File.
	Perform Write-Heading-Line.
	Perform Process-Records
	     Until Data-Remains-Switch='No'.
	Close Student-File
	      Print-File
	Stop Run.

Write-Heading-Line.
	Move Heading-Line to Print-Line.
	Write Print-Line.

Process-Records.
	Read Student-File
	   At End Move 'NO' to Data-Remains-Switch
	End-Read.
	If Stu-Credits > 110 And Stu-Major= 'Engineering'
	   Move Stu-Name To Print-Name
	   Move Detail-Line to PRint-Line
	   Write Print-Line
	End-If.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol student question

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-17T22:57:24-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<945t2j$isr$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3A669AD5.4D948C92@slic.com> <945sfc$354o$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com>`

```
I don't know if you text book (or other class material) covers this or not -
but you might want to look for the subject "priming read".  (This is one of
those things that CAN cause "religious debates" among programmers - but the
"change" that you made will not work as you desire in all cases - but might
in some others.)

Another topic that you might want to look for in your class material is the
"WITH TEST BEFORE" and "WITH TEST AFTER" options of the PERFORM statement.
Normally, this is NOT taught right at the beginning of a COBOL class - but
if you have had some "programming logic" courses or have programmed in other
"structured" languages, this may help you understand the issue.

P.S.  Please ignore if these terms don't mean anything to you - but the
default COBOL "PERFORM UNTIL" is equivalent to the "normal" (theoretical
programming) DO WHILE loop and is NOT the same as a "DO UNTIL" loop.  This
often causes a LOT of confusion to new COBOL programmers - who are familiar
with other languages or approaches.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol student question

- **From:** "TJ Dombrowski" <keldin@earthlink.not>
- **Date:** 2001-01-18T05:38:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vjv96.283$tD2.29340@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3A669AD5.4D948C92@slic.com>`

```
This a big debate, but it is more effecient to never go to the process
routine if there are no records
most programs prime the pump before testing wether to process the first
record.

There are numerous occasions where the file could be empty on the first
record.

TJ

In article <3A669AD5.4D948C92@slic.com>, Eoyore <mac3@slic.com> writes:

>
>There are two Procedure Divisions below.  The first is verbatim
>from the handout in class.  I altered the second.  Will both 
>produce the same results?  I changed the Read sentences around.
>

I personally suspect that the altered code will produce almost the 
same results.  The only exception that I see has to do with the
procedures coded after END-READ that are exercised regardless
of your EOF condition.  Often times this results in the program crashing.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol student question

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>

On Wed, 17 Jan 2001 23:27:17 -0800 Eoyore <mac3@slic.com> wrote:

:>I just started class.  There is no compiler or book yet.
:>The following was taken from a handout.

:>There are two Procedure Divisions below.  The first is verbatim
:>from the handout in class.  I altered the second.  Will both 
:>produce the same results?  I changed the Read sentences around.

It depend if the last input record fits 

   Stu-Credits > 110 And Stu-Major= 'Engineering'

and what the implementation of this version of cobol places in the record area
when the read causes end of file.

:>Thanks
:>Eoyore

:>Procedure Division.
:>Prepare-Senior-Report.
:>	Open Input Student-File
:>	     Output Print-File.
:>	Read Student-File
:>	     At End Move 'No' to Data-Remains-Switch
:>	End-Read.
:>	Perform Write-Heading-Line.
:>	Perform Process-Records
:>	     Until Data-Remains-Switch='No'.
:>	Close Student-File
:>	      Print-File
:>	Stop Run.

:>Write-Heading-Line.
:>	Move Heading-Line to Print-Line.
:>	Write Print-Line.

:>Process-Records.
:>	If Stu-Credits > 110 And Stu-Major= 'Engineering'
:>	   Move Stu-Name To Print-Name
:>	   Move Detail-Line to Print-Line
:>	   Write Print-Line
:>	End-If.
:>	Read Student-File
:>	   At End Move 'NO' to Data-Remains-Switch
:>	End-Read.

:>Procedure Division.
:>Prepare-Senior-Report.
:>	Open Input Student-File
:>	     Output Print-File.
:>	Perform Write-Heading-Line.
:>	Perform Process-Records
:>	     Until Data-Remains-Switch='No'.
:>	Close Student-File
:>	      Print-File
:>	Stop Run.

:>Write-Heading-Line.
:>	Move Heading-Line to Print-Line.
:>	Write Print-Line.

:>Process-Records.
:>	Read Student-File
:>	   At End Move 'NO' to Data-Remains-Switch
:>	End-Read.
:>	If Stu-Credits > 110 And Stu-Major= 'Engineering'
:>	   Move Stu-Name To Print-Name
:>	   Move Detail-Line to PRint-Line
:>	   Write Print-Line
:>	End-If.

#### ↳ Re: Cobol student question

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-19T05:29:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A67D0B0.A4AF1046@optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3A669AD5.4D948C92@slic.com>`

```
Eoyore wrote:

> I just started class.  There is no compiler or book yet.
> The following was taken from a handout.
…[59 more quoted lines elided]…
>         End-If.

If you're just learning COBOL why not learn to use the balanced
constructs, e.g.:

Process-Records.
        Read Student-File
           At End
                Move 'NO' to Data-Remains-Switch
           not At End
                process the record
        End-Read.

This way you don't need a priming read. Your logic works even if you
have a null input file. The way you've coded it, you'll process the last
record twice, once when you read it & again when you you reach the "at
end" condition. Also, you have an infinite read loop. I'll let you
figure out why.

HTH,
LiamD

(I have a thing against verbs on the same line as "at end" or "else",
you haven't made a mistake)
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol student question

- **From:** lcccpiasabird@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2001-01-19T18:01:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<949vee$q6e$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<3A669AD5.4D948C92@slic.com>`

```
In article <3A669AD5.4D948C92@slic.com>,
  Eoyore <mac3@slic.com> wrote:
> I just started class.  There is no compiler or book yet.
> The following was taken from a handout.
…[60 more quoted lines elided]…
>
-----------------
when you have a series of statements based on an if statement the if
ends after the first period and no "END-IF" is required.

Usually "END-IF" is only used when nesting if statement one after
another.

Usually EOJ, the part of the program that closes the files and ends the
program is in a separate paragraph.  That way it can be used in a
perform statement if you try to open a file that does not exist.  Thus
if an error code or bad file access code is found you can say if file-
status not = zero perform end-of-job.

HERE IS HOW A SIMPLE PROGRAM THAT READS ONE FILE IS NORMALLY WRITTEN IN
STRUCTURED PROGRAMMING (as opposed to top-down programming):

001-MAINLINE-LOGIC.
    PERFORM 101-INITIALIZE.
    PERFORM 103-PROCESS
       UNTIL END-OF-FILE = 'YES'.
    PERFORM 102-END-OF-JOB.
    STOP RUN.

Usually read is separate paragraph and one read is in the initialize
paragraph and then another read at the 'END' of the process.  This pre-
reads the first record.  You do not really want to write a header file
or anything if you can not read the first the record. That would be a
waste of time and paper.

A top-down approach isnt incorrect, but the reason for paragraph
notation in COBOL is to make the program have reusable code.  This was
a pre-cursor to object oriented programming (OOP).  So COBOL has some
aspects of OOP already built in.  I don't think the term OOP was
conceived until after COBOL came into use.  Just don't tell your C++ or
JAVA instructor that COBOL is an OOP language unless you want to
infuriate them!

I think COBOL is the easiest programming language to learn.  If you
want to know if a program works just copy it and rewrite it and run it.

  Try it the isntructor's way first and then try something else and see
what works better!!!  You may actually see that the instructor's way is
better.  COBOL isnt just a programming language but a unique way of
thinking.  When you have to do a hard program, do it in steps.  Just
get part of it to work and then work on the rest.  If you do it all at
once you will not know where to start, when you get a whole bunch of
errors after you compile the program.  Have fun!



Sent via Deja.com
http://www.deja.com/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol student question

- **From:** "TJ Dombrowski" <keldin@earthlink.not>
- **Date:** 2001-01-20T00:39:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<475a6.77$2E1.7118@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3A669AD5.4D948C92@slic.com> <949vee$q6e$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Well I totally agree below is a shell program that I use to start like 95%
of my programs
most programs are read record, process record, write record, loop until eof.
this basic shell has been used in hundreds of production jobs....
make sure you have a courier fixed font on so that it looks right.


       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.    PGMSHELL.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *                    P G M S H E L L                            *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *     DEPARTMENT NAME HERE-- SAMPLE SHELL PROGRAM               *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      * MODIFICATIONS/ENHANCEMENTS:                                   *
      *                        COMPILE                                *
      * REVISION  PROGRAMMER     DATE   MODIFICATIONS                 *
      * -------- ------------  -------- ----------------------------- *
      * TDYYMM   Your name   YY/MM/DD PGM SHELL                     *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      * LAST COMPILE:   YYYY, MM, DD      TIME: HH:MM AM              *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.

           SELECT IN-FILE1
             ASSIGN BDWAIFLE
             ORGANIZATION IS SEQUENTIAL
             ACCESS IS SEQUENTIAL
             STATUS I-FILE1-FS.

           SELECT O-FILE1
             ASSIGN BDWAOFLE
             ORGANIZATION IS SEQUENTIAL
             ACCESS IS SEQUENTIAL.

           SELECT I-CARD-FILE
             ASSIGN BDWACARD
             ORGANIZATION IS SEQUENTIAL
             ACCESS IS SEQUENTIAL
             STATUS I-CARD-FS.

       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.

      /
       FD  IN-FILE1
           BLOCK CONTAINS 0
           RECORDING MODE IS F.
      *01  I-FILE1-REC            PIC X(9999).
       01  I-FILE1-REC.
       COPY INCPYBK.

      /
       FD  O-FILE1
           BLOCK CONTAINS 0
           RECORDING MODE IS F.
      *01  O-FILE1-REC            PIC X(9999).
       01  O-FILE1-REC.
       COPY OUTCPYBK.

       FD  I-CARD-FILE
           BLOCK CONTAINS 0
           RECORDING MODE IS F.
       01  I-CARD-REC             PIC X(80).

      /
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  CTR-AREA               COMP-3.
           05 I-FILE1-CTR         PIC S9(11) VALUE +0.
           05 O-FILE1-CTR         PIC S9(11) VALUE +0.

       01  DIPLAY-AREAS.
           05 D-I-CTR             PIC ZZ,ZZZ,ZZZ,ZZ9.
           05 D-O-CTR             PIC ZZ,ZZZ,ZZZ,ZZ9.

       01  STATUS-AREA.
           05 I-FILE1-FS          PIC X(02).
              88 I-FILE1-AT-EOF             VALUE '10'.
           05 I-CARD-FS           PIC X(02).
              88 I-CARD-AT-EOF              VALUE '10'.

      *01  RETURN-CODE GLOBAL     PIC S9(4) BINARY VALUE ZERO.

       01  DATE-AREAS.
           05 WS-CARD-REC.
              10                  PIC X(10)  VALUE SPACES.
              10 WS-HEADING-1     PIC X(60)  VALUE SPACES.
              10                  PIC X(10)  VALUE SPACES.

       01  DATE-AREA-2.
           05 WS-SYS-DATE.
              10 SYS-YY           PIC 99      VALUE 01.
              10 SYS-MM           PIC 99      VALUE 01.
              10 SYS-DD           PIC 99      VALUE 01.
           05 WS-Y2K-SYS-DATE.
              10 Y2K-SYS-CENT     PIC 99      VALUE 20.
              10 Y2K-SYS-YY       PIC 99      VALUE 01.
           05 WS-SYS-TIME.
              10 SYS-HH           PIC 99      VALUE 12.
              10 SYS-MIN          PIC 99      VALUE 00.
              10 SYS-SS           PIC 99      VALUE 00.
           05 DATE-TABLE.
              10 MONTHS-OF-THE-YEAR.
                 15               PIC X(9) VALUE '  JANUARY'.
                 15               PIC X(9) VALUE ' FEBRUARY'.
                 15               PIC X(9) VALUE '    MARCH'.
                 15               PIC X(9) VALUE '    APRIL'.
                 15               PIC X(9) VALUE '      MAY'.
                 15               PIC X(9) VALUE '     JUNE'.
                 15               PIC X(9) VALUE '     JULY'.
                 15               PIC X(9) VALUE '   AUGUST'.
                 15               PIC X(9) VALUE 'SEPTEMBER'.
                 15               PIC X(9) VALUE '  OCTOBER'.
                 15               PIC X(9) VALUE ' NOVEMBER'.
                 15               PIC X(9) VALUE ' DECEMBER'.
              10 MONTH-TABLE REDEFINES MONTHS-OF-THE-YEAR.
                 15 MONTH-NAME    PIC X(9) OCCURS 12.
           05 PRINT-DATE.
              10                  PIC X(02)   VALUE SPACES.
              10 O-TYPE-DATE      PIC X(16).
              10                  PIC X(02)   VALUE SPACES.
              10 O-MM             PIC X(09).
              10                  PIC X       VALUE SPACE.
              10 O-DD             PIC Z9.
              10                  PIC X(02)   VALUE ', '.
              10 O-YYYY           PIC 9(04).
           05 PRINT-TIME.
              10                  PIC X(02)   VALUE SPACES.
              10                  PIC X(16)   VALUE '    SYSTEM TIME:'.
              10                  PIC X(12)   VALUE SPACES.
              10 O-HH             PIC Z9.
              10                  PIC X       VALUE ':'.
              10 O-MIN            PIC Z9.
              10                  PIC X       VALUE ':'.
              10 O-SS             PIC Z9.

      /
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       MAIN SECTION.
           PERFORM E000-INIT THRU
                   E000-INIT-EXIT.
           PERFORM 1000-PROCESS THRU 1000-PROCESS-EXIT
                   UNTIL I-FILE1-AT-EOF.
           PERFORM X000-EOJ THRU
                   X000-EOJ-EXIT.
           STOP RUN.

      /
       0000-INIT SECTION.
       E000-INIT.
           ACCEPT WS-SYS-DATE FROM DATE
           ACCEPT WS-SYS-TIME FROM TIME

           IF SYS-YY > 50 THEN
              MOVE 19 TO Y2K-SYS-CENT
           ELSE
              MOVE 20 TO Y2K-SYS-CENT
           END-IF

           OPEN INPUT  IN-FILE1
                       I-CARD-FILE
                OUTPUT O-FILE1.
           READ I-CARD-FILE INTO WS-CARD-REC
             AT END
                CLOSE I-CARD-FILE
                PERFORM X000-EOJ THRU X000-EOJ-EXIT
                DISPLAY '*** CARD FILE EMPTY ***'
                MOVE 16 TO RETURN-CODE
                STOP RUN
             NOT AT END
                CLOSE I-CARD-FILE
           END-READ
           READ IN-FILE1
             AT END
                PERFORM X000-EOJ THRU X000-EOJ-EXIT
                DISPLAY ' '
                DISPLAY '*** INPUT FILE IS EMPTY ***'
                DISPLAY '*** MAKE SURE THERE ARE NO RECS ***'
                DISPLAY ' '
                MOVE 16 TO RETURN-CODE
                STOP RUN
           END-READ
           .

       E000-INIT-EXIT.
           EXIT.

      /
       X000-EOJ.
           DISPLAY '=============================================='
           DISPLAY '  '
           DISPLAY ' PROGRAM FUNCTION                   | PGMSHELL'
           DISPLAY '----------------------------------------------'
           DISPLAY '  '
           DISPLAY WS-HEADING-1
           DISPLAY '  '

           MOVE '    SYSTEM DATE:' TO O-TYPE-DATE
           MOVE MONTH-NAME(SYS-MM) TO O-MM
           MOVE SYS-DD TO O-DD
           MOVE WS-Y2K-SYS-DATE TO O-YYYY
           DISPLAY PRINT-DATE

           MOVE SYS-HH       TO O-HH
           MOVE SYS-MIN      TO O-MIN
           MOVE SYS-SS       TO O-SS
           DISPLAY PRINT-TIME

           MOVE I-FILE1-CTR  TO D-I-CTR
           MOVE O-FILE1-CTR  TO D-O-CTR

           DISPLAY '  '
           DISPLAY 'FILE IN CNTR   = ' D-I-CTR
           DISPLAY 'FILE OUT CNTR  = ' D-O-CTR
           DISPLAY '  '
           DISPLAY '----------------------------------------------'
           DISPLAY '  '
           IF I-FILE1-CTR = O-FILE1-CTR THEN
              DISPLAY 'COUNTERS BALANCE'
           ELSE
              DISPLAY '****COUNTERS OUT-OF-BALANCE*****'
           END-IF
           DISPLAY '  '
           DISPLAY '=============================================='

           CLOSE IN-FILE1,
                 O-FILE1.

       X000-EOJ-EXIT.
           EXIT.

      /
      ************************************************************
      **   PGM PROCESS ROUTINE                                  **
      ************************************************************
       1000-INPUT-PROCESS SECTION.
       1000-PROCESS.
           ADD 1 TO I-FILE1-CTR

           INITIALIZE O-FILE1-REC

*******    DO WHATEVER HERE
*******    PROCESS CODE GOES HERE
*******


           WRITE O-FILE1-REC
           ADD 1 TO O-FILE1-CTR

           READ IN-FILE1
           .

       1000-PROCESS-EXIT.
           EXIT.





> -----------------
> when you have a series of statements based on an if statement the if
…[50 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-c3102504d1"></a>
## Thread: Can the COBOL function current-date be used in a CICS program — 8 this month / 10 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates)_

### Can the COBOL function current-date be used in a CICS program

- **From:** "Kevin" <kevinek1@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-18T04:50:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sCu96.60959$55.9515063@news1.rdc1.ne.home.com>`

```
Here is a quick example:
MOVE FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE TO WA-HOLD-DATE

I ask this because EIBDATE is in Julian format

TIA,
Kevin
```

#### ↳ Re: Can the COBOL function current-date be used in a CICS program

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-17T23:19:06-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<945uba$omd$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<sCu96.60959$55.9515063@news1.rdc1.ne.home.com>`

```
Yes, as this is an alphanumeric (and not a numeric) Intrinsic Function, it
may be used as the sending operand in a MOVE statement (in any COBOL
compiler that includes ANSI/ISO conforming support for Intrinsic Functions).
Your COBOL documentation will tell you what format this will be in.  You
*may* be interested in just the first 8 bytes (and not want to use "default"
truncations) - so you may be interested in:

 Move Function Current-Date (1:8) to WA-HOLD-DATE

or if you don't want the century (after all you won't be around for the Y3K
problem <G>) you could code:

 Move Function Current-Date (3:6) to WA-HOLD-DATE
```

#### ↳ Re: Can the COBOL function current-date be used in a CICS program

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-18T04:59:20-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<946e7c$3kt$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<sCu96.60959$55.9515063@news1.rdc1.ne.home.com>`

```
Kevin,

        Yes it can. CURRENT-DATE pulls two calls. One to CEELOCT and the
other to CEEGMTO, neither of which use any MVS Macro services.

        Alternatively, you could always issue an ABSTIME ABSTIME followed by
a FORMATTIME ABSTIME, where you can specify a plethora of different date
formats.

HTH....

Bill

"Kevin" <kevinek1@home.com> wrote in message
news:sCu96.60959$55.9515063@news1.rdc1.ne.home.com...
> Here is a quick example:
> MOVE FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE TO WA-HOLD-DATE
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>

```
On 18 Jan 2001 14:26:04 GMT scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock) wrote:

:>Binyamin Dissen writes ...

:>>On Thu, 18 Jan 2001 04:50:32 GMT "Kevin" <kevinek1@home.com> wrote:

:>>:>Here is a quick example:
:>>:>MOVE FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE TO WA-HOLD-DATE

:>>:>I ask this because EIBDATE is in Julian format

:>>It may violate your local standards.

:>>Why not use ASKTIME / FORMATTIME?

:>Binyamin, what are ASKTIME / FORMATTIME? Never heard of them; they seem more
:>likely to violate locat standards for more people than intrinsic functions that
:>are part of the official standard.

They are the standard CICS methods of getting of formatting the date and time.

As the original poster referred to EIBDATE, it would seem obvious that this is
for a CICS transaction.
```
