## Thread: COBOL'ers of tomorrow - PLEASE read and act now !! — 2 msgs

### COBOL'ers of tomorrow - PLEASE read and act now !!

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-18T00:15:43-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9461l8$v5a$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
If you think that COBOL may be in your future (your shop's future, or even
creating your paychecks <G>) for the next few years, please read the
following AND act upon it.

The FCD (Final Committee Draft) of the next COBOL revision is now available
and desperately (IMHO) needs reviewing.

You can get a copy (download) from:

     http://www.ncits.org/tc_home/j4.htm

Look for the line:

 COBOL FCD (6.8 MB) or  Compressed Self-Extracting File (2 MB)

Time is OF THE ESSENCE in reviewing this document and getting your input
(good, bad, whatever) to the appropriate people.  The official "review
period" will begin (unless something changes between now and then) on
February 1, 2001 and last 4 months (internationally) but only 3 months (for
US Public Review - subject to possible modification).  I will point "you
all" to the official Press Release when it becomes available - but I wanted
to give a "heads up" as soon as this document became generally available.

If you are in the US (or wish to submit your comments via US-only channels),
then you will need to send them to NCITS - details on how to do this will be
in the Press Release - when available.

   HOWEVER,

if you do not live/work in the US and/or are a citizen of another country
and are interested in submitting your comments thru your own (non-US)
national Standards body (and I strongly, STRONGLY recommend this), then go
to the following page to get "contact information" - and find out ASAP how
and when they will be taking comments:

 http://www.iso.ch/addresse/membodies.html

  ***

So much for "preface" - now let me make some suggestions (and these are ONLY
personal opinions) on how and what to do for your "personal" review of what
will happen to COBOL in the near, medium-near, and somewhat far futures.

Step 1: (critical)
  Contact your COBOL compiler "vendor of choice".  Find out:
    - how much resource THEY are spending on reviewing this document - both
for its "ease" of implementation - but also for any compatibility issues tha
t it will be causing THEIR customers
    - what their current position is on whether or not they think they will
ever implement this version of COBOL - in part or whole.  Let them KNOW
whether or not this is important to you - and how much you will PAY for it.
    - whether they (the vendor) or any of its user groups plan on providing
"consolidated" input to the review process.

(If they aren't reviewing this document now, you can be pretty certain that
either they won't be providing a conforming compiler in the short-term - or
that you and they will BOTH be unhappy with what they will be required to
provide.)

Step 2: (minimum - if you are interested in what MAY happen to COBOL in the
next few years)
 Download a copy of the draft Standard - and if nothing else review the two
appendices:
    - D.1 Substantive changes potentially affecting existing programs
    - D.2 Substantive changes not affecting existing programs

the first section will tell you about changes that MIGHT "change behavior"
in your existing programs; the second section will tell you (at a HIGH
level) what the major enhancements in this revision are.

Step 3: (if the last step "got you interested at all")
 Look at the section
    - Concepts

This section "describes" (in as non-technical as possible - which is still
pretty technical - way) many of the old and new features included in this
revision of COBOL.  Some (not as many as some would want) examples are even
included.

Step 4: (If you are a "do-er" and not someone who thinks that someone else
will make YOUR life easy/better)
  REVIEW the document in as much or as little detail as you want.
  Formulate your "comments" and send them to your national body (not to me,
not to J4, not to any electronic newsgroup or distribution list, but to your
national Standards body) and do this as FAR before the end of April 2001 as
possible.

REAL COMMENTS from REAL reviewers DO make a difference - and they DO get
looked at in detail (or at least in as much detail as you provide "us").

   ***

Some comments and suggestions on what *I* think will be "good and useful"
comments.

A) This document is pretty much at the "final" stage of development.  It is
HIGHLY unlikely that new features or enhancements to existing features will
be added at this time (unless "you" can justify that it would be worth
waiting another 2-5 years to get ANY revision of the '85/'89/'92 Standard
out.)

PLEASE do not use this review period to make suggestions of "nice to have"
additions for THIS revision of the Standard.  If there is something that you
just MUST tell people to think about adding, I strongly suggest that you
CLEARLY document this as an idea for a (possibly the next) revision of
COBOL.  Better yet, create a separate document with these "ideas" and send
them to the chair of the J4 COBOL committee marked "candidates for a future
revision" and include as much or as little detail as you think appropriate.
He can be reached at:

  doncobol <at> mediaone.net

B) It really is useful to say that you think that the whole document is
"swell" and you wouldn't change a thing (or at least wouldn't change a thing
if that means a "delay" in getting the Standard approved and out).  You can
even add "minor" editorial issues, suggestions, to such a comment.  However,
please do NOT assume that everyone in the world thinks that this is a great
idea (or even a worthwhile revision).  Therefore, it is important to hear
from people who think it is - unless you fall into the other category, see
below.

C) If you think that this revision should NOT be approved and published as
an ISO Standard, this too is useful information to convey.  However, it is
important that you ALSO tell why you think this.  Don't just be general and
say that "COBOL is dead" or something similar, but give specific reasons why
you think that the "programming world" will be a BETTER place (even for
COBOL programmers) if this revision is NOT adopted as an International
Standard.  (FYI - if you have read this far, this is the category that I fal
l in - but I won't go into details on that in this note. I will in future
input.)

D) ALL editorial notes are useful, but be aware that the ISO "drafting
rules" are specific and some things that are done in this document MUST be
done this way.  However, it is particularly useful to hear about additional
index entries that would be helpful (and where they should point to) or
cross-references and index entries that are pointing to the wrong place.
Similarly, if you find sentences or sections that seem to have been "messed
up" in editing (and don't make any sense), this too may be useful
information.  It really does help if you tell what the correction is and NOT
just point out what the problem is.  (For non-native English speakers this
may be difficult - but often if something doesn't make sense to you, it is
more important to get your editorial comments identifying problems than it
is to get comments who can "guess" at what was intended.)

E) PLEASE tell "us" about any technical errors that you find.  Not so much
things that are "fuzzy" but things where two (or more) parts of the Standard
conflict with each other - or where one part assumes something that another
says cannot happen. Similarly, parts of the draft that are "impossible" to
implement or otherwise technically "wrong". Again, the more specific you can
be (and the more you can provide "corrections") the more likely such
comments are to be "accepted" and acted upon.

F) If you do find "fuzzy" or unclear sentences (or sections), don't just say
that they were unclear but give SPECIFIC suggestions on how they should be
re-worded.  (Make certain that you clearly state which part of the draft -
page, section, and paragraph - you are talking about.  This is really true
for ALL comments that you submit).

G) If you think that there is something SO awful or so seriously flawed that
you think that it should be REMOVED from the draft Standard, this has a
"little" better chance of happening than adding something new.  HOWEVER,
almost everything in this document has been fought over for over half a
decade (some for close to a decade now) - so getting it removed would
require some PRETTY STRONG evidence of a critical problem (not just a
preference - or the way that you or your operating environment works today).

  ***

A special note about "spreading the word" - as well as spreading the
document.

PLEASE feel free to forward this document to as many people as you think
MIGHT be interested and willing to do ANY work on reviewing (and commenting)
on this document. You should, however, be aware that page ii of this
document states,

"This ISO document is a draft International Standard and is
copyright-protected by ISO. Except as permitted under the applicable laws of
the user's country, neither this ISO draft nor any extract from it may be
reproduced, stored in a retrieval system or transmitted in any form or by
any means, electronic, photocopying, recording or otherwise, without prior
written permission being secured. ..."

*HOWEVER* page xx states,

"Any organization interested in reproducing the COBOL standard and
specifications in whole or in part, using ideas from this document as the
basis for an instruction manual or for any other purpose, is free to do so.
..."

If you work in a "large" and/or "legalistic" environment, I suggest that you
contact *YOUR* personal legal authorities and/or advisors for an
interpretation on how these two statements (and their surrounding text)
impact you and your distribution of the document itself.

   ***

Happy reviewing - and please feel free to send me personal or public
questions if you need help in understanding what YOU can do and why I am
asking for you to do so.
```

```
>
```

```
> for the COBOL standard edition.  It should also be in the vs 3.0 supplied
> with Thane's book and the vs 4.0 supplied in Carol Baroudi's book.

You should know that PowerCobol 3 and PowerCobol 4 are completely

mechanisms.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PowerCOBOL

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-01-19T12:26:11+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<94816c$kef$2@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<946kd5$2qaf$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <20010118093117.06978.00000656@nso-fd.aol.com> <3A67FCEE.49543C9A@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

Richard Plinston wrote in message <3A67FCEE.49543C9A@Azonic.co.nz>...
>>
>> I think that it is included in the vs 3.0 download from www.adtools.com
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>mechanisms.

You should also know that it makes very little difference. Version 4
supports structured COBOL event coding just as Version 3 does.

I migrated everything from Version 3 to Version 4 without incident when
Version 4 was released. The Object Oriented extensions in Version 4 are
OPTIONAL and it is certainly misleading to suggest that a "completely
different" way of coding is REQUIRED. (Some of the Objects and Methods
provided had their names changed in Version 4, but with a global edit the
impact is minimal.)

Now in Version 5, I DO use the OO extensions for building COM components. (I
am also experimenting with OO COBOL in COBOL97, as I believe this will be
the future direction of COBOL).

Building ActiveX (COM) components is the ONLY place in PowerCOBOL where OO
COBOL is REQUIRED. Even then, you can avoid using OO if you simply "wrap"
structured code in a PowerCOBOL COM Wrapper.

(I am not suggesting that you SHOULD "avoid OO COBOL", merely that you CAN.)

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: powercobol 6 - common dialog

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-19T00:14:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hFL96.1201$k26.77328@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3a67285a.32670457@news.skynet.be>`

```
Sure. We use it a lot (or at least save/save as and printer).

In the form design area, right click on the controls toolbar. Pick
Custom Controls from the pop-up menu. Check Microsoft Common Dialog
Control 6.0. This adds the control to the toolbar. After that, use as
you would any other control. But be prepared for the oddities:

For example, PowerCobol does not recognize when the user selects the
'Cancel' button on a control. So, in printer selection for example,
your program must ask the user whether he's ready to print because
your program cannot detect whether the user selected the 'Cancel'
button in the Common Dialog control. FJ says they'll get that fixed
about Version 7(!).


"Alain Reymond" <alain.reymond-nospam@ceia.com> wrote in message
news:3a67285a.32670457@news.skynet.be...
> Does someone knows the way to call the microsoft common dialog 6
from
> Power Cobol ?
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: powercobol 6 - common dialog

- **From:** alain.reymond-nospam@ceia.com (Alain Reymond)
- **Date:** 2001-01-19T08:34:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3a67f4d0.783316@news.skynet.be>`
- **References:** `<3a67285a.32670457@news.skynet.be> <hFL96.1201$k26.77328@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Thank you for your indications. I immediately discovered what you
explain in your second paragraph. I am unable to control if the user
pressed OK or Cancel except using the return-code status.

First activate the CancelError property. 
Then, call the common dialog and immediately after, control the
return-code status. If the user selected Ok, the return-code value is
0, if he selected Cancel, the return-code is not set to 0.

The only problem is that you first receive a warning message :
"CommonDialog. Cancel was selected."
Just press Ok and ignore the message.

It seems to work. Hereunder is a example:

 01  ReturnValue         PIC S9(4) COMP-5.
 01  DisplayReturnValue1  pic +9999999999.
 01  DisplayReturnValue2  pic +9999999999.
 01  STYL    PIC S9(9) COMP-5.
 01  M-TITLE PIC X(15) VALUE "Message".
 01  RET     PIC S9(9) COMP-5.
 01  str     pic x(80).
 PROCEDURE       DIVISION.

     COMPUTE STYL = POW-DMOK + POW-DMNOICON
     
     move POW-True to "CancelError" of CommonDialog1
     INVOKE CommonDialog1 "ShowOpen"
     move return-code to DisplayReturnValue2
     move "CancelError" of CommonDialog1 to DisplayReturnValue1
     string 
     	"CancelError : "
     	DisplayReturnValue1
     	" return-code : "
     	DisplayReturnValue2
     	delimited by size
     	into str
     end-string
     INVOKE POW-SELF "DisplayMessage"
         USING str M-TITLE STYL
         RETURNING RET

A bient�t ...

>Sure. We use it a lot (or at least save/save as and printer).
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>about Version 7(!).
```

```
I see what you mean: RETURN-CODE of 0 = OK, non-zero = Cancel.

I don't trust a RETURN-CODE of -147352567 (and other miscellaneous
numbers).

Especially after an extended dialog in this group over 0=false and 1 =
true (or was it the reverse?).

Anyway, I hope you enjoy the CommonDialog control.
```

## Thread: MF COBOL & memory allocation & API's — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MF COBOL & memory allocation & API's

- **From:** tracey@bframe.com
- **Date:** 2001-01-18T21:08:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<947m0l$s9p$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I'm having trouble with a program that once worked and I'm trying to
find that needle in a hay stack.

I have NT and just applied SP6 when this problem immediately began.

I have a MF COBOL program that is making API calls.  All calls work
fine except one.  It worked, now fails.  Preceding the call I allocate
space using a cobol call, which works fine.  This space is then
associated with a pointer that is one of several variables in one of
several parameters in the problematic call.

The error code for the call is 'invalid parameter', but doesn't tell me
which one.

I need to know if there are any special issues to be had with allocated
space in regards to these two languages (the API's are in C).

Any ideas are most welcome.
```

```
Perhaps you could tell us what API and possibly show us your code. I had
problems with a change Microsoft made to the common controls library after
installing Internet Explorer 5.01 (which replaced COMCTL32.DLL). It turned
out that my code, which worked, did not adhere to Microsoft's documentation.
The newer version of COMCTL32.DLL was less forgiving.
```

```
...
       RECEIVE-MESSAGES.

           MOVE ZEROS              TO SUBM.

      ******************************************************************
      *    BODY SIZE MESSAGE
      *
           ADD 1                   TO SUBM.
           MOVE PROPID-M-BODY-SIZE TO myOrigPropID (SUBM).
           MOVE VT-UI4             TO vartype of myOrigPropVar (SUBM).

           MOVE SUBM               TO BODY-SUB.

      ******************************************************************
      *    BODY MESSAGE
      *
           ADD 1                   TO SUBM.
           MOVE PROPID-M-BODY      TO myOrigPropID (SUBM).
           MOVE VT-VECTOR-VT-UI1   TO vartype of myOrigPropVar (SUBM).
           MOVE 60000              TO BodyBufferSize.
           MOVE BodyBufferSize     TO celems OF myOrigPropVar (SUBM).

           MOVE 2                  TO BodyBufferFlag.
           CALL "CBL_ALLOC_MEM"
                       BY REFERENCE pBodyBufferSize
                       BY VALUE     BodyBufferSize
                       BY VALUE     BodyBufferFlag
               RETURNING            RETURN-CODE.

           IF RETURN-CODE > 0
               DISPLAY "ALLOCATE MEMEORY CALL FAILED - GET HELP".

           DISPLAY "ALLOCATED POINTER: " pBodyBufferSize.
           SET ADDRESS OF AllocatedBody TO pBodyBufferSize.
           MOVE pBodyBufferSize-char TO pelems of myOrigPropVar (SUBM).


      ******************************************************************
      *    LABEL MESSAGE LENGTH
      *
           ADD 1                   TO SUBM.
           MOVE PROPID-M-LABEL-LEN TO myOrigPropID (SUBM).
           MOVE VT-UI4             TO vartype OF myOrigPropVar (SUBM).
           MOVE 256                TO lpwstr  OF myOrigPropVar (SUBM).

           MOVE SUBM               TO LABEL-SUB.


      ******************************************************************
      *    LABEL MESSAGE

           ADD 1                   TO SUBM.
           MOVE PROPID-M-LABEL     TO myOrigPropID (SUBM).
           MOVE VT-LPWSTR          TO vartype OF myOrigPropVar (SUBM).
           SET  lpwstr-label-p     TO ADDRESS OF WS-MSG-LABEL-UNICODE.
           MOVE lpwstr-Label-p-char TO lpwstr OF myOrigPropVar (SUBM).


     ******************************************************************
      *    SET FINAL STRUCTURE MQMSGPROPS

           MOVE SUBM               TO cProp-ctr of myOrigMQMSGPROPS.
           SET aPropID of myOrigMQMSGPROPS
                                   TO ADDRESS OF myOrigPropID   (1).
           SET aPropVar of myOrigMQMSGPROPS
                                   TO ADDRESS OF myOrigPropVar  (1).
           SET aStatus OF myOrigMQMSGPROPS
                                   TO ADDRESS OF myOrigPropStatus (1).
           SET myOrigMQMSGPROPS-P  TO ADDRESS OF myOrigMQMSGPROPS.


           CALL WINAPI "MQReceiveMessage"
               USING
                   BY VALUE       OP-ORIGHANDLE
5 min              BY VALUE       300000
                   BY VALUE       RC-RECACTION
                   BY VALUE       myOrigMQMSGPROPS-p
                   BY VALUE       x"00"
                   BY VALUE       x"00"
CURSOR             BY VALUE       x"00"
                   BY VALUE       MQ-NO-TRANSACTION
               GIVING
                   THE-RESULT.

      ****FREE ALLOCATED MEMORY SPACE
           CALL "CBL_FREE_MEM"
               USING   BY VALUE     pBodyBufferSize
               RETURNING            RETURN-CODE.

           IF RETURN-CODE > 0
               DISPLAY "FREE MEMORY CALL FAILED - GET HELP".

       RECEIVE-MESSAGES-EXIT.
           EXIT.
...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MF COBOL & memory allocation & API's

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-19T16:52:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oh_96.188412$59.50061996@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<947m0l$s9p$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <949gvq$c1c$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Here's the call as documented by Microsoft:

HRESULT APIENTRY MQReceiveMessage(
  QUEUEHANDLE hSource,
  DWORD dwTimeout,
  DWORD dwAction,
  MQMSGPROPS pMessageProps,
  LPOVERLAPPED lpOverlapped,
  PMQRECEIVECALLBACK fnReceiveCallback,
  HANDLE hCursor,
  Transaction *pTransaction
);


Here's what you have:

>            CALL WINAPI "MQReceiveMessage"
>                USING
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>                    BY VALUE       MQ-NO-TRANSACTION   GIVING  THE-RESULT.


Here's what you should probably have:

        CALL WINAPI "MQReceiveMessage" USING
            BY VALUE
                                OP-ORIGHANDLE
                                30000 size 4
                                RC-RECACTION
            BY REFERENCE
                                myOrigMQMSGPROPS
            BY VALUE 0 size 4
                                0 size 4
                                0 size 4
            BY VALUE MQ-NO-TRANSACTION GIVING THE-RESULT.


Karl


<tracey@bframe.com> wrote in message news:949gvq$c1c$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> The call is MQReceiveMessage
>
…[285 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF COBOL & memory allocation & API's

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-19T20:40:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WC1a6.189486$59.50259806@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<947m0l$s9p$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <949gvq$c1c$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <oh_96.188412$59.50061996@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <949u6t$op4$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In answer to your question, yes I am familiar with memory allocation
although I haven't used CBL_ALLOC_MEM in version 4.0 of MF COBOL on NT
because the (old) documentation claims that it is only supported for GNT and
INT, and I always compile to native code when using that compiler. As well,
since INT and GNT require the runtime, I cannot be sure how MERANT
implemented the CBL_ALLOC_MEM function, so the pointer returned may not be
usable by the Win32 API. Instead I use the Win32 API functions:
LocalAlloc(), LocalLock(), LocalUnlock() and LocalFree().

In terms of your program, I am unsure why you are bothering to allocate
memory since it would be far easier to declare 'AllocatedBody' in
WORKING-STORAGE and use a single "SET my-pointer to TO ADDRESS OF ..."
statement to get the address for setting the 'pelems' variable.

However, your original message states that you were having a problem with a
call parameter  which you assume to be related to your memory allocation
code. I haven't made that assumption.

Karl

> suggestion and a number of additional combinations based on your
```

```
I'd appreciate it

Tom Wright
```

#### ↳ Re: COM components in cobol

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-01-19T12:12:52+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<94816b$kef$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<X_I96.478$9r5.83468@news1.primary.net>`

```
Tom Wright wrote in message ...
>>>
>Is it possible to do COM in cobol?  Has anyone done this?  I have MF cobol
>4.032 on a winnt workstation.  If anyone can point me to a snippet of code
>I'd appreciate it
<<<
I have been doing this for just over 2 years now and it is a major part of
my ongoing work.

I wouldn't now develop systems any other way than by connecting components.

You will need a compiler that supports OO COBOL and, as this is still in its
infancy, it is worth getting to grips with Object Orientation concepts (if
you haven't already) so that you can evaluate what is going to be most
useful for you.

As you are in a MF environment you will probably progress to Merant's Net
Express which does support COM and OO COBOL.

However, in my opinion, the best environment for developing components in
COBOL is Fujitsu PowerCOBOL which caters SPECIFICALLY for building ActiveX
components and is ideal for an entry level to the exciting world of
components. PowerCOBOL is intrinsically Object Oriented and fits easily and
naturally into the component world.

If you don't like the sound of building components using OO COBOL, you CAN
put a COM "wrapper" around structured code (at least, you can with Fujitsu;
I'm not sure what NE provides in this area).

While this may make the purists shudder, it is a very effective way to
"leverage" your existing structured modular COBOL into "pluggable"
components which can be dropped into Windows Forms and Web Pages.

Good luck with your endeavours. Mail me privately if you need help or want
to clarify anything.

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: COM components in cobol

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)

- **References:** `<X_I96.478$9r5.83468@news1.primary.net>`

```
That version of MF COBOL is probably lacking the necessary support for
COM - however, Fujitsu COBOL allows you "dp COM" both as a client and
as a server.  NetExpress 3.1 also allows this, if I recall correctly.
```

#### ↳ Re: COM components in cobol

- **From:** "Neil Hayes" <NeilH@syspro.co.za>
- **Date:** 2001-01-22T13:47:23+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3a6c1d69$0$226@helios.is.co.za>`
- **References:** `<X_I96.478$9r5.83468@news1.primary.net>`

```
I've just started developing COM using NetExpress, firstly taking some of
their supplied examples then building on it, depending on what you want to
do in terms of your COM design it can be hard in getting examples of exactly
what you want to do.

One thing that perhaps I'm not keen on is the editing of the idl files, each
dll has several idl file that get compiled to make up the type library,
adding a method in NetExpress updates these idl files but when you need to
remove a method or property you have to delete it from the .idl files and
the header file (.h).

The other thing regarding the idl files are if you create a method that
perhaps has one in parameter, "dothis(theparameter) returning hResult, in VB
it comes out like "dothis(p0 as string) As string" so you'll have to edit
the idl files to provide a more meaning full explanation and some help.

Otherwise help is at hand via newsgroups like this or Merant's Answerlabs
time permitting.

Regards

Neil
"Tom Wright" <twright@@larimore.net> wrote in message
```

<a id="t-06f952a39c"></a>
## Thread: Isn't this weird? — 1 msgs

### Isn't this weird?

- **From:** "Jacques Vidal" <jvidal@mail.dotcom.fr>
- **Date:** 2001-01-19T14:45:50+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<949gje$3l1$1@wanadoo.fr>`

```
Hello,

I have this strange problem compiling COBOL programs with OCDS 4.1.30 on my
RedHat 6.1 Linux system (both are supposed to support shared libraries):

-------------------------------- Makefile

ALL: COBFUNC.so CALLER

COBFUNC.so: COBFUNC.cbl
 cob -Q -shared -x -o $@ $<

CALLER: CALLER.cbl
 cob -x -o $@ $< COBFUNC.so

-------------------------------- CALLER.cbl

       PROGRAM-ID. CALLER.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  X PIC 9(9) VALUE 123456789.
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
      *     ACCEPT X FROM COMMAND-LINE.
           DISPLAY "CMD=" X.
           CALL "COBFUNC".
           DISPLAY "Done.".
           STOP RUN.

-------------------------------- COBFUNC.cbl

       PROGRAM-ID. COBFUNC.
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
           DISPLAY "in COBFUNC".
           EXIT PROGRAM.


When I run 'make', both programs compile/link flawlessly, and the CALLER
program runs fine.

However, if I uncomment the 'ACCEPT ...' statement in CALLER.cbl, 'make'
issues the

following warnings:

ld: warning: type and size of dynamic symbol `_mF4701' are not defined
ld: warning: type and size of dynamic symbol `_mF3331' are not defined
ld: warning: type and size of dynamic symbol `_mF4701' are not defined
ld: warning: type and size of dynamic symbol `_mF3331' are not defined

and when I try to run the CALLER program:

Execution error : file 'CALLER'
error code: 115, pc=0, call=1, seg=0
115     Unexpected signal (Signal 4)

The problem disappears if I modify "COBFUNC" to make it call another
subprogram "COBFUNC2":

-------------------------------- COBFUNC.cbl (second version)

       PROGRAM-ID. COBFUNC.
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
           DISPLAY "in COBFUNC".
           CALL "COBFUNC2".
           EXIT PROGRAM.

-------------------------------- COBFUNC2.cbl

       PROGRAM-ID. COBFUNC2.
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
           DISPLAY "in COBFUNC2".
           EXIT PROGRAM.

-------------------------------- Makefile (updated)

ALL: COBFUNC2.so COBFUNC.so CALLER

COBFUNC2.so: COBFUNC2.cbl
 cob -Q -shared -x -o $@ $<

COBFUNC.so: COBFUNC.cbl
 cob -Q -shared -x -o $@ $< COBFUNC2.so

CALLER: CALLER.cbl
 cob -x -o $@ $< COBFUNC.so



Anyone understand what happens here?

TIA,

Jacques Vidal
```

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2000/07/02
Version: 3.01

Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Sunday, July 2, 2000

The COBOL FAQ has moved.  To view the most recent version, please check out the
web version at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send private
email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

The following is Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

 o Recorded new location of the FAQ
 o Updated some old (bad) web links
 o Added some new COBOL books (particularly those written or contributed to - by
frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).
 o Added information about LegacyJ (PERCobol) and changed the company name from
"Synkronix" to "LegacyJ".
 o Updated information for changes in product and company names and addresses
where available
 o Updated the URLs for downloading the draft of the next COBOL Standard and
CobCy
 o Started the Samples/Example Section
 o Tried to get rid of most specific references to Windows/NT, Windows/95,
Windows/98, and Windows/2000.
 o Added links to Xinotech
 o Added information on Norcom and GUI ScreenIO
 o Added (back) information on Wang
 o Added (back) information on the Tiny-COBOL ('74 Standard) project
```

## Thread: Create MVS file? — 17 this month / 18 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Create MVS file?

- **From:** Angel <angel@smart.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-20T21:41:05-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Pine.LNX.4.10.10101202125470.26751-100000@smarty.smart.net>`

```

I would like to create a file on an MVS system. 
Something like this: 

SELECT XYZ-FILE
    ASSIGN TO WS-XYZ-FILE-NAME
    ORGANIZATION IS SEQUENTIAL
    FILE STATUS IS XYZ-STATUS. 

...

01  WS-XYZ-FILE-NAME    PIC X(100). 

...


DISPLAY "PLEASE ENTER XYZ FILE NAME: ".
ACCEPT WS-XYZ-FILE-NAME.
OPEN OUTPUT XYZ-FILE. 

The point is that I don't know the file name
until run time.  Is this possible?  What about
the DD statement? 

If it is not possible, that's okay.  But please
please let me know so I can figure out another 
way to work around the problem.
```

#### ↳ Re: Create MVS file?

- **From:** "DBuck" <dbuck@prairieinet.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-21T00:05:21-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<94du0k$d2jvm$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de>`
- **References:** `<Pine.LNX.4.10.10101202125470.26751-100000@smarty.smart.net>`

```

Angel <angel@smart.net> wrote in message
news:Pine.LNX.4.10.10101202125470.26751-100000@smarty.smart.net...
>
> I would like to create a file on an MVS system.
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>

They allow you to accept from the console????

Anyway, what you are talking about would require a dynamic allocation
routine.  The DD would be assigned at run time through an SVC99 call (thus,
not coded in the JCL).  Your SELECT/ASSIGN and FD would like this:

SELECT  DYNAMIC-FILE-IN    ASSIGN TO UT-S-FILEIN
                                                      FILE STATUS IS
FILEIN-STATUS.

FD  DYNAMIC-FILE-IN
       RECORDING MODE IS F
       BLOCK CONTAIN 0 RECORDS
       LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD.

01  FILEIN-RECORD                  PIC X(100).

If the file can be different sizes, or even different formats (such as
variable), then it becomes a little more difficult (although still
possible).  You may even want to consider an Assembler I/O module.

If this is work, I suggest that you talk to your senior programmers or
system programmers.  If this is school, perhaps you should take this to your
instructor....

Dave
```

#### ↳ Re: Create MVS file?

- **From:** Angel <angel@smart.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-22T21:46:34-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Pine.LNX.4.10.10101222119260.30119-100000@smarty.smart.net>`

- **References:** `<Pine.LNX.4.10.10101202125470.26751-100000@smarty.smart.net>`

```
I wasn't clear.  Let's leave the ACCEPT statement out,
and say that the user has a way of getting a selection
into the program without a problem. 

What I imagine is that the user enters a selection 
and the program reads the database and creates from 1 
to several thousand text files.

The user then uses the text files for another process.

Here is my problem.  I can't define thousands of DD
statements for these output files.  New file names would
be created daily, so I simply can't maintain DD statements
this way.  I don't even know the names of the files until
the user has selected the ones they want.

I want the program to just create these files.

I see a file transfer program upload thousands of text files 
to the MVS from Windows NT and it creates the files without 
DD statements.  How do I do that? 

> 
> I would like to create a file on an MVS system. 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

OK, not so easy. But another idea, if the originator of the question
knows C, but not ASSEMBLER. It is easy to call a C-program. And it is
easy to fopen, fwrite and fclose to a sequential or PDS file from C.
Dynalloc is 'natural' in C, just fopen a DSN instead of DD:ddname.
```

```
Quite by accident (meaning I wasn't looking for it) I found something posted by
Steve Comstock sometime in 1998 on this very topic. It involves calling an

program. A deja.com search should turn it up for anyone who's really interested.

LiamD
```

```
Thanks to all for your responses.  You've given me the direction
I need to go forward.

The application is already written in COBOL.
I want to maintain just one set of code as much as possible.  
I want to upload it to MVS.  Luckily, all of the I/O statements
in question are in a single paragraph.  All I have to 
do is to make that paragraph a copyfile.  On the MVS the 
copyfile could be replaced with CALL's to do the SVC99.
That way I can maintain one program on both systems.

Thats what I envision anyway.  Wish me luck. 

Thanks again for all of your help.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Create MVS file?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-23T12:56:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Dbfb6.441$ek2.23632@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<Pine.LNX.4.10.10101202125470.26751-100000@smarty.smart.net> <Pine.LNX.4.10.10101222119260.30119-100000@smarty.smart.net>`

```
Try searching at www.mvshelp.com for "SVC99" in the "programming languages"
section. (I'd do it now, but I am writing this off-line). Failing that, try
Gilbert St-Fleur's web site http://members.home.net/gsf/     He has all
kinds of neat suff.

I think you'll find some code you can use from COBOL to handle this.
Basically, you CALL a routine, passing the DDname (you only need one DD) and
the DSN plus some parameters (NEW, MOD, SHR, space, UNIT,, etc) and the file
becomes available to you.

I have a routine at one of my client sites which is in production now, but I
have not personally tested it. (Yes, I know: if it's in production, why do I
need to test it? Because I am paranoid about it, that's why!)
```

```
Rather than letting an assembler subroutine modify the job file control
block or some other supervisor call, can you submit a job using the
internal reader?  Accept the date in some appropriate format, perhaps
with the time of day and insert it as part of the DSN on the DD of the
job your program will create.  HTH
```

#### ↳ Re: Create MVS file?

- **From:** tscoffey@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2001-01-23T19:49:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<94kn78$d4f$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<Pine.LNX.4.10.10101202125470.26751-100000@smarty.smart.net>`

```
Some months ago, I posted that I have COBOL callable subroutines that
access ALL of the SVC 99 (dynamic allocation) features. SVC 99 is meant
to be used from assembler, and is VERY hard to use it in that
environment. My subroutines let you fill in a COBOL copybook with
things like LRECL, DSN, BLKSIZE, etc - and call SVC 99. Much easier.
I have used these routines for 5+ years. Others from this group have
requested the routines from me, and (I hope) are using them.

Let me know if you would like them.

Tim

In article <Pine.LNX.4.10.10101202125470.26751-100000@smarty.smart.net>,

>
> I would like to create a file on an MVS system.
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Sent via Deja.com
http://www.deja.com/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Counting COBOL LOC

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-22T14:21:56-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<94i1ab$dm$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<3A6C55C8.4D7B7AEF@raytheon.com> <3A6C5CFA.CC0671C2@brazee.net>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote in message <3A6C5CFA.CC0671C2@brazee.net>...
>
>Dwight Johnson wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>kind of count is.

If I were a bidding on a COBOL source maintenance contract,
or a project to convert COBOL to another language, I might wish
to know LOC to determine the overall effort; thereby, helping
to establish a reasonable bid price. For a large system, using
an estimate of LOC that is wrong could cost millions -- an
accurate number could save millions.

If I were setting up CMM for my organization, I might want to
know LOC to serve as a baseline for monitoring improvement.

The value of LOC depends upon its use. LOC per hour is
worthless; but LOC is not neessarily worthless.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Counting COBOL LOC

```

> If I were a bidding on a COBOL source maintenance contract,
> or a project to convert COBOL to another language, I might wish
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> know LOC to serve as a baseline for monitoring improvement.

So Joe and Martha have two programs, Martha has twice as many lines of code.
Whose program will be the most work to maintain?  Whose program has the most
logic?  Whose program is clearer?  Whose program is more complex?  Whose
program was harder to write?

I don't know either.

I know - I will use in-line programs instead of performs so that I have more
lines of code and get a raise!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Counting COBOL LOC

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-22T19:50:51-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<94iki0$mud$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<3A6C55C8.4D7B7AEF@raytheon.com> <3A6C5CFA.CC0671C2@brazee.net> <94i1ab$dm$1@news.hitter.net> <3A6C9367.B774C401@brazee.net>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote in message <3A6C9367.B774C401@brazee.net>...
>Rick Smith wrote:
[...]
>> If I were setting up CMM for my organization, I might want to
>> know LOC to serve as a baseline for monitoring improvement.
>
>So Joe and Martha have two programs, Martha has twice as many lines of
code.
>Whose program will be the most work to maintain?  Whose program has the
most
>logic?  Whose program is clearer?  Whose program is more complex?  Whose
>program was harder to write?
>
>I don't know either.

I can't answer these questions within the context of CMM;
because the questions are irrelevant! Under PSP (see below),
the questions are irrelevant! Monitoring improvement is not
the same as comparing Joe and Martha!

>I know - I will use in-line programs instead of performs so that I have
more
>lines of code and get a raise!

With Capability Maturity Model (CMM), the emphasis is on quality
through process improvement. Were you to artificially inflate the
LOC, more errors would occur, more lines would be changed
unnecessarily during maintenance, and overall quality would
decrease. Just the opposite of a goal of CMM. It is far more
likely that you would be given the opportunity to advance within
another organization. ;-)

From this point, I will ramble a bit, provide references, quotes
and observations.

The Personal Software Process (PSP), which is associated
with CMM, requires that the individual track their own productivity.
Under PSP, LOC per hour is a number for your use only! As an
individual, you use that number along with other numbers to
estimate the time you require to complete your tasks. The better
your estimates -- the greater your contribution to overall process
improvement.

The Software Engineering Institute at Carnegie-Mellon
University is the starting point for understanding CMM and PSP
and other programs. The home page is
< http://www.sei.cmu.edu/ >; but you might wish to start with
< http://www.sei.cmu.edu/managing/managing.html >.

There are also several books by Watts S. Humphrey which
explain these programs. In his book on PSP, "A Discipline
for Software Engineering," Addison-Wesley, 1995,
page 73-74, Watts S. Humphrey wrote:

"LOC can be defined in so many ways that it is easy to get
confused. It is such a conceptually simple measure that people
often treat the subject superficially. Because of the many
possible LOC definitions, the proper use of size measures
depends on the care with which you specify and apply the
measure. When usage specifications are not precise, different
LOC measures of identical products often very so widely that
the measures are useless. Flaherty, for example, found
productivity variations of 56 to one, largely due to differences
in LOC definitions."

On page 76, of the same book, is a template describing
statement types as: Executable, Declarations, Compiler
directives, Comments, etc. -- nearly the same words used
by Dwight Johnson. Apparently, Dwight Johnson is aware of
PSP and has chosen to define COBOL LOC as statements,
data description entries, and compiler directives while
excluding comments, blank lines, procedure names, headers,
etc. I don't agree with that definition because it leaves out
potentially significant entries in the Environment Division.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Counting COBOL LOC

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 2001-01-23T07:05:57+11:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A6C92A5.7897CA59@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<3A6C55C8.4D7B7AEF@raytheon.com> <3A6C5CFA.CC0671C2@brazee.net>`

```
I had to do this. I downloaded the code to Unix, then using grep
and wc it was easy. I was also looking for duplicate code; I did
this with a 30 line python script and found about 30% of the code
was duplicated (same whole paragraph occurred somewhere else in
the application).

Having to do it on the mainframe I would use SAS.

Lines of code is a fallible measure. Eg The famous story of IBM
and MSFT working together on OS/2. The MSFT people started
cleaning up the code at the end of a project phase which prompted
furious objections from the iBMers because they were assessed on
the LOC they produced.

But it dowes have its uses. I think my cobol compiler will take
about 120,000 LOC and we have 50,000 lines now(including
comments). However not everyone produces the same amount of
function per LOC, and you need to make adjustments accordingly.

Tim Josling

Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> Dwight Johnson wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> kind of count is.
```

```
post his query here.  For his purposes this:

MOVE A TO B MOVE C TO D

Is TWO lines of code - not one. Additionally, copybooks must be
automatically expanded.  This makes it hardly "trivial" - and while I
offered to actually write one, I think there's probably someone out
there that actually already has one and I'm hoping they will speak up.

As far as the metric being useless - that's a religous discussion, but
I could use such a tool just to show management how VAST a task it
would be to foolishly take their COBOL and try to rewrite it in the
language of the day.  I'm not convinced it's useless.  

I'm hoping someone can assist.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Counting COBOL LOC

- **From:** "Ira D. Baxter" <idbaxter@semdesigns.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-23T09:50:35-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<t6r9q7rc8mk72b@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3A6C55C8.4D7B7AEF@raytheon.com> <3A6C5CFA.CC0671C2@brazee.net> <3a6cdec5.175307049@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
What we discover here is that the  notion of a metric is often relative
to what a specific observer wants to count, i.e., definitions vary.
So what you really want is a tool in which you can change such definitions
quickly.

The DMS Reengineering Toolkit is generalized compiler technology,
used to automate customized analyses and modifications of sources
for large scale software systems in many langauges, including COBOL.
See http://www.semdesigns.com/Products/DMS/DMSToolkit.html.

The DMS COBOL front end handles copybooks, including optional expansion.

DMS parses COBOL into compiler data structures that match the
structure of the COBOL language.   A DMS component,
called the "attribute evaluator", can be directed to visit these
data structures and carry out arbitrary computations.
So it is "easy" to write a metric over the langauge structures
that matches your definitions.  ("Easy" meaning you do
have to learn how to use the tool).

And if you change your mind about the definitions, you can
straightforwardly modify the computation to take that into account.

Last but not least... DMS can use such analyses to drive
automated modifications to the source code.
(The hard part is defining what to do under what circumstances).
```

#### ↳ Re: Counting COBOL LOC

- **From:** croxbo@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2001-01-22T20:41:30+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<94i5to$79r$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<3A6C55C8.4D7B7AEF@raytheon.com>`

```
If you work with ISPF, your will probably have option 3.1 with the
possibility to print the index of the partitioned dataset you use. The
index will give all the amount of lines.

If you don't have ISPF statistics, you can reset them by option 3.5. By
resetting them, the amount of lines will be calculated.

If you know what functions are behind option 3.1 x, you can perhaps use
them also in the batch.



Sent via Deja.com
http://www.deja.com/
```

#### ↳ Re: Counting COBOL LOC

- **From:** lcccpiasabird@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2001-01-22T20:56:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<94i6qb$832$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<3A6C55C8.4D7B7AEF@raytheon.com>`

```
The space the source code takes up on a mainframe is one thing.

The space the object code takes up is another.

What if there are copybooks with commands in them?

What if a more wordy program actually runs faster?

structural design increases total words in some cases, not in others.

Count the goto's?  Count the Paragraph's?  Lines with no verbs?

Anyway dont forget to count the copybook lines, and how do they fit in?
What about suppressed versus just a copy?

What about the amount of space the object code takes up?


Good Luck!
```

#### ↳ Re: Counting COBOL LOC

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-24T00:15:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Y8pb6.1309$KJ3.63716@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3A6C55C8.4D7B7AEF@raytheon.com>`

```
OPEN INPUT MYFILE.
PERFORM WITH NO LIMIT
   READ MYFILE  AT END EXIT PERFORM END-READ
   IF MYREC(7:1) NOT = '*'
          ADD 1 TO LOC
         END-IF
     END-PERFORM.
CLOSE MYFILE.
DISPLAY LOC
STOP RUN.

Jeeze
```

### MICROFOCUS NETEXPRESS 3.0

<a id="t-3dba91d3e5"></a>
## Thread: AIX, Acucobol data conversion — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### AIX, Acucobol data conversion

- **From:** "Milton Bradley" <nospam@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-22T14:27:52-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<94i5em$nlr$1@news.chorus.net>`

```
Hello,

We have the need to convert data from an application running on an RS6000
(old AIX v4.1.5). The programs were written by a vendor so there appears to
be no access to source code. We did find a directory that contains the data
files and the program executables. Some of the AIX scripts on the machine
refer to acucobol so we are assuming it is a COBOL application with
proprietary indexed data files. The programs appear to have been written
around 1992 if that helps.

What we are looking for is advice on whether these data files could be
converted to ASCII text or some other common format. Does acucobol provide
some sort of utility for exporting data? Maybe we could pay someone to
convert it for us. Any advice appreciated.

- Linda
```

#### ↳ Re: AIX, Acucobol data conversion

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-01-23T01:39:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3a6ce08f.175765335@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<94i5em$nlr$1@news.chorus.net>`

```
Without the source code, at least for the data descriptions, you
basically out of luck.  You would be better served capturing any
reports the system is capable of generating and using these to
reproduce the data.


On Mon, 22 Jan 2001 14:27:52 -0600, "Milton Bradley"
<nospam@nospam.com> wrote:

>Hello,
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>

---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

#### ↳ Re: AIX, Acucobol data conversion

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik-nws@siber.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-23T02:35:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A6CEE04.57497B44@siber.com>`
- **References:** `<94i5em$nlr$1@news.chorus.net>`

```
Siber Systems has developed DataReader technology 
that allows to read Cobol data files when you have
source for FD and SELECT and when you have NO source 
of the Cobol program.

DataGuess tool from our family of tools performs
statistical analysis of records in your data file
and comes up with a record layout that fits the pattern 
that we discover in the file.

Then a technician would manually refine the record layout
(or you can do it yourself, if you also get Data2Flat tool
for testing different data layouts).

The service is not cheap, but if you really *really* need it, 
then we can create reader of any Cobol data file for you.

A trial version of the DataGuess tool and 
more info on DataReader technology is available 
from http://www.siber.com/sct/datafile/

Regards,
Vadim Maslov


Milton Bradley wrote:
> 
> Hello,
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> - Linda
```

#### ↳ Re: AIX, Acucobol data conversion

- **From:** <fred@basset.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-25T15:43:05+11:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<94oaps$cv@newshost.fujitsu.com.au>`
- **References:** `<94i5em$nlr$1@news.chorus.net>`

```
We use ACUCOBOL (version 4.3.1) on SCO and HP Unix, so don't know if the
following will help.
First, try issuing the command                                    runcbl -v
This should show if the ACUCOBOL runtime is loaded, and what version it is.
Always a good starting point.

Our versions of ACUCOBOL come with a tool called vutil.
If you issue the command
                                         vutil -info data_file_name
you get such info as number of records, record size and number of keys.
Using the command
                                                  vutil -info -x -k
data_file_name
provides more data on the keys in the file.
Then try issuing the command
                                   vutil -unload -t data_file_name
new_data_file_name
this should extract the data and put it in the new_data_file_name in an
ASCII format.
Vutil has many other options so it might pay to get a copy of the doco
(available on CD).

ACUCOBOL also comes with Alfred, an Indexed File Record Editor. Alfred is a
menu-driven, record-level editor. Alfred allows you to view, modify, add, or
delete individual records stored in an indexed file.

Hope this is of help,

Glenn Wood

xsys@hotpop.com


Milton Bradley <nospam@nospam.com> wrote in message
news:94i5em$nlr$1@news.chorus.net...

data

> refer to acucobol so we are assuming it is a COBOL application with
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
```

## Thread: Help ! Switch archive. — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help ! Switch archive.

- **From:** "U." <upallot@tin.it>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B3b6.18557$dD2.205949@twister2.tin.it>`

```
I' ve an archive divided in two files: "archive" and "archive.INX". This
archive was created by a cobol application in a UNIX/SCO enviroment. How can
I read it in win Enviroment, how can I translate it in a txt.file or ODBC
compatible archive ?

Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: Help ! Switch archive.

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-01-23T01:40:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<3B3b6.18557$dD2.205949@twister2.tin.it>`

```

entries and create the file in any format you desire.

On Mon, 22 Jan 2001 23:43:59 GMT, "U." <upallot@tin.it> wrote:

>I' ve an archive divided in two files: "archive" and "archive.INX". This
>archive was created by a cobol application in a UNIX/SCO enviroment. How can
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

---
```

#### ↳ Re: Help ! Switch archive.

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-01-23T17:55:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A6DC5A9.8DAE423B@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3B3b6.18557$dD2.205949@twister2.tin.it>`

```
> I' ve an archive divided in two files: "archive" and "archive.INX". This
> archive was created by a cobol application in a UNIX/SCO enviroment. How can
> I read it in win Enviroment, how can I translate it in a txt.file or ODBC
> compatible archive ?

SCO Unixware and Open Server have an SMB server VisionFS that will allow
the files on the Unix system to be accessed as a sharable drive from
Windows.  Or install Samba to do this for free (get the SCO binaries
from www.sco.com/skunkware).  Then you can run a Cobol program in the
Windows environment to access the Unix file.  The Cobol program should
use the same brand of compiler and run-time that is used on Unix to
ensure compatability.
```

#### ↳ Re: interview questions

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2001-01-23T15:16:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pfhb6.10637$Sl.556701@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<94k31e$p9p$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <94k31e$p9p$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,  <hmstoo@my-deja.com> wrote:
>Can someone give me a source of interview questions for Cobol/CICS
>programmers? We have had to fire three recently hired people because
>they couldn't code. They were very good in the technical interview
>because we asked the same old questions.

So let me get this straight... you wan to replace the Same Old Questions
with some New Questions... which, rapidly enough, become the Same Old
Ones?

'It doesn't matter if the horse is dead... we can still get somewhere if
we beat it hard enough!'

>How do you determine if
>a person is skilled at being interviewed or skilled at programming?

It might just be the same way you determine that *anyone* is better at
(doing x) than at (talking about doing x).

Now permit me to ask a question... how was it determined that 'they
couldn't code'?  There's a world of difference between being able to
generate a program and being able to settle down, productively, into a new
shop.

(I was once asked Interview Question Number 17 (DB2 sheet) of 'What do you
do to get a COBOL source-code file which contains SQL statements to run?';
my answer was 'Well, what *I* do is go into my library, grab the right
compile skeleton, do a global replace on the member-name and SUB the
result... but you're probably looking for the Textbook Answer of
'Precompile, Compile, Link and Bind', right?'

I got the contract... but imagine what would have happened if the
interviewer was not familiar with the steps of 'go into my library, grab
the right compile skeleton, do a global replace on the member-name and SUB
the result'.)

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: interview questions

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-23T08:56:06-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A6DA995.79A6587B@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<94k31e$p9p$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


hmstoo@my-deja.com wrote:

> Can someone give me a source of interview questions for Cobol/CICS
> programmers? We have had to fire three recently hired people because
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: interview questions

- **From:** mano@mano.com (free)
- **Date:** 2001-01-31T04:49:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3a779421.5417276@news.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<94k31e$p9p$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3A6DA995.79A6587B@brazee.net>`

```
On Tue, 23 Jan 2001 08:56:06 -0700, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes, testing is a good option and you don't need formal tests, just be
creative.  For example, I once had an interviewer push a COBOL listing
across the table and leaving the room he said,  *Tell me what you
think this program does, I'll be back in five minutes*.   The program
was just a simple application menu driver which called other programs.

When he returned, he was very polite and explained that he had been
*burned* before and felt it was necessary for his own sake to test my
knowledge of COBOL, regardless of how strong my resume was.   (I later
discovered he had once hired a COBOL programmer that knew very little
about COBOL even though the person's resume said otherwise)

He picked up the listing and the interview was over.   This is a point
worth noting.  He chose to test at the very end of the interview
process and I think that should you try this, you should do the same.

EF
```

#### ↳ Re: interview questions

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-01-23T11:49:28-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<96983DF493CEFD39.D6FC28161C6C2CC6.DD496143E6BA7B9C@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<94k31e$p9p$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

>Can someone give me a source of interview questions for Cobol/CICS
>programmers? We have had to fire three recently hired people because
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.deja.com/

Ask their former employers.  Can't do that?  Have a technical person
interview them or sit in on the interview with your human resource
person.  Another way is to give them a program to debug.  I once
interviewed for an Assembler programmer position and they gave me a
70-75 line program and left me alone for 15 minutes then came back and
asked me what was wrong with it.  I knew and got the job.  You could
do soemthing similar.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Only in America...do drugstores make the sick walk all the way to the
back of the store to get their prescriptions.

Help Save The Rainforest
For more info, please see:  
http://www.ran.org/ran/

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

## Thread: Book on programming: Cobol to Java — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Book on programming: Cobol to Java

- **From:** emtinc@flash.net (emtinc)
- **Date:** 2001-01-23T14:08:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Ufgb6.16474$J%.1271390@news.flash.net>`

```
Here is a book that you might be interested in:
Learn Java by Association with C Cobol Fortran Pascal Visual Basic
Dictionary of Programming Languages

More info (including sample chapters)  at http://www.TechEdNet.com

This book is for anyone who knows C, Fortran, Cobol, Pascal or
Visual Basic and  wants to quickly learn Java.

When trying to learn a new programming language like Java you
will often say to yourself "Gee, I know how to do that in Pascal",
or whatever language you are currently fluent in, " but I wish I
knew how to do that in Java". This book is an answer to that wish.

The book is setup like a dictionary but is much more than a dictionary.
Like a dictionary it helps you with the translation of words (syntax),
in addition, it also tells you how you build "sentences" in various
programming languages.

The CD includes JDK, documentation and several Java tools.

```

- **Date:** 2001-01-23T15:40:25+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A6D97D9.BA1787C9@baeurer.de>`

```
How can I switch the bell off in Micro Focus WB 4.0? With WB 3.4 there
is a runtime-switch P1 to turn the bell on/off? But it is only 16-Bit.

Frank
```

#### ↳ Re: Turn bell off?

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <Todd.Lucas@C-Cubed.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-23T09:21:36-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gcib6.9298$d25.58332@newsfeed.slurp.net>`
- **References:** `<3A6D97D9.BA1787C9@baeurer.de>`

```
Try the "NOBELL" compiler directive using either the "$SET NOBELL" statement in
the program, or place it in a directives file (COBOL.DIR).

TL

```

## Thread: tcpip calls and ebcdic to ascii translation — 8 this month / 9 total

### tcpip calls and ebcdic to ascii translation

- **From:** Clarita Davey <cdavey@scspa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-23T14:48:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<94k5kg$rrs$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
We are using tcpip calls in our CICS Cobol program to connect with a
client socket on a workstation.  We are able to connect, but now we
need to translate the data from our mainframe (ebcdic) to the client
(ascii) and vise-versa.  Where in the program can we translate or call
a module to translate the data?  I have searched the IBM S/390 books
for several ways to do this, but have been unsucessful.


Sent via Deja.com
http://www.deja.com/
```

#### ↳ Re: tcpip calls and ebcdic to ascii translation

- **From:** J@nowhere.moc (J)
- **Date:** 2001-01-23T15:56:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ILhb6.1760$53.304792@cletus.bright.net>`
- **References:** `<94k5kg$rrs$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
It is an API call to QDCXLATE


In article <94k5kg$rrs$1@nnrp1.deja.com>, Clarita Davey <cdavey@scspa.com> 
wrote:
>We are using tcpip calls in our CICS Cobol program to connect with a
>client socket on a workstation.  We are able to connect, but now we
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.deja.com/
```

#### ↳ Re: tcpip calls and ebcdic to ascii translation

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <Todd.Lucas@C-Cubed.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-23T09:28:06-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<miib6.9299$d25.57505@newsfeed.slurp.net>`
- **References:** `<94k5kg$rrs$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On the MVS side, you could use the following TCP/IP entry points.  They are
documented in the "IBM TCP/IP for MVS - Application Programming Interface
Reference, V3R2" manual.  I don't think they are documented in any of the OS/390
manuals, though they are still supported as our products use them (currently
running OS/390 V2R7).

CALL  EZACIC04,(CIFIELDS, CILENGTH),VL  *Convert MVS EBCDIC to PC ASCII
CALL  EZACIC05,(CIENTRY, CILENCVT),VL   *Convert PC ASCII to MVS EBCDIC

Hope it helps ...

TL

Clarita Davey <cdavey@scspa.com> wrote in message
news:94k5kg$rrs$1@nnrp1.deja.com...

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
```

#### ↳ Re: tcpip calls and ebcdic to ascii translation

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-01-24T12:22:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Are you using the EZA calls for your socket interface?  If so then
this call does the translation:

     if client-is-ascii                            
        Call 'EZACIC05' using read-buffer-total     
            read-request-read                       
      end-if             

read-request-read is a counter of length.  This converts EBCDIC to 
ASCII.  

I used the EZA calls for all the socket stuff. 


On Tue, 23 Jan 2001 14:48:52 GMT, Clarita Davey <cdavey@scspa.com>
wrote:

>client socket on a workstation.  We are able to connect, but now we
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.deja.com/
```

<a id="t-3e8c9faa66"></a>
## Thread: INITIALIZE? — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### INITIALIZE?

- **From:** C Pac <WhiteDragon@dexchange.de>
- **Date:** 2001-01-23T21:53:23+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A6DEF43.5F990B5A@dexchange.de>`

```
Hi,
I know, this is an old question, sorry: How das the INITIALIZE-Order
works in groups, numerics and alphanumeric vars?
Thanks, Chris.
```

#### ↳ Re: INITIALIZE?

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-24T00:05:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d%ob6.1249$KJ3.61403@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3A6DEF43.5F990B5A@dexchange.de>`

```
My manual has three pages on the actions of INITIALIZE.

At the most basic level, the verb sets elementary items to spaces or
zero depending on the PIC. The verb skips FILLERs (or implicit FILLERs
like "02  PIC X(10)"  ). INITALIZE ignores group items (but not the
elementary items subordinate to a group).

So,
01  MYDATA.
   02  MYALPHA      PIC X(10).       *>Set to spaces
   02  MYNUM           PIC 9(5).         *>Set to '00000'.
   02  MYVAL.                                    *>Ignored, but ends
up as low-values because of next item
        05  MYCOMP       PIC S9(4) COMP-5     *>Set to low-values
   02  FILLER           PIC X(10).                *> Undefined

There are variations depending on REDEFINES, NATIONAL CHARACTER,
OCCURS DEPEDING ON, REPLACING, and other exotics.


"C Pac" <WhiteDragon@dexchange.de> wrote in message
news:3A6DEF43.5F990B5A@dexchange.de...
> Hi,
> I know, this is an old question, sorry: How das the INITIALIZE-Order
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-b865c5ed30"></a>
## Thread: New COBOL std re examine and 88 conditions — 20 this month / 24 total

### New COBOL std re examine and 88 conditions

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES01@CS.COM>
- **Date:** 2001-01-24T01:03:54-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<94lser$8fc$1@sshuraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
Does anyone know (or agree with me that this would be desirable)
whether or not the new standard will have some way of
limiting the scope of the evaluate verb when using
evaluate true?

Ie

77 SW1           pic x.
  88 AA  VALUE "A".
  88 BB   VALUE "B".

77 SW2            PIC X.
  88 CC VALUE "C".
  88 DD VALUE "D".


MOVE "A" TO SW1.
MOVE "C" TO SW2.

EVALUATE TRUE
  WHEN AA  DISPLAY "A"
  WHEN BB  DISPLAY "B"
  WHEN CC DISPLAY "C"
  WHEN DD DISPLAY "D"
END-EVALUATE.

        I would like for the compiler to at least complain about
the above evaluate.  Since this is currently legal, it would
need some new syntax, or a compiler option.
```

#### ↳ Re: New COBOL std re examine and 88 conditions

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-24T09:53:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A6EA56C.FC71CDDB@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<94lser$8fc$1@sshuraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
Russell Styles wrote:
> 
> Does anyone know (or agree with me that this would be desirable)
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> need some new syntax, or a compiler option.

EVALUATE TRUE will take the first matching case if there are multiple
TRUE conditions.  

The example you created will display "A".  If you want it to display "A"
and then display "C", you could code something like this: 

EVALUATE TRUE
  WHEN AA DISPLAY "A"
  WHEN BB DISPLAY "B"
END-EVALUATE

EVALUATE TRUE
  WHEN CC DISPLAY "C"
  WHEN DD DISPLAY "D"
END-EVALUATE
    

What would you like the compiler to do instead?
```

#### ↳ Re: New COBOL std re examine and 88 conditions

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2001-01-24T13:10:02+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dodt6t8llfu99j4gqkcig6de74ia5apblu@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<94lser$8fc$1@sshuraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
On Wed, 24 Jan 2001 01:03:54 -0500 "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES01@CS.COM> wrote:

:>Does anyone know (or agree with me that this would be desirable)
:>whether or not the new standard will have some way of
:>limiting the scope of the evaluate verb when using
:>evaluate true?

:>Ie

:>77 SW1           pic x.
:>  88 AA  VALUE "A".
:>  88 BB   VALUE "B".

:>77 SW2            PIC X.
:>  88 CC VALUE "C".
:>  88 DD VALUE "D".


:>MOVE "A" TO SW1.
:>MOVE "C" TO SW2.

:>EVALUATE TRUE
:>  WHEN AA  DISPLAY "A"
:>  WHEN BB  DISPLAY "B"
:>  WHEN CC DISPLAY "C"
:>  WHEN DD DISPLAY "D"
:>END-EVALUATE.

:>        I would like for the compiler to at least complain about
:>the above evaluate.  Since this is currently legal, it would
:>need some new syntax, or a compiler option.

What would you like it to complain about?

It is possible for none of the conditions to be true, one to be true or two to
be true. 

Do you wish the compiler to flag every conditional where more than one path
may be true? 

What about:

    IF SALARY > some-numbler
           .
           .
    ELSE IF NUMBER-OF-CHILDREN > other-number
           .
           .

Both conditions may be true, both may be false, and one may be true with the
other false.
```

#### ↳ Re: New COBOL std re examine and 88 conditions

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-24T13:25:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SIAb6.398$tV5.31877@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<94lser$8fc$1@sshuraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
I can't see why the compiler should complain, but if you want to pair up two
unrelated conditions you can...

EVALUATE TRUE ALSO TRUE
   WHEN AA  ALSO CC  DISPLAY 'AA ALSO CC'
   WHEN AA  ALSO DD  DISPLAY 'AA ALSO DD'
   [etc]
END-EVALUATE
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: New COBOL std re examine and 88 conditions

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz
- **Date:** 2001-01-25T04:34:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<94oacc$ik3$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<94lser$8fc$1@sshuraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <3A6EE1A9.99546499@brazee.net>`

```


  Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> I don't agree with you that this would be desirable.
>
> An EVALUATE TRUE should not give a warning if you were looking at more
> than one condition to make your choice.

> > EVALUATE TRUE
> >   WHEN AA  DISPLAY "A"
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > END-EVALUATE.

What might be desirable is if a variable can be restrained in the values
it can hold in some way and setting and testing of this variable can be
checked at compile time.  For example:

    01  My-flag          PIC X VALUE "N"  VALID "Y" "N".

Then 'MOVE "X" TO My-Flag' or 'IF My-Flag = "X"' should be considered as
compile time errors.

Of course I have no idea how a compiler could ever determine this, while
it may appear trivial in the above examples it may be that another
variable is moved to My-Flag and the compiler could never know what
value it will hold at run-time.

Given the compiler could do this it will still not error the EVALUATE
TRUE example but it might be able to check:

      EVALUATE SW1
      WHEN "A" ..
      WHEN "B"
      WHEN "C"      <-- invalid value for SW1

I doubt it will ever be a feasible proposition.  I certainly wouldn't
rely on this check fixing any _programmer_ errors (as above).



Sent via Deja.com
http://www.deja.com/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: New COBOL std re examine and 88 conditions

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-01-26T06:55:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A711F54.2071BFA0@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<94lser$8fc$1@sshuraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <3A6EE1A9.99546499@brazee.net> <94oacc$ik3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3gpv6t8brmg8e77t6viacjqt5bbaeodh7h@4ax.com>`

```
Binyamin Dissen wrote:
> 
> :>What might be desirable is if a variable can be restrained in the values
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> That what a CLASS test is for.

A Class test does not happen at compile time and must be explicity coded
to happen at run-time, ie moving an invalid value to a field or testing
it against an invalid value will not generate an error.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: New COBOL std re examine and 88 conditions

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-25T15:48:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qVXb6.1124$uN3.96554@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<94lser$8fc$1@sshuraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <3A6EE1A9.99546499@brazee.net> <94oacc$ik3$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
<riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz> wrote in message news:94oacc$ik3$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
>
>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Why not just use what's already available?

01  MY-FLAG    PIC X(01) VALUE IS 'N'.
    88 VALID-FLAG   VALUES 'Y', 'N''
    88 MY-FLAG-TRUE   VALUE 'Y'
    88 MY-FLAG-FALSE  VALUE 'N'

IF VALID-FLAG
     EVALUATE TRUE
        WHEN MY-FLAG-TRUE
          yadda,yadda
        WHEN [MY-FLAG-FALSE | OTHER]
          hum,dum,de,dum
     END-EVALUATE
{ ELSE
   error message
}
END-IF

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: New COBOL std re examine and 88 conditions

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz
- **Date:** 2001-01-26T00:26:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<94qg88$g5m$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<94lser$8fc$1@sshuraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <3A6EE1A9.99546499@brazee.net> <94oacc$ik3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <qVXb6.1124$uN3.96554@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```


  "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:

> > What might be desirable is if a variable can be restrained in the
values
> > it can hold in some way and setting and testing of this variable can
be
> > checked at compile time.  For example:

> Why not just use what's already available?
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> END-IF

Your 'Valid-Flag' is entirely unnecessary:

     EVALUATE TRUE
     WHEN My-Flag-True
         do this
     WHEN My-Flag-False
         do that
     WHEN OTHER
         error message
     END-EVALUATE

In fact your [My-Flag-False | OTHER] implies that the 'IF Valid-Flag' is
unwanted.  One of these two looks like a programmer error - do you want
to do 'hum,dum,de,dum' if My-Flag is not "Y" and not "N" or not ?  Or
are you trying to catch conditions where the content is valid but is not
one of the valid values ???   ;-)

But the main reason for not doing that is that the original request was
for a <<COMPILE>> time test, which yours is not.

If you could get the original programmer to do these sorts of tests for
validity then you could probably get him to program his original code
correctly in the first place, making these addons unnecessary.



Sent via Deja.com
http://www.deja.com/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: New COBOL std re examine and 88 conditions

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-01-26T13:26:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fWec6.695$23.49654@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<94lser$8fc$1@sshuraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <3A6EE1A9.99546499@brazee.net> <94oacc$ik3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <qVXb6.1124$uN3.96554@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <94qg88$g5m$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
<riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz> wrote in message news:94qg88$g5m$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
>
> But the main reason for not doing that is that the original request was
> for a <<COMPILE>> time test, which yours is not.
>

Yeah, but I was just trying to satisfy what seemed an irresistible urge for
a explicit "validity" test...

Well, no, that's not it. What it is, is, ...you got a few minutes?

I surf this newsgroup and a couple of BASIC-language newsgroups as well. It
continually amazes/frustrates me to see the large number of people whose
thought processes somehow lead to the conclusion, "if I can't figure out how
to do this, it must be because the language/compiler is inadequate and it's
time to ask for a new feature."

There also seems to be a great number of aspiring programmers who seem to
think there IS a free lunch; that is, with some enhancement to the
language/compiler, they can avoid that pesky little task of actually
creating the number of source code lines required to get the job done
correctly.

Last but not least - although this ng (comp.lang.cobol) is by far the best
at self-policing - are the "students" who seem to think that somehow one can
learn programming if only they can be handed the answers to their homework
and not be bothered with all that studying and stuff like that.

Even in the pending case, I find it somewhat remarkable that the individual
who first asked the question did not bother to reflect for a moment and
realize that COBOL is not a product with "compile-time validation" - or even
that there is a difference between "compile time" and "execution time." (Not
that the modern IDEs make that all too clear!).

I guess I'm just old-fashioned enough to believe that both COBOL and BASIC
contain plenty of tools to get the job done if only the programmer will make
a sincere effort - that is, with a little dedication, professionalism and an
honest day's work you can go further than if you just 'slide on through.'

And sometimes I just gotta share.
```
